chapterTitle: The Complete Picture - In Plain English
text: Chapter 7.16 recaps the full attention computation in plain English as a 9-step pipeline: words become embeddings, multiplied by W_Q/W_K/W_V to make Queries, Keys, Values; Q dot K gives relevance scores; scale and softmax turn scores into percentages; percentages weight the Values into a context-aware output; steps 2-5 repeat across 8 heads with different grids; the 8 results are concatenated and multiplied by W_O for a combined summary; output is one blended vector per word ready for the next layer. The W matrices (W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O) are tables of numbers trained from random and then frozen, each learning a distinct role. The grids are the machine; the sentence is raw material.
summary: Plain-English recap of the entire attention pipeline as 9 steps with the role of each W matrix.

chapterTitle: The Complete Picture - In Plain English
text:
The complete picture in plain English, step by step:
1. Words become numbers (embedding lookup).
2. Those numbers times three grids (W_Q, W_K, W_V give Query, Key, Value).
3. Queries dot-product with Keys (find who is relevant to whom).
4. Scores get normalized (scale plus softmax give percentages).
5. Percentages blend the Values (weighted sum gives context-aware output).
6. Steps 2-5 happen 8 times with different grids (multi-head gives 8 separate results).
7. Stick the 8 results end-to-end (concatenate, like 8 sealed envelopes taped together).
8. That list times one more grid (W_O opens all envelopes and writes a combined summary).
9. Output: one blended vector per word, ready for the next layer.
summary: Lists the 9 plain-English steps of attention from embeddings to one blended vector per word.

chapterTitle: The Complete Picture - In Plain English
text:
Every grid in attention (W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O) is a table of numbers that started random, got nudged into useful values during training, and stays frozen forever after. All grids work the same way: multiply input by grid, get output. The only difference is what each one was trained to do:
- W_Q grids: learned to extract what a word is looking for.
- W_K grids: learned to extract what a word can be found for.
- W_V grids: learned to extract what actual info a word carries.
- W_O grid: learned to blend all heads' findings into one combined summary.
summary: Explains that all W matrices are frozen trained number grids that differ only in what they were trained to extract.

text: Chapter 8.1 introduces the original 2017 Transformer's encoder-decoder design for machine translation. The Encoder reads input in parallel and builds rich contextual representations; the Decoder generates output token by token. Cross-Attention bridges them: decoder Queries attend to encoder Keys and Values. The full stack is 6 encoder layers (Attention, Add & Norm, FFN, Add & Norm) and 6 decoder layers (Masked Attention, Add & Norm, Cross-Attention, Add & Norm, FFN, Add & Norm). Three model families emerge from this design: Encoder-Decoder (T5, BART) for translation, Encoder-Only (BERT, RoBERTa) for classification, Decoder-Only (GPT, Claude) for generation.
summary: Summarizes the encoder-decoder Transformer, cross-attention, the 6+6 layer stack, and the three model-family variants.

text: The original Transformer from the 2017 paper "Attention Is All You Need" was built to solve machine translation. Translation needs two different operations: take an English input like "I love cats" and produce a Hindi output like "Mujhe billiyaan pasand hain". English and Hindi are different languages, so the model must fully understand the English input before it can generate the Hindi output. These are two separate tasks, which motivates splitting the architecture into two halves.
summary: Introduces the 2017 Transformer paper and why machine translation requires two distinct stages: understanding the source and generating the target.

text: Solution: two separate components. The Encoder reads the input and builds a deep understanding, outputting a compressed representation. The Decoder reads the encoder's output and generates the translation one word at a time. Encoder properties: processes input in parallel, all words attend to all words, builds context for each word, outputs rich representations of "I", "love", "cats". Decoder properties: reads encoder output, generates translation token by token, uses attention to look back at input, producing "Mujhe" then "billiyaan" then "pasand" then "hain".
summary: Splits the Transformer into Encoder (parallel understanding) and Decoder (token-by-token generation) and lists their distinct jobs.

text: Cross-Attention is the bridge between encoder and decoder. The decoder doesn't generate from nothing; it looks back at the encoder's output through a special connection called cross-attention. Flow: Encoder output (rich understanding of "I", "love", "cats") provides Keys and Values, which feed into the Cross-Attention Layer, where the decoder's Query asks the encoder "What's relevant now?". The decoder then generates "Mujhe billiyaan pasand hain". When generating a Hindi noun, the decoder's Query asks "what noun am I translating?" and the encoder's Keys/Values answer with the meaning of "love" and "cats", so the decoder picks the right Hindi word.
summary: Explains cross-attention as the mechanism where decoder Queries attend to encoder Keys/Values to ground generation in the source.

text: The full encoder-decoder stack: the 2017 Transformer stacks both halves with 6 layers each. Encoder Stack (6 layers) per layer: Attention, Add & Norm, Feed-Forward, Add & Norm. Decoder Stack (6 layers) per layer: Masked Attention, Add & Norm, Cross-Attention, Add & Norm, Feed-Forward, Add & Norm. Masked Attention means the decoder can only look at past tokens; Cross-Attention means it looks at encoder output. The encoder feeds into the decoder.
summary: Lays out the 6-layer encoder and 6-layer decoder stacks and the sublayers inside each, including masked and cross-attention in the decoder.

text: Why two halves? Different model types. The encoder-decoder design is great for translation, but other tasks need different shapes: Encoder-Decoder - Translation (T5, BART), input English, output Hindi. Encoder-Only - Classification (BERT, RoBERTa), input Sentence, output Sentiment score. Decoder-Only - Generation (GPT, Claude), input Prompt, output Continued text.

text: Chapter 12.2 covers Add & Norm, the stabilizer inside every Transformer block. A Transformer block has 4 steps: Multi-Head Attention, Add & Norm, FFN, Add & Norm - so Add & Norm appears twice per block. The 'Add' is a residual connection that adds the original input back to the sublayer output, preserving information and letting gradients flow back through 96 layers without vanishing. The 'Norm' is Layer Normalization, which rescales values using the formula LayerNorm(x_i) = gamma * (x_i - mu) / sqrt(sigma squared + epsilon) + beta. Mu is the mean, sigma squared is the variance, epsilon (1e-5) prevents division by zero, and gamma and beta are learnable per-dimension scale and shift parameters that start at 1.0 and 0.0. Without Add & Norm, values drift: weights averaging 0.9 shrink 0.7 to 0.00003 by layer 96, weights averaging 1.1 grow it to 4,838. With Add & Norm, values stay stable across all 96 layers and training works.
summary: Overview of Add & Norm: residual connection plus Layer Normalization that stabilizes Transformer training across deep layers.

text: The Problem: Values Drift in Deep Networks. GPT-3 has 96 layers. Each layer multiplies values by weights - if weights are slightly below 1.0, values shrink; slightly above, values explode. Keeping them balanced across 96 layers is nearly impossible. Take 0.7 from the 'cats' embedding. Scenario A: weights average 0.9 (shrink) - Start 0.7, Layer 10 = 0.24, Layer 50 = 0.004, Layer 96 = 0.00003 (vanishes to nearly zero). Scenario B: weights average 1.1 (grow) - Start 0.7, Layer 10 = 1.8, Layer 50 = 82, Layer 96 = 4,838 (explodes to thousands). Both are broken - whether the value goes to 0.00003 or 4,838 it is no longer a useful embedding. Gradients during backpropagation drift the same way, so the model cannot learn either.
summary: Without stabilization, values shrink to ~0 or explode to thousands across 96 layers, breaking both forward signal and gradients.

text: The 'Add' - Keep the Original. The fix is simple: add the original input back to the output. The fancy name is 'residual connection' - 'residual' just means 'leftover.' You keep the leftover original so it never gets lost. How it works for 'cats' (embedding = [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6]): original input [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6] goes through Multi-Head Attention producing attention output [0.3, -0.1, 0.2, 0.4]. The Add Step: original + attention output = [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6] + [0.3, -0.1, 0.2, 0.4] = [-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0]. Think of it as a highway: even if attention learns nothing useful, the original embedding still passes through untouched. During backpropagation, error can travel backward along this highway without shrinking to zero. Without the Add: Input -> Attention -> Output (if attention messes up, values are lost). With the Add: Input -> Attention -> Output + Input (original values always survive).
summary: Residual connection adds the original input back to the sublayer output, preserving information and enabling gradient flow.

text: The 'Norm' - Layer Normalization. After adding, values can drift to unpredictable ranges. Layer Norm rescales them. The formula used in every Transformer: LayerNorm(x_i) = gamma * (x_i - mu) / sqrt(sigma squared + epsilon) + beta. Where: x_i = each value in the vector (raw input we want to normalize); mu = average of all values (centers the data around zero); sigma squared = variance of all values (measures how spread out values are); epsilon = tiny number (1e-5) that prevents division by zero if all values are identical; gamma = learnable scale (starts at 1.0), lets the model stretch or shrink the range; beta = learnable shift (starts at 0.0), lets the model move the center up or down. Gamma and beta are learned during training - they let the model undo the normalization if needed, stretching, shrinking, or shifting the normalized values to whatever range works best for each dimension.
summary: Full Layer Normalization formula with gamma, beta, mu, sigma squared, and epsilon explained.

text: Computing Layer Norm Step by Step on the result from Add: [-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0]. Step 1: Compute the mean mu = (-0.2 + 0.2 + 0.9 + 1.0) / 4 = 0.475. Step 2: subtract the mean from each value (x_i - mu): -0.2 - 0.475 = -0.675; 0.2 - 0.475 = -0.275; 0.9 - 0.475 = 0.425; 1.0 - 0.475 = 0.525. Centered: [-0.675, -0.275, 0.425, 0.525]. Step 3: Compute variance = mean of squared deviations = (0.675^2 + 0.275^2 + 0.425^2 + 0.525^2) / 4 = 0.2469. sqrt(0.2469 + 0.00001) = 0.4969 (epsilon = 1e-5 prevents division by zero). Divide each centered value: -0.675 / 0.4969 = -1.36; -0.275 / 0.4969 = -0.55; 0.425 / 0.4969 = 0.86; 0.525 / 0.4969 = 1.06. After this step: [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] - centered at 0, unit variance. Step 4: Scale by gamma, shift by beta. Gamma and beta are per-dimension learnable parameters. Initially gamma=1.0 and beta=0.0, so 1.0 * -1.36 + 0.0 = -1.36; 1.0 * -0.55 + 0.0 = -0.55; 1.0 * 0.86 + 0.0 = 0.86; 1.0 * 1.06 + 0.0 = 1.06. Final output: [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]. During training the model may learn dimensions need more spread (gamma > 1) or non-zero center (beta != 0).
summary: Worked example computing LayerNorm on [-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0] through mean, centering, variance, normalize, scale and shift.

text: The Complete Add & Norm Pipeline. This exact same Add & Norm happens again after FFN. Every Transformer block does this twice - once after Attention, once after FFN.

text: GELU activation function graph. Line graph over x from -3 to 3. Curve is a smooth S-shape: nearly zero for large negative x, dips slightly below zero around x = -1 forming a small leak region, passes smoothly through the origin with a continuous derivative, and asymptotically matches y = x for large positive x. Annotations highlight the leak (gradient alive) and the smooth S at the origin. Title: GELU(x) = x · Φ(x). The smooth shape keeps gradients alive for negative inputs.
summary: Diagram of the GELU activation showing the leak region near x=-1 and the smooth S-curve through the origin.

text: Chapter 12.3 covers the Feed-Forward Network (FFN) inside a Transformer encoder block. The FFN sits between the two Add & Norm layers, after Multi-Head Attention. It is two linear layers with a GELU activation in between: FFN(x) = W2 · GELU(W1 · x + b1) + b2. The first layer expands the dimension by 4x (e.g. 512 to 2048), GELU activates each hidden neuron, then the second layer compresses back to the original size. Each token is processed independently with no cross-token communication. GELU(x) = x · Φ(x) replaces ReLU's hard cutoff with a smooth curve so gradients stay alive for negative inputs. FFN stores knowledge in W1 rows (pattern detectors) and W2 columns (output writers): attention gathers context, FFN adds stored facts, implicit relationships, and disambiguates meanings. FFN holds roughly two-thirds of a block's parameters (about 2.1M of 3.15M per block in a 512-dim model).
summary: Chapter-level summary: FFN as expand-then-compress two-layer network with GELU, storing knowledge while attention routes context.

text: Where does the Feed-Forward Network sit? In chapter 12.2 we covered Add & Norm after Attention. The flow inside one encoder block is: Input Embeddings → Multi-Head Attention → Add & Norm → FFN (Feed-Forward Network) → Add & Norm → Output. The FFN sits between the two Add & Norm layers. It processes each token independently - no communication between words here. That is what makes it different from Attention.

text: FFN = two linear layers with activation. A layer multiplies by weight matrix W and adds bias b (chapter 3.4). FFN is two such layers stacked with an activation function in between. The FFN Formula: FFN(x) = W2 · GELU(W1 · x + b1) + b2. Where x = input from Add & Norm, W1 and b1 = first layer weights and bias, GELU = activation function, W2 and b2 = second layer weights and bias. No attention, no softmax, no multi-head anything - just two matrix multiplies with an activation in between.

text: The expand-then-compress shape. First layer expands the dimension, second layer compresses back. In GPT-2 with d_model = 512: Input (512) → W1 (512 x 2048) → Hidden (2048) → +GELU → W2 (2048 x 512) → Output (512). The hidden layer has 4x more neurons than the input or output. Every input neuron connects to every hidden neuron via W1, GELU activates each hidden neuron, then every hidden neuron connects to every output neuron via W2. Data expands into a wider thinking space (512 to 2048), then compresses back (2048 to 512). Why 4x? Imagine spreading notes across a big desk (expand to 2048), working things out, then summarizing on a small card (compress to 512). The 4x ratio comes from the original Attention is All You Need paper and has become standard across GPT-2, GPT-3, and most Transformers.
summary: Explains the 4x expand-then-compress shape (512→2048→512) and the desk-and-card intuition for the ratio.

text: Step-by-step: FFN on "cats". In chapter 12.2 Add & Norm output [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] for "cats". Using a tiny 4-dim version (real models use 512 dims but the process is identical). Step 1: Multiply by W1 + b1 (expand 4 → 8 dims). Compute [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06] · W1 + b1. Each of the 8 output values is a dot product of the input with one column of W1, plus a bias - exactly like chapter 3.4. Result (8 dims): [0.42, -1.80, 0.95, 2.10, -0.33, 1.47, -0.88, 0.61].
summary: Worked Step 1: the cats vector is multiplied by W1+b1 to expand from 4 to 8 dimensions.

text: Step-by-step: FFN on "cats" continued. Step 2: Apply GELU activation - applied to each value independently, similar to ReLU but smoother. Per-element transformation: 0.42 → 0.28, -1.80 → -0.04, 0.95 → 0.77, 2.10 → 2.07, -0.33 → -0.12, 1.47 → 1.37, -0.88 → -0.17, 0.61 → 0.44. Result: [0.28, -0.04, 0.77, 2.07, -0.12, 1.37, -0.17, 0.44]. Step 3: Multiply by W2 + b2 (compress 8 → 4 dims). The 8-dim expanded vector gets compressed back to the original 4 dims. Compute [0.28, -0.04, ..., 0.44] · W2 + b2. FFN output for "cats" (4 dims): [0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22]. Same size in, same size out - but values have been transformed through a thinking space 4x wider. This output now goes to the second Add & Norm.
summary: Worked Steps 2 and 3: GELU is applied elementwise, then W2+b2 compresses back to 4 dims producing [0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22].

text: GELU - the activation function. Chapter 1.6 covered ReLU. Modern Transformers use a smoother alternative called GELU (Gaussian Error Linear Unit). Real formula: GELU(x) = x · Φ(x), where Φ(x) is the cumulative distribution function of the standard normal: Φ(x) = (1/2) [1 + erf(x / √2)]. Symbols: x = the input value, Φ(x) = probability x is kept, ranges from 0 (fully blocked) to 1 (fully passed), erf = Gauss error function, a smooth S-curve mapping any number to [-1, 1]. Plain English: GELU multiplies x by the probability that x is considered large under a standard normal bell curve. Large positives pass through almost fully (Φ near 1). Large negatives get almost fully blocked (Φ near 0). Values near zero get partially passed - the smooth part. Worked examples: x=2.0, Φ=0.977, result=1.95 (large positive, almost fully passed); x=0.5, Φ=0.691, result=0.35 (69% passed through); x=0.0, Φ=0.500, result=0.00; x=-0.5, Φ=0.309, result=-0.15 (31% leaked through); x=-2.0, Φ=0.023, result=-0.05 (almost fully blocked). ReLU vs GELU: ReLU is hard cutoff - if x<0 output=0, if x>0 output=x, gradient=0 forever for negatives. GELU is smooth - small negatives leak through, gradient never fully dies. Why the smooth curve matters: during backpropagation (chapter 2.8), ReLU's gradient is exactly 0 for all negative inputs - the neuron is dead and can never recover. GELU's smooth curve gives even slightly negative values a small gradient, so neurons recover. GPT-2 and GPT-3 both use GELU.
summary: Defines GELU(x) = x · Φ(x) with worked examples and contrasts ReLU's dead-neuron problem against GELU's smooth gradient.
queries: Why use GELU instead of ReLU?

text: What FFN stores - knowledge vs routing. Attention decides which words to listen to, FFN decides what to do with what it heard. Inside every block, Attention runs first, then FFN. FFN never sees the raw input - it always receives attention's output. Example 1: "The area of this rectangle depends on its length" - breadth is never mentioned but the model knows it matters. Attention runs first: "area"'s Query asks what determines me, "length"'s Key scores 0.55 after softmax, "rectangle"'s Key scores 0.30. Weighted sum: Output = 0.55 · V_length + 0.30 · V_rectangle + ... The area vector now carries "I am the area of a rectangle and length is involved." Result: context assembled but incomplete - attention can only mix what is there, it cannot add knowledge missing from the text. Then FFN adds stored knowledge: W1 row 1203 (rectangle-area detector) was trained on millions of math texts. Dot product W1[1203] · x = 3.9. GELU(3.9) = 3.89 → neuron 1203 fires strongly. Meanwhile neuron 502 (circle-area-pi) got -0.8 → GELU suppresses it. W2 column 1203 shifts the vector into a region encoding "area = length x breadth, breadth is the missing factor." The model now implicitly knows breadth matters even though the word never appeared. During training, FFN saw "area = length x breadth" thousands of times - that relationship got baked into W1 row 1203 (detector) and W2 column 1203 (output).
summary: Example 1 (area/breadth) shows attention assembling context while FFN adds implicit training-baked knowledge via W1 detectors and W2 writers.

Example of FFN adding missing concept

text: Example 2: "The capital of India is ___" - attention gathers context, FFN recalls the fact. Attention runs first: ___'s Query asks what country am I about. "India"'s Key scores highest (0.72 after softmax), "capital" gets 0.18, rest scraps. Weighted sum: Output = 0.72 · V_India + 0.18 · V_capital + ... This is a blend - a weighted average. It mixes India info into the ___ position. Result: context assembled but no answer yet. The ___ vector now encodes "I need the capital of India," but attention can only average existing vectors - it cannot look up a fact. Then FFN recalls the fact: W1 row 847 (capital-of-India detector) trained on millions of geography texts. Dot product W1[847] · x = 4.2. GELU(4.2) = 4.19 → neuron 847 fires. GELU(-1.3) = -0.05 → neuron 200 (capital-of-France) is suppressed. Of 2048 neurons, maybe 100-300 fire meaningfully for any given input. W2 column 847 was trained to shift vectors toward Delhi. Since neuron 847 fired at 4.19, its column dominates the output: output += 4.19 · W2[:,847]. The answer is now in the vector. Key insight: W1 rows = "what to detect," W2 columns = "what to output." This is the key-value memory interpretation from research. The FFN is a lookup table: 2048 entries, each asking "does this pattern match?" and outputting the corresponding knowledge.
summary: Example 2 (India/Delhi) shows FFN as a key-value memory: W1 row 847 detects capital-of-India, GELU gates, W2 column 847 writes Delhi into the vector.

text: Attention + FFN across multiple blocks. Example 3: "She sat by the river bank" - does bank mean money or riverbed? Attention and FFN collaborate across blocks to resolve ambiguity. Block 1 basic context gathering: Attention - bank's Query matches river's Key (adjacent, high positional score). After softmax, bank absorbs ~40% of river's Value vector, sat gets ~20%. Bank now carries "I am next to river and someone sat." FFN - sees bank + some river context. W1 row 310 (river-location detector) fires weakly at 1.2, W1 row 744 (financial-institution detector) fires weakly at 0.8. GELU passes both. W2 nudges vector slightly toward both meanings. Ambiguity not yet resolved. Block 5 deeper semantic understanding: Attention - bank now attends to sat, by, river with much higher scores than Block 1, because Blocks 1-4 FFNs already strengthened nature/location features in sat and river. FFN - row 310 (river-location) fires at 3.8 (strong match). Row 744 (financial) fires at -1.1 → GELU(-1.1) = -0.03, effectively dead. W2 column 310 shifts vector hard toward geography/nature, column 744 contributes almost nothing. Bank now means riverbank - ambiguity resolved. Why this takes multiple blocks: each round, attention provides better context because FFN improved the vectors, and FFN makes better decisions because attention gathered more context. They bootstrap each other across all 96 blocks. Three examples, three types of FFN knowledge: 1. Implicit knowledge (area/breadth) - FFN adds related concepts not in input. 2. Factual recall (India/Delhi) - FFN looks up a stored fact. 3. Disambiguation (bank/river) - FFN suppresses wrong meanings and boosts right one.
summary: Example 3 (river bank) shows attention and FFN bootstrapping across blocks to disambiguate word meanings, plus summary of three FFN knowledge types.

text: Deep questions you should be able to answer. Q: Why can't attention replace FFN? A: Attention computes weighted averages of Value vectors - a linear operation. It can mix information from different tokens but can never create new features. "If river AND bank are both present, output riverbank" requires non-linearity (GELU). Only FFN has that. Q: Why can't FFN replace attention? A: FFN processes each token in complete isolation. It never sees any other word. Without attention first injecting river into bank's vector, FFN would process bank identically in "river bank" and "investment bank" - same input, same output, unable to disambiguate. Q: What exactly does FFN stores knowledge mean? A: Each row of W1 is a pattern detector trained during pre-training. Row 847 learned to fire when the input looks like capital-of-India. The corresponding column of W2 learned to output a vector shifting toward Delhi. This row-column pair is literally one stored fact. GPT-3 has 2048 to 49152 such detectors per block across 96 blocks. Q: How do researchers prove this isn't just a metaphor? A: Ablation studies - zero out specific FFN neurons and watch "The capital of India is" stop producing Delhi while grammar stays intact. Activate those neurons artificially and watch Delhi appear in unrelated contexts. The knowledge is localized to specific neurons, not distributed across the whole network.
summary: Four deep Q&A items explaining why attention and FFN are non-redundant, what stored knowledge means, and how ablation studies confirm it.
queries: What does it mean that FFN stores knowledge?
How do researchers prove FFN stores facts?
Localized vs distributed knowledge in neural networks
Why is the FFN needed in addition to attention?

text: Where do the parameters live? Parameters are all the learnable numbers in the model - every weight and bias tuned during training (chapter 1.4). They are the model's memory. More parameters = more capacity to store knowledge. One Transformer Block has about 3.15M parameters total, split as Attention 1/3 (~1.05M) and FFN 2/3 (~2.1M). Attention ~1.05M: W_Q, W_K, W_V = 3 matrices that create Queries, Keys, Values (chapter 9.9). W_O = matrix that blends multi-head outputs (chapter 11.4). These learn how to find relationships between words. FFN ~2.1M: W1 (512 x 2048) expands to thinking space, W2 (2048 x 512) compresses back to model size. These learn facts and transformations - the actual knowledge. GPT-3 has 96 blocks. 96 copies of this structure = 96 x 3.15M = ~302M parameters just in one layer type. GPT-3's total is 175 billion because d_model is 12,288 (not 512), making each matrix vastly larger.
summary: Parameter breakdown per Transformer block: FFN holds about two-thirds (~2.1M) while attention holds one-third (~1.05M); GPT-3 scales this across 96 blocks at d_model 12,288 to reach 175B.

chapterTitle: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: Chapter 12.4: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter. The FFN weight matrices W1 (d_model x d_ff) and W2 (d_ff x d_model) have shapes that depend only on d_model and d_ff, never on the number of words. Stack any number of word vectors as rows of an input matrix, multiply once by W1, apply GELU, multiply by W2 - rows never interact. Toy example: 18 weights handle 1, 3, 6, 10, or 1000 words. GPT-2 FFN: 1,048,576 weights handle any length. This row-independence makes FFN embarrassingly parallel on GPU, unlike Attention which has cross-token Q middle-dot K dependencies, and is why KV cache stores only attention K,V not FFN outputs.
summary: Chapter overview: FFN weight count depends only on d_model and d_ff, so any sequence length runs as one parallel matrix multiply with no cross-token interaction.

text: Recap: FFN With Just One Word. In chapter 12.3 we traced one word ("cats") through FFN. Make matrix shapes explicit. Say "cats" is a 3-dimensional vector: [0.5, -0.3, 0.9] (1x3, one word, 3 dims) times W1 (3x6, 3 dims in, 6 dims out) = Hidden (1x6, expanded). One word: 1x3 times 3x6 = 1x6. The weight matrix W1 has 3 x 6 = 18 weights. These 18 weights are the same for every single word that ever goes through this FFN.

chapterTitle: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: The Trick: Stack Words Into a Matrix. With 3 words we don't run the FFN 3 times. Stack all 3 word vectors into a single matrix and multiply once. Input 3x3 ([0.5,-0.3,0.9], [0.2,0.8,-0.1], [0.7,-0.5,0.3]) for "I", "love", "cats". Times same W1 (3x6, unchanged) equals 3 outputs (3x6, one row per word). 3x3 times 3x6 = 3x6. Still the same W1. Still 18 weights. Row 1 of the input only interacts with W to produce row 1 of the output. No mixing between words - that's how matrix multiplication works.
summary: Three words stacked into a 3x3 matrix multiply by the same W1 to give 3x6 output with no cross-word mixing.

text: 6 Words: "The cat sat on the mat". 6 words, each a 3-dim vector. Stack into a 6x3 matrix. Input 6x3 (rows: [0.2,-0.9,0.5], [0.6,0.3,-0.2], [-0.5,0.9,0.1], [0.1,-0.4,0.8], [0.2,-0.9,0.5], [0.7,0.2,-0.6]) times same W1 (3x6, 18 weights) = 6 outputs (6x6, one row per word). 6x3 times 3x6 = 6x6. W1 still 3x6 = 18 weights. Going from 3 words to 6 doesn't change weight matrix size. Row 1 ("The") hits W and produces its own output. Row 4 ("on") hits the exact same W and produces its own output. Rows never interact. Each word independently transformed by the same 18 weights.

chapterTitle: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: 10 Words? 1000 Words? Same 18 Weights. The number of words is just the number of rows in the input matrix. The weight matrix W never changes. 1 word: 1x3 times 3x6 = 1x6, W1 = 18 weights. 3 words: 3x3 times 3x6 = 3x6, W1 = 18 weights. 6 words: 6x3 times 3x6 = 6x6, W1 = 18 weights. 10 words: 10x3 times 3x6 = 10x6, W1 = 18 weights. 1000 words: 1000x3 times 3x6 = 1000x6, W1 = 18 weights. The Pattern: (num_words x d_model) times W1 (d_model x d_ff) = (num_words x d_ff). The num_words dimension is a free variable. W1 only cares about d_model (3 in the toy example, 512 in real transformers). That's why the model can accept any length input with the same parameters. In GPT-2 (d_model=512, d_ff=2048): W1 has 512 x 2048 = 1,048,576 weights. That number stays the same whether you feed it 10 words or 100,000 words.
summary: FFN weight matrix size depends only on d_model and d_ff, never on sequence length, so 18 toy weights or GPT-2's ~1M weights handle any length input.

chapterTitle: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: Scaling Back Down: W2 Has the Inverse Shape. After GELU activation, compress back to original dimension. W2 has the flipped shape. 6 words example: Input 6x3 times W1 3x6 = Expanded 6x6 → GELU'd 6x6 times W2 6x3 = Output 6x3. 10 words example (same W1, same W2): Input 10x3 times W1 3x6 = Expanded 10x6 → GELU'd 10x6 times W2 6x3 = Output 10x3. The General Pattern: Layer 1: (words x d_model) times W1 (d_model x d_ff) = (words x d_ff). GELU: (words x d_ff) → (words x d_ff). Layer 2: (words x d_ff) times W2 (d_ff x d_model) = (words x d_model). Word count flows through as the row dimension. W1 and W2 only depend on d_model and d_ff. In, expand, compress back out - same size going in and coming out, regardless of how many words.
summary: W2 inverts W1's shape (d_ff x d_model) so the FFN expands then compresses, preserving the input dimension across any sequence length.

chapterTitle: FFN - Why Word Count Doesn't Matter
text: Why This Matters: Massive GPU Parallelism. Stacking words into a matrix isn't just math elegance - it's about hardware speed. Without stacking (for loop): for word in words: output = word times W. 1000 words = 1000 sequential matrix multiplies. Each waits for previous to finish. GPU sits mostly idle because each multiply is tiny (1x3 times 3x6). With stacking (one multiply): output = all_words times W. 1000 words = one massive parallel matrix multiply (1000x3 times 3x6). GPU processes all rows simultaneously across thousands of cores. One instruction, all words done. The Deep Insight: parallel trick works because FFN treats each word independently. No word's output depends on any other word. That's the fundamental difference from Attention, which creates cross-word dependencies (Q middle-dot K). You can't parallelize Attention the same way because token 3's output depends on tokens 1 and 2. But FFN? Each row hits the same W, gets the same GELU, comes out independently. Embarrassingly parallel - the GPU's dream workload. Also why the KV cache during generation only caches attention K,V - not FFN outputs. FFN has no cross-word state to remember. Run it on the new word, get the answer. Previous words' FFN results are already baked into the K,V cache.
summary: Stacking lets the GPU do one big matrix multiply instead of looping, making FFN embarrassingly parallel because tokens don't interact - unlike Attention - which is also why KV cache stores only attention K,V.

text: Chapter 12.5 introduces the second Add & Norm that follows the FFN in each Transformer block. It uses the same residual + LayerNorm pattern as Add & Norm #1: add FFN input ([-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]) to FFN output ([0.51, -0.73, 1.14, 0.22]) to get [-0.85, -1.28, 2.00, 1.28], then normalize with mu=0.2875, sqrt(variance+epsilon)=1.3846, gamma=1.0, beta=0.0 to produce block output [-0.82, -1.13, 1.24, 0.72]. Each sub-layer (Attention, FFN) gets its own Add & Norm to prevent value drift and enable stacking 96 blocks deep.

text: The Add step (residual around FFN): same idea as chapter 12.2 - keep the original input by adding it back. The input to FFN was the first Add & Norm's output. The residual connection ensures that even if the FFN distorts values, the original signal from Add & Norm #1 survives by being added back.

text: The Norm step - stabilize again. Same Layer Normalization formula from chapter 12.2: compute mean, subtract, divide by standard deviation, scale by gamma, shift by beta. Normalizing [-0.85, -1.28, 2.00, 1.28]: mu = (-0.85 + -1.28 + 2.00 + 1.28) / 4 = 0.2875. variance = 1.9172, sqrt(variance + epsilon) = 1.3846. Normalize each: (x - mu) / 1.3846: -0.85 -> -0.82, -1.28 -> -1.13, 2.00 -> 1.24, 1.28 -> 0.72. Block output for 'cats': [-0.82, -1.13, 1.24, 0.72]. With gamma=1.0 and beta=0.0 (initial values). The model will learn optimal gamma and beta during training.

text: Why Twice? - One per sub-layer. Add & Norm appears twice because each Transformer block has two sub-layers: Attention and FFN. Each sub-layer gets its own Add & Norm because each one can independently cause value drift. Sub-layer 1 (Attention): Input x -> Attention(x) -> Add & Norm #1 (stabilizes after attention changes). Sub-layer 2 (FFN): From A&N #1 -> FFN(x) -> Add & Norm #2 (stabilizes after FFN changes). The Rule: in Transformers, every sub-layer that transforms values gets wrapped with its own residual connection and layer norm. This is what makes it possible to stack 96 blocks deep without the signal degrading. If you only normalized once at the end of the block, the FFN's output could already be in a bad range before it gets stabilized.
summary: Explains the architectural rule: each sub-layer (Attention, FFN) gets its own residual + LayerNorm to enable deep stacking.

text: Chapter 12.6 explains why the Transformer block is stacked N times (the Nx notation in architecture diagrams). One block only captures simple word associations; stacking many blocks (12 in GPT-2 Small, 24 Medium, 36 Large, 96 in GPT-3) refines representations from surface patterns to deep reasoning. Every block shares the same structure (Attention → Add & Norm → FFN → Add & Norm) but each has its own independently learned weights: W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O for attention; gamma1, beta1 for first Add & Norm; W1/b1, W2/b2 for FFN; gamma2, beta2 for second Add & Norm. Early blocks (1-4) learn part-of-speech and word order; blocks 5-12 learn syntax and pronoun resolution; blocks 13-24 learn semantics and word sense; blocks 25-96 learn abstract reasoning and world knowledge. Full encoder pipeline: tokenize, embed, add positions, pass through N blocks with bidirectional attention, output contextual hidden states.
summary: Transformer encoder stacks N identical blocks, each with its own weights, building from surface patterns up to abstract reasoning.

text: One Transformer block is not enough. We have seen all 4 steps inside one block: Attention, Add & Norm, FFN, Add & Norm. But one block can only learn simple patterns. To understand language the model must repeat the entire block many times. Think of it: 1 block learns basic word associations ("cat" relates to "sat"); 4 blocks learn grammar structure (subject-verb agreement); 12 blocks learn context and nuance (sarcasm, tone); 96 blocks learn deep reasoning, world knowledge, complex inference. Each repeat of the block refines the token representations further. The output of block 1 becomes the input of block 2, and so on.
summary: One block only learns simple word associations; many stacked blocks progressively refine representations from grammar to deep reasoning.

text: The Stack - How Many Blocks? Different models use different numbers of blocks. In the original paper and in practice this number is called N (hence "Nx" in architecture diagrams). GPT-2 Small: 12 blocks, d_model=768. GPT-2 Medium: 24 blocks, d_model=1024. GPT-2 Large: 36 blocks, d_model=1280. GPT-3: 96 blocks, d_model=12288. What this means: in GPT-3, every single token in your prompt passes through 96 identical blocks in sequence. Each block runs the full Attention → Add & Norm → FFN → Add & Norm pipeline. That is 96 rounds of attention, 96 rounds of FFN, and 192 Add & Norm operations per token.
summary: Real models use 12 to 96 stacked blocks; GPT-3 puts every token through 96 attention+FFN pipelines and 192 Add & Norm ops.

text: Same Structure, Different Weights. Every block has the same structure (Attention → Add & Norm → FFN → Add & Norm) but every learnable weight inside each block is unique, learned separately during training. Multi-Head Attention weights: W_Q creates Queries ("what am I looking for?"); W_K creates Keys ("what do I contain?"); W_V creates Values ("what info do I pass along?"); W_O blends all 8 heads back together. Add & Norm #1 (after Attention): gamma1 is the learnable scale per dimension that stretches or shrinks normalized values; beta1 is the learnable shift per dimension that moves the center up or down. Feed-Forward Network: W1, b1 are the first layer expanding from 512 to 2048 dims (the thinking space); W2, b2 are the second layer compressing 2048 back to 512 dims. Add & Norm #2 (after FFN): gamma2 and beta2 are a separate copy of gamma and beta, learned independently. Every block has its own copy of ALL these weights. Block 1 has its own W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O, W1, W2, gammas, betas. Block 2 has completely different weights. Block 96 has yet another set. If they shared weights, stacking 96 blocks would be no better than having one - you would just repeat the same transformation. Each block's unique weights let it specialize: Block 1 learns different patterns than Block 50.
summary: Every block has identical structure but its own copies of W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O, gamma1/beta1, W1/W2/b1/b2, gamma2/beta2, so each layer can specialize.

text: What Each Layer Learns - Shallow to Deep. Research on Transformer internals reveals a clear pattern: early blocks learn simple things, deep blocks learn abstract things. Blocks 1-4 learn Surface Patterns: part-of-speech (noun, verb, adjective), basic grammar and word order, simple co-occurrence ("New" near "York"). Blocks 5-12 learn Syntax & Structure: subject-verb agreement across distance, clause boundaries and nesting, pronoun resolution ("she" refers to "Alice"). Blocks 13-24 learn Semantics & Meaning: word sense disambiguation ("bank" = river vs money), sentiment and tone detection, entity relationships and facts. Blocks 25-96 learn Abstract Reasoning: multi-step inference and logic, world knowledge and common sense, task-specific meaning and generation. This is why depth matters - you need many blocks to build from grammar all the way up to meaning and reasoning. It is the same principle as deep networks from chapter 4.1, but now each block has both attention (for context) and FFN (for knowledge).
summary: Probing studies show shallow Transformer blocks capture POS and grammar, middle blocks capture syntax and semantics, deep blocks handle reasoning and world knowledge.

text: The Complete Picture - Token to Output Through N Blocks. Full journey of a single token: Token Embedding + Positional Encoding (Sections 8.2-8.7) → Block 1 (Attention, Add & Norm, FFN, Add & Norm) → Block 2 → ... → Block N → Encoder Output (contextual hidden states for each token). An Nx bracket spans Block 1 to Block N. Examples: GPT-2 Small N=12 blocks, 117M parameters; GPT-3 N=96 blocks, 175B parameters. Full recipe of a Transformer encoder: Tokenize → Embed → Add positions → Pass through N blocks (each: Self-Attention → Add & Norm → FFN → Add & Norm) → Rich contextual hidden states. The encoder uses bidirectional attention - every word can see every other word, no masking. This gives the encoder a full understanding of the entire input.

text: Chapter 12.7 Residual Connections - The Gradient Highway. Deep networks suffer vanishing gradients because each layer multiplies the gradient by a derivative that may be below 1, shrinking it exponentially (0.9^96 ≈ 0.00004). Residual connections fix this by computing output = F(x) + x. Differentiating gives F'(x) + 1, so the skip path contributes a constant gradient of 1 even when F'(x) approaches zero. In a 6-layer example, weight-path gradient decays to 0.17 (0.7^5) while skip-path gradient stays at 1.00. Every transformer block has two residuals (around Attention and around FFN), so GPT-3 has 192 gradient highways across 96 layers, making deep transformer training possible.
summary: Residual connections add the input back to the layer output so the gradient picks up a constant +1 on the skip path, preventing vanishing gradients and enabling training of 96+ layer transformers.

text: Problem: gradients vanish in deep networks. From chapter 2.8, gradients shrink as they travel backward through layers. During backpropagation, each layer multiplies the gradient by its derivative. If derivative is even slightly below 1.0, gradient shrinks exponentially. Multiplying through 96 layers: F'(x) = 0.99 gives 0.99^96 = 0.38 (even near-perfect derivatives lose 62% of the gradient). F'(x) = 0.9 gives 0.9^96 = 0.0000396 (gradient effectively zero by layer 1). F'(x) = 0.5 gives 0.5^96 = 1.3 x 10^-29 (completely gone, no learning possible). GPT-3 has 96 layers. Without a fix, the gradient arriving at layer 1 from layer 96 would be essentially zero. Layer 1 would never learn. The model would be dead on arrival.
summary: Deep networks suffer vanishing gradients because each layer multiplies the gradient by a sub-1 derivative, shrinking it exponentially across 96 layers.

text: The residual connection: output = F(x) + x. The fix is deceptively simple: add the input directly to the output. The + x creates a shortcut path that bypasses the entire layer. Path A without residual: input x then weight1 then activation then weight2 then activation then output. Gradient must travel through every layer and shrinks at each step. Path B with residual: same stack but final output is F(x) + x. The + x adds a direct highway that bypasses all layers via a skip connection from input straight to output.
summary: A residual connection adds the input x back to the layer output F(x), creating a skip shortcut so gradients can bypass the layer stack.

text: Why it solves vanishing gradients - the math. Take the derivative of the residual output: d/dx [F(x) + x] = F'(x) + 1. F'(x) is the gradient through the layer weights and can shrink toward 0. The + 1 is the gradient through the skip connection and is ALWAYS exactly 1, no matter what. That + 1 is everything. Even if F'(x) approaches 0, the total gradient is at least 1. The gradient through the skip connection is always 1, regardless of what the layer weights do. Without residual: gradient = F'(x); if F'(x) = 0.01, gradient = 0.01. With residual: gradient = F'(x) + 1; if F'(x) = 0.01, gradient = 1.01.
summary: Differentiating F(x) + x yields F'(x) + 1, so the skip path contributes a constant gradient of 1 that prevents the total gradient from collapsing.

text: The gradient highway. Visualize a 6-layer transformer. Gradients travel backward from Layer 6 to Layer 1 via two paths. At each layer the weight path gradient is multiplied by 0.7, so progress through weights: layer 6 = 1.00, layer 5 = 0.70, layer 4 = 0.49, layer 3 = 0.34, layer 2 = 0.24, layer 1 = 0.17. Through skip: always 1.00 at every layer. Through weights total: 0.7^5 = 0.17 (lost 83% of the signal). Through skip connections: 1.00 (full signal preserved). The skip path delivers the gradient from layer 6 to layer 1 without any multiplication.
summary: A 6-layer worked example shows weight-path gradient decaying from 1.00 to 0.17 across layers while the skip path stays at 1.00 throughout.

text: Why this is critical for transformers. Modern transformers are absurdly deep. Without residual connections, training them would be impossible. GPT-2: 12 layers, 24 residuals. GPT-3: 96 layers, 192 residuals. GPT-4 (est.): 120 layers, 240 residuals. TWO residual connections per block: every transformer block has two sub-layers, Attention and FFN. Each gets its own residual connection (chapter 12.2). GPT-3's 96 blocks have 192 gradient highways - one around every Attention layer and one around every FFN layer. Block flow: Input then Attention + Residual #1 then FFN + Residual #2 then Block Output. Without residual connections, gradients would vanish by layer 3. With them, gradients flow freely through all 96 layers on the skip path, letting even the earliest layers learn from the loss signal. This is what makes deep transformers trainable.
summary: Each transformer block has two residual connections (around Attention and around FFN), giving GPT-3 192 gradient highways that make 96-layer training possible.

text: Chapter 12.8 Pre-Norm vs Post-Norm contrasts the two LayerNorm placements in a transformer block. Post-Norm (original 2017 paper, BERT) places LayerNorm after the residual Add so the skip path passes through normalization. Pre-Norm (GPT, LLaMA, Mistral, Claude, and modern LLMs) places LayerNorm before each sub-layer and keeps the residual stream as pure addition with the raw input x. A numeric trace on [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6] shows the difference: post-norm normalizes the summed output, while pre-norm normalizes only the sub-layer input. Pre-norm wins for deep models because the pure-addition skip path gives gradient derivative = 1 across blocks and tolerates larger learning rates without the careful warmup post-norm requires.
summary: Chapter overview of pre-norm vs post-norm, covering block order, a worked numeric example, gradient and training-stability advantages of pre-norm, and which historical and modern models use each.

text: Post-Norm (Original 2017 Paper). In the original "Attention Is All You Need" paper, LayerNorm comes after the residual Add. This is the version covered in chapter 12.2. Exact order: Input x -> Attention(x) -> Add: x + Attention(x) -> LayerNorm -> FFN -> Add: prev + FFN(prev) -> LayerNorm. Key feature: LayerNorm sits after the residual add. The residual path passes through LayerNorm before reaching the next sub-layer.
summary: Post-Norm places LayerNorm after the residual Add so the skip path itself passes through normalization.

text: Pre-Norm (Modern Approach). In pre-norm, LayerNorm comes before each sub-layer. The residual add receives the raw, unnormalized input - not the normalized version. Order: Input x -> LayerNorm(x) -> Attention(LayerNorm(x)) -> Add: x + Attention(LayerNorm(x)) -> LayerNorm(prev) -> FFN(LayerNorm(prev)) -> Add: prev + FFN(LayerNorm(prev)). Key difference: the Add step receives the raw input x, not the normalized version. LayerNorm only feeds into the sub-layer, not into the skip path.
summary: Pre-Norm applies LayerNorm before each sub-layer and keeps the residual skip path on the raw, unnormalized input.

text: Side-by-Side Comparison. Trace input [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6] ("cats" embedding) through the Attention sub-layer in both approaches. Post-Norm: Input x = [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6]; Attention(x) = [0.3, -0.1, 0.2, 0.4]; Add: x + Attn = [-0.2, 0.2, 0.9, 1.0]; LayerNorm = [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]. Norm is last. Skip path passes through Norm. Pre-Norm: Input x = [-0.5, 0.3, 0.7, 0.6]; LayerNorm(x) = [-1.36, -0.55, 0.86, 1.06]; Attn(LN(x)) = [0.28, -0.12, 0.18, 0.38]; Add: x + Attn = [-0.22, 0.18, 0.88, 0.98]. Norm is first. Skip path is clean - just addition. Critical difference: in post-norm, the skip path goes through LayerNorm; in pre-norm, the skip path is pure addition - no transformations on the highway.

text: Why Pre-Norm Wins. Two key advantages make pre-norm the clear winner for deep models. (1) Cleaner Gradient Flow: In post-norm, the gradient on the skip path must pass through LayerNorm at every block. LayerNorm has its own derivative that modifies the gradient - not as bad as vanishing, but it adds noise and makes optimization harder. In pre-norm, the skip path is pure addition. The gradient flows from the last block to the first block on a clean highway with derivative = 1 at every step. No LayerNorm interference on the residual stream. (2) Training Stability: Post-norm requires very careful learning rate warmup - start with a tiny learning rate and slowly increase it over thousands of steps. Without warmup, gradients in early training are unstable and the model diverges (loss goes to infinity). Pre-norm is more forgiving. It works with larger learning rates from the start. The clean gradient highway stabilizes training even without careful warmup schedules. This makes training faster and less finicky.
summary: Pre-norm wins because its pure-addition skip path gives derivative=1 gradient flow and tolerates larger learning rates without the careful warmup that post-norm demands.

text: Who Uses What. Post-Norm: "Attention Is All You Need" (2017), early BERT models, some early translation models. Historical - used mainly in the original paper. Pre-Norm: GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4; LLaMA, LLaMA 2, LLaMA 3; Mistral, Mixtral; Claude; virtually all modern LLMs. The industry standard since ~2019. The Field Converged: the shift happened because pre-norm trains faster, scales better to deep models, and is more robust. When you hear about a modern LLM with 96+ layers, it is almost certainly using pre-norm. The original paper used post-norm, but practice proved pre-norm superior for large-scale training.
summary: Industry usage: post-norm appears in the 2017 paper and early BERT, while pre-norm dominates GPT, LLaMA, Mistral, Claude, and essentially all modern deep LLMs since ~2019.

text: Chapter 8.9 contrasts Batch Normalization and Layer Normalization. Batch Norm (Ioffe and Szegedy, 2015) normalizes across the batch dimension - for each feature, it computes mean and variance across all examples in the batch. It works for CNNs because images share structure, have fixed sizes, and provide meaningful per-position statistics, and it sped up CNN training 10-14x. But Batch Norm fails for language because sequences have variable lengths (position 5 may not exist in shorter sentences), inference batch size is often 1 (no batch to compute statistics over), and tokens at the same position across different sentences share nothing semantically. Layer Norm (Ba et al., 2016) instead normalizes across the feature dimension - each token computes mean and variance across its own features, independent of the batch. This is why Transformers use Layer Norm: each token is its own universe, normalization works at any batch size or sequence length, and statistics are semantically meaningful per token.
summary: Compares Batch Norm (normalizes down the batch column, used in CNNs) with Layer Norm (normalizes across one token's features, used in Transformers) and explains why language demands Layer Norm.

text: Batch Normalization (Ioffe and Szegedy, 2015) normalizes across the batch dimension. Given a batch of examples, for each feature, compute the mean and variance across all examples in the batch. Concrete grid: a batch of 4 examples, each with 3 features. Example 1 = [0.5, 1.2, -0.3]. Example 2 = [0.8, 0.9, 0.1]. Example 3 = [0.3, 1.5, -0.5]. Example 4 = [0.6, 0.6, 0.2]. Batch Norm picks one feature column and computes mean and variance across the 4 examples. For Feature 2: mean = (1.2 + 0.9 + 1.5 + 0.6) / 4 = 1.05. Then it normalizes each example's value for that feature using that mean and variance.
summary: Defines Batch Normalization as normalizing each feature column across all examples in the batch, with a worked 4-example, 3-feature example computing mean 1.05 for Feature 2.

text: Why Batch Norm works for images and CNNs. Three reasons. (1) Shared statistics: images in a batch share similar structure - the feature 'edge detector at position (3,5)' has a meaningful distribution across a batch of cat photos because they all have edges in roughly similar places. (2) Fixed input size: images are always the same dimensions (e.g., 224x224). Position (3,5) means the same thing in every image, so computing statistics across the batch at that position makes sense. (3) Training speedup: BN reduced internal covariate shift (layer inputs changing distribution during training), letting CNNs train 10-14x faster, and it acted as a mild regularizer. Batch Norm revolutionized CNN training in 2015. But language is a fundamentally different domain.
summary: Explains why Batch Norm succeeds for CNNs: shared image structure across batch, fixed 224x224 input size, and 10-14x training speedup via reduced internal covariate shift.

text: Why Batch Norm fails for language. Three fundamental problems. (1) Variable Sequence Lengths: sentences have different lengths. In a batch of 4 sentences - 'I love cats' (3 tokens), 'The cat sat on the mat last week' (8 tokens), 'Hello' (1 token), 'Deep learning is fascinating' (4 tokens) - what is 'position 5' across this batch? Only one sentence even has a position 5. Normalizing across the batch at position 5 is meaningless because there's nothing to compare. (2) Batch Size 1 at Inference: when you chat with an LLM, your prompt is batch size 1. There's no batch to compute statistics over. Batch Norm must fall back to running averages stored from training - a fragile approximation that can drift from the true distribution. (3) Tokens Are Fundamentally Different: normalizing feature 42 across 'the', 'cat', 'sat', and 'love' from different sentences treats them as comparable, but they're completely different words with different roles. Unlike pixels at the same position in different images, tokens at position 3 across different sentences share nothing semantically.
summary: Lists three reasons Batch Norm fails for language: variable sequence lengths make positions non-comparable, batch size 1 at inference forces fragile running averages, and tokens at the same position across sentences share no semantic meaning.

text: Layer Normalization (Ba et al., 2016) normalizes across the feature dimension. For each token independently, compute mean and variance across its own features. Same 4x3 grid as before. Layer Norm picks one example row and computes mean and variance across its 3 features. For Example 2 = [0.8, 0.9, 0.1]: mean = (0.8 + 0.9 + 0.1) / 3 = 0.60. Each token normalizes itself independently. Three properties matter. No batch dependency: each token normalizes using only its own features, works with batch size 1. Any sequence length: token 5 normalizes itself the same way whether the sentence has 5 or 500 tokens. Semantically meaningful: normalizing across a single token's features rescales its representation without mixing different words' statistics.
summary: Defines Layer Normalization as per-token normalization across feature dimensions, with worked example mean 0.60 for [0.8, 0.9, 0.1], highlighting batch independence, length independence, and semantic validity.

text: The key difference: which dimension is normalized? Batch Norm normalizes down columns (across examples in the batch); the highlighted column = one BN operation. Properties: needs a batch of examples, used in CNNs (image models), fragile at batch size 1. Layer Norm normalizes across rows (across features of one token); the highlighted row = one LN operation. Properties: independent per token, used in Transformers (LLMs), works with any batch size. The Key Insight: Layer Norm treats each token as its own universe. Its mean and variance depend only on that token's own feature values - not on what other sentences happen to be in the same batch, not on how long other sequences are, and not on what other tokens exist. This independence is exactly what Transformers need: each token's normalization should be a property of that token alone.
summary: Side-by-side summary: Batch Norm normalizes down columns (CNNs, fragile at batch=1), Layer Norm normalizes across rows (Transformers, batch-independent), with each token as its own universe.

text: Chapter 9.1 Decoder-Only - How Modern LLMs Work explains why ChatGPT, Claude, LLaMA, and Gemini use only the decoder half of the original 2017 Transformer. Translation needs encoder + decoder because input (English) and output (Hindi) are different languages. Chatting is one continuous English stream so encoder is unnecessary. Decoder-only relies on causal masking: token N attends only to tokens 0..N-1. The chapter compares three architectures: Encoder-Decoder (T5, BART) for translation, Encoder-Only (BERT, RoBERTa) for classification, Decoder-Only (GPT-4, Claude, LLaMA, Gemini) for generation. Concludes the CNN -> RNN -> Transformer -> Decoder-only evolution.
summary: Modern LLMs use only the Transformer decoder half with causal masking because chatting is one continuous stream, not two-language translation.

text: Plot twist: Modern LLMs are decoder-only. ChatGPT, Claude, LLaMA, Gemini all use only the Decoder half. They threw away the Encoder. Why throw away half the architecture? Because translation and chatting are fundamentally different.

text: Translation vs Chatting: different tasks. Translation: input (English) and output (Hindi) are different languages - you must fully process the input before generating output. Chatting: input (your prompt) and output (my response) are both English. They are part of one continuous conversation, not two separate things. Translation has two separate sides: English input (complete) goes to Hindi output (generated). Chatting is one continuous stream: prompt 'What is 2+2?' to response 'The answer is 4.'
summary: Translation needs two-sided encoder-decoder because languages differ; chatting is one continuous English stream so a single decoder suffices.
queries: is chatting one stream or two
two languages vs one language transformer

text: Causal masking: the key difference. In a decoder-only model, when generating token N, you can only look at tokens 0 through N-1 (past and present). You cannot look at future tokens. This is called causal masking - causality: effect cannot precede cause. Processing 'What is 2+2?' sequentially: Step 1 generate 'What' looking at nothing []; Step 2 generate 'is' looking at [What]; Step 3 generate '2+2' looking at [What, is]; Step 4 generate '?' looking at [What, is, 2+2]. This is the fundamental constraint of LLMs: they generate one token at a time, and each new token is based only on what came before.
summary: Causal masking forces each generated token to attend only to previous tokens, never the future.
queries: why can't LLM see future tokens
token N attends to which tokens
why no future attention in GPT
why one token at a time

text: Why decoder-only wins. Decoder-only is simpler (half the architecture) but a large decoder trained on enough data can: 1) Read and understand your prompt (implicit encoder task), 2) Generate natural, coherent responses (explicit decoder task), 3) Reason, code, translate, summarize (with prompting/fine-tuning). The result: decoder-only models are more general-purpose than any other architecture. They are simpler, faster, and more scalable.
summary: A large decoder-only model implicitly handles understanding and generation, making it simpler, faster, and more general-purpose than encoder-decoder.

text: Three architectures: the complete picture. Encoder-Decoder (E + D): task Translation; models T5, BART, mT5; best for when input and output are different languages or modes. Encoder-Only (E): task Understanding; models BERT, RoBERTa, DistilBERT; best for classification, sentiment, similarity - no generation. Decoder-Only (D): task Generation; models GPT-4, Claude, LLaMA, Gemini; best for chat, writing, reasoning, all modern LLMs.
summary: Three transformer flavors map to three task types: encoder-decoder for translation (T5/BART), encoder-only for understanding (BERT), decoder-only for generation (GPT/Claude).

text: You now understand the complete evolution: CNN (local patterns) -> RNN (sequential memory, slow) -> Transformer (attention, parallel) -> Decoder-only (the architecture of modern AI). You now have the full history. The upcoming sections go deep into each piece of the Transformer: how input enters, how attention works, and how the complete encoder and decoder blocks are built.

text: Chapter 9.2 explains causal masking in transformer attention. During text generation, future tokens don't exist yet, but during training the model sees the whole sentence at once for efficiency. The causal mask artificially hides future positions by adding -∞ to the upper triangle of the Q·K^T/√d_k score matrix, so softmax turns those scores into exactly 0. The chapter walks through the score matrix, the mask matrix, masked scores, softmax output, bidirectional vs causal comparison, the training insight that masking lets N tokens produce N honest next-token predictions in one forward pass, and the three architectures: encoder-only (BERT, no mask, bidirectional understanding), decoder-only (GPT/Claude/LLaMA, causal mask, generation), and encoder-decoder (T5/BART, both plus cross-attention).
summary: Causal masking hides future tokens by adding -∞ to upper triangle of score matrix so softmax produces 0; enables honest training and powers encoder-only, decoder-only, and encoder-decoder architectures.

text: The problem: during generation, future words don't exist yet. When the model generates text one token at a time, it has only produced the tokens so far - the next word hasn't been generated, so it can't attend to something that doesn't exist. Generating 'The cat sat on the mat': step 1 'The' can only see itself and predicts 'cat'; step 2 'cat' sees 'The' and itself and predicts 'sat'; step 3 'sat' sees 'The', 'cat', and itself and predicts 'on'; step 4 'on' sees all 4 previous tokens and predicts 'the'. But during training, the model sees the entire sentence at once for efficiency - so we need to artificially hide future tokens. That's what the causal mask does.
summary: Generation produces one token at a time so future tokens don't exist, but training feeds the whole sentence at once - causal mask artificially recreates the generation constraint during training.

text: Who can look at whom? Each row is a word asking 'who can I attend to?' Each column is a word being looked at. A word can only see words generated before it plus itself. Using words ['The', 'cat', 'sat', 'on']: 'The' (1st) - only itself exists; 'cat' (2nd) sees 'The' + itself; 'sat' (3rd) sees 'The', 'cat' + itself; 'on' (4th) sees everything before + itself. The diagonal = each word seeing itself (always allowed). Below diagonal = looking at earlier words (allowed - they already exist). Above diagonal = looking at future words (forbidden - not generated yet). The actual Q·K^T/√d_k score matrix is shown with the same color coding: green cells (past + self, allowed) keep their scores, red cells (future) must be blocked. Raw scores row 1: [2.1, 0.5, 0.3, 0.8]; row 2: [1.2, 2.4, 0.7, 0.4]; row 3: [0.6, 1.8, 2.0, 1.1]; row 4: [0.9, 0.3, 1.5, 2.3].
summary: Lower triangle plus diagonal of the score matrix is allowed (past + self), upper triangle is forbidden (future tokens) - shown as checkmarks/crosses and color-coded scores.

text:
The mask: a triangle of negative infinity. We add a mask matrix to the scores. The mask has 0 where a word is allowed to look, and -∞ where it's forbidden. Formula: masked_scores = scores + mask, where mask[i][j] = 0 if j ≤ i, else -∞. Adding -∞ to a score makes it -∞. When softmax sees -∞, it outputs exactly 0 - zero attention, as if that word doesn't exist. The lower triangle (0s) keeps scores unchanged. For 4 tokens the mask matrix is:
row 'The' → [0, -∞, -∞, -∞]
row 'cat' → [0, 0, -∞, -∞]
row 'sat' → [0, 0, 0, -∞]
row 'on'  → [0, 0, 0, 0]
summary: Mask matrix has 0 on lower triangle (keeps scores) and -∞ on upper triangle (forces zero after softmax); masked_scores = scores + mask.

text:
After masking → softmax only sees allowed tokens. The masked scores grid - future positions are now -∞:
'The' → [2.1, -∞, -∞, -∞]
'cat' → [1.2, 2.4, -∞, -∞]
'sat' → [0.6, 1.8, 2.0, -∞]
'on'  → [0.9, 0.3, 1.5, 2.3]
The green cells keep their original scores. The red -∞ cells will become exactly 0 after softmax - as if those future words don't exist.
summary: Masked scores keep original values on lower triangle and replace upper triangle with -∞, ready to feed into softmax.

text: Softmax turns masked scores into attention weights (probabilities). Applying softmax row-by-row to the masked scores yields the attention weight matrix. 'The' puts 100% attention on itself: [1.00, 0.00, 0.00, 0.00]. 'cat' splits between 'The' and itself with weights summing to 1.0 across visible tokens only. 'sat' splits across 'The', 'cat', 'sat'. 'on' attends across all 4 tokens. Every -∞ became exactly 0.00 after softmax. Each row still sums to 1.0 - but only across the visible (past + self) tokens. The future positions are dead.
summary: Row-wise softmax on masked scores produces attention probabilities where future positions are 0.00 and each row sums to 1.0 across visible tokens only.

text: Bidirectional vs Causal - side by side visual comparison. The attention formula is identical; the ONLY difference is whether the upper triangle is masked. Left: 'No mask (bidirectional)' shows full 4x4 grid where every word sees every word - all 16 cells active with softmax probabilities. Right: 'Causal mask' shows only the lower triangle filled with probabilities, upper triangle dark/zero - each word sees only past + self. Same Q, K, V matrices, same formula, same softmax. The mask is a single triangle of -∞ values and it completely changes what the model can do.

text: The Training Insight: Every Position Makes a Prediction. 4 tokens go in, 4 output vectors come out - always, regardless of masking. Each output vector v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4 passes through Linear + Softmax to predict the next token. The mask determines whether those predictions are honest. Without mask (bidirectional): every position saw the full sequence including the answer it was supposed to predict → 0 usable training examples, every prediction 'saw the answer'. With causal mask: position 1 ('The') saw ['The'] → predicts 'cat' (HONEST); position 2 ('cat') saw ['The','cat'] → predicts 'sat' (HONEST); position 3 ('sat') saw ['The','cat','sat'] → predicts 'on' (HONEST); position 4 ('on') saw all 4 → predicts 'the' (HONEST). Result: 4 usable training examples from one forward pass. This is why causal masking exists during training - from one forward pass with N tokens you get N honest next-token predictions. Without the mask you'd get zero - the model would 'cheat' at every position, then completely fail at actual generation.
summary: Causal mask gives N honest next-token training examples from one forward pass; without it the model cheats by seeing the answer and fails at generation.

text: Encoder-only architecture - BERT, RoBERTa. No mask - every word sees every other word (bidirectional). The attention grid is the full 4x4 matrix with all 16 cells active. Why? The encoder's job is to understand the full input. When classifying 'The bank by the river', 'bank' NEEDS to see 'river' (which comes after it) to know it means riverbank, not a financial bank. Hiding future words would cripple understanding. Used for: text classification, sentiment analysis, question answering, named entity recognition.
summary: Encoder-only models like BERT use no mask so every token sees every other token bidirectionally - needed for full-input understanding tasks like classification and NER.
queries: why does BERT use no mask
why does bank need to see river
why no causal mask for understanding

text: Decoder-only architecture - GPT, Claude, LLaMA. Causal mask - each word only sees past words + itself. The attention grid shows only the lower triangle active (10 cells of 16 for 4 tokens), upper triangle masked. Why? The decoder generates text one token at a time. At generation step 3, token 4 doesn't exist yet - attending to it would be looking at something that isn't there. During training, the full sentence is fed at once for speed, so we use the mask to simulate the generation condition. Without it, the model would 'cheat' by seeing the answer, then fail at actual generation. Used for: text generation, chatbots, code completion, reasoning - all modern LLMs.
summary: Decoder-only LLMs (GPT, Claude, LLaMA) use causal mask so each token sees only past + self - matches generation constraint and enables N honest predictions during training.
queries: why does GPT use causal mask
why mask simulates generation during training
all modern LLMs use what mask

text: Encoder-decoder architecture - Original Transformer, T5, BART. Both - encoder uses no mask, decoder uses causal mask, plus cross-attention connecting them. Encoder shows full 4x4 matrix, decoder shows lower triangle, joined by '+'. Why? The input (e.g., English sentence) already exists fully - the encoder should see all of it. But the output (e.g., Hindi translation) is generated word by word - the decoder must not peek ahead. Cross-attention lets the decoder look at the encoder's full understanding while still generating left-to-right. Used for: translation, summarization, any 'input → different output' task. The core rule: if the text already exists (input you're reading) → no mask needed, let every word see everything for maximum understanding. If the text is being generated (output you're writing) → causal mask required, because future words don't exist yet.
summary: Encoder-decoder models (T5, BART, original Transformer) use no mask in encoder + causal mask in decoder + cross-attention; core rule: existing text uses no mask, generated text uses causal mask.

text: Cross-attention architecture diagram showing the encoder stack producing K and V projections that are cached, then fanned out to every decoder layer; the decoder supplies Q while K and V come from the encoder output. Includes dimensions: encoder output [3 x 512], W_K and W_V [512 x 64] per head producing K [3 x 64] and V [3 x 64], decoder input [4 x 512], Q [4 x 64] per layer, and Q·K^T scores [4 x 3].

text: Cross-attention is the bridge between encoder and decoder in an encoder-decoder Transformer. The decoder generates the Query (Q) while the encoder supplies the Keys (K) and Values (V) - so the decoder asks 'which input token should I look at?' against the encoder's final output. The math is identical to self-attention: softmax(Q·K^T / √d_k) · V. The encoder runs once; K and V are computed once from the encoder's final layer using W_K and W_V, then cached and reused by every decoder layer. Only Q changes per decoder layer because the decoder's representation evolves. The score matrix is rectangular (M decoder tokens x N encoder tokens), not square. Each decoder layer in the original Transformer has three sub-layers: masked self-attention, cross-attention, and feed-forward. Decoder-only models like GPT, Claude, and LLaMA have no cross-attention at all because they have no encoder. Encoder-decoder models like the original Transformer, T5, and BART use cross-attention in every decoder layer for tasks like translation and summarization.
summary: Chapter overview of cross-attention: Q from decoder, K and V from encoder computed once and cached across all decoder layers.

text: Setup: translating 'I love cats' to Hindi 'Mujhe billiyaan pasand hain'. The encoder has fully processed the English sentence, producing 3 contextual 768-dim vectors - one per word - where 'love' no longer just means 'love' but 'love in the context of I-love-cats'. The decoder generates Hindi word by word: 'Mujhe', 'billiyaan', 'pasand' are already generated, and 'hain' is the next prediction. The decoder's self-attention lets Hindi words look at other Hindi words, but cross-attention is what lets the decoder look at the English words coming out of the encoder.
summary: Translation setup motivating cross-attention: encoder emits 3 contextual English vectors that the decoder must consult while generating Hindi.

text: Key idea: in self-attention Q, K, V all come from the same sentence. In cross-attention they come from different sources. Query (Q) comes from the decoder (Hindi) - the decoder asks 'what English word should I pay attention to right now?'. Key (K) comes from the encoder (English) - the encoder's words offer their labels for matching, like 'I'm the subject' or 'I'm the verb'. Value (V) also comes from the encoder (English) - the encoder's words offer their actual content, the rich contextual meaning to borrow from. The formula is identical: Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(Q·K^T / √d_k) · V. Only the source of Q, K, V changes.
summary: Q sourced from decoder while K and V are sourced from encoder, using the same scaled dot-product attention formula.

text: Worked cross-attention scores for 'I love cats' to 'Mujhe billiyaan pasand hain'. The score matrix is 4 Hindi rows x 3 English columns (not square). Raw scores (Q·K^T / √d_k): Mujhe → [2.8, 0.4, 0.3] on [I, love, cats]; billiyaan → [0.3, 0.7, 3.4]; pasand → [0.5, 3.1, 0.6]; hain → [0.4, 2.7, 0.5]. After softmax of each row, attention weights peak heavily on the aligned English word: 'Mujhe' puts most weight on 'I' (subject/experiencer), 'billiyaan' puts most weight on 'cats' (direct object), 'pasand' puts most weight on 'love' (core verb meaning), 'hain' aligns with 'love' to complete the predicate (auxiliary 'are/is').

text: Self-attention vs cross-attention - same formula, different wiring. Self-attention: Q, K, V all from the same sentence, words attend to each other within one language, score matrix is square (N x N). Cross-attention: Q from decoder, K and V from encoder, decoder attends to encoder's output, score matrix is rectangular (M x N). Each decoder layer has BOTH types of attention: (1) Masked self-attention - Hindi words look at other Hindi words with a causal mask; (2) Cross-attention - Hindi words look at English words with no mask because the full English sentence already exists.
summary: Side-by-side comparison: self-attention has one source and a square N x N matrix; cross-attention has two sources and a rectangular M x N matrix, and both live in every decoder layer.

text: Critical detail: the encoder runs ONCE. A common misconception is that encoder layer 1 feeds decoder layer 1, encoder layer 2 feeds decoder layer 2, and so on - this is WRONG. The CORRECT picture: the encoder runs all 6 layers once on 'I love cats'. The output of layer 6 (final layer) is the encoder's output - 3 contextual vectors, one per English word. K and V are computed once from that final output using W_K and W_V weight matrices, then cached. Decoder layers 1-6 each compute their own Q because the decoder's representation changes layer by layer as it gets refined - but K and V are always the same encoder-final-output projections.
summary: Only the encoder's final layer feeds every decoder layer; K and V are computed once from it and reused while each decoder layer recomputes its own Q.

text: Architecture diagram with dimensions (d_model=512, d_k=64, 8 heads). Left: ENCODER stack of 6 layers processing 'I love cats' [3 x 512]; only Layer 6 (final) is highlighted. An arrow leaves layer 6 into a Projection block that computes K = encoder_out · W_K and V = encoder_out · W_V, where [3 x 512] · [512 x 64] = [3 x 64] per head. The result enters a dashed K, V CACHE box (K: [3 x 64], V: [3 x 64]). From the cache, six dashed fan-out arrows reach every layer of the DECODER stack on the right (processing 'Mujhe billiyaan pasand hain' [4 x 512]). Each decoder layer shows a Q badge labeled 'changes' and a K,V badge labeled 'same', emphasizing that Q is recomputed per layer while K and V are reused.

text: Each decoder layer computes Attn(Q_n, K, V) = softmax(Q_n · K^T / √64) · V, with the same K and V across all layers and only Q_n changing per layer n. Layer 1 uses Q_1 from decoder layer 1's output; layer 6 uses Q_6 from layer 6's output. The decoder's representation evolves layer by layer - Q captures 'what I'm looking for' at each stage, while K and V capture 'what the encoder said' and never change. Dimension walkthrough with d_model=512, heads=8, d_k=64: Encoder output [3 x 512] (3 English tokens). W_K projection [512 x 64] per head. Cached K [3 x 64], cached V [3 x 64]. Decoder layer n state [4 x 512]. Q_n [4 x 64] per layer. Q_n · K^T scores [4 x 3] - every Hindi word scores every English word. Why efficient: encoder runs once, K and V computed once; only Q changes per layer, saving roughly 6x encoder-side computation for a 6-layer decoder. Each decoder layer has 6 separate learned weight matrices: W_Q, W_K, W_V for masked self-attention (all from Hindi) and another W_Q, W_K, W_V for cross-attention (Q from Hindi, K and V from English), all learned independently.
summary: Per-layer formula Attn(Q_n, K, V) = softmax(Q_n·K^T/√64)·V with cached K, V and changing Q, plus full dimension walkthrough and the count of 6 weight matrices per decoder layer.

text: Where cross-attention lives. Decoder-only models (GPT, Claude, LLaMA): no cross-attention at all - there's no encoder, so nothing to cross-attend to; the decoder handles everything with self-attention, and prompt plus response are one continuous sequence. Encoder-decoder models (original Transformer, T5, BART): cross-attention in every decoder layer. Each decoder layer has three sub-layers in order: (1) Masked self-attention - Hindi looks at Hindi (causal); (2) Cross-attention - Hindi looks at English (full, no mask); (3) Feed-forward network - process each position independently. Cross-attention is elegant but optional: modern LLMs show world-class results without it, but for tasks with clear input/output separation like translation and summarization, encoder-decoder with cross-attention remains powerful.
summary: Decoder-only LLMs skip cross-attention entirely; encoder-decoder Transformers place it as sub-layer 2 in every decoder block between masked self-attention and the feed-forward network.

text: Chapter 9.4 explains the Transformer's output head: the final stage that converts a 512-dim hidden state into a predicted token. It covers the Linear layer (a 512 x 50,257 weight matrix W producing logits via dot products with each vocabulary word), Softmax (exponentiating logits and normalizing so probabilities sum to 1), Temperature scaling (dividing logits by T to control how peaked the distribution is), Sampling strategies (Greedy, Top-k, Top-p / nucleus), Weight tying (reusing the embedding matrix transposed as W to save 25.7M parameters), and how the head behaves differently during training (every position predicts) vs inference (only the last position).
summary: Chapter-level overview of the Transformer output head: Linear, softmax, temperature, sampling, weight tying, and training vs inference behavior.
