how does domain adaptation for embeddings work
how to adapt embeddings to medical legal code domain
fine-tune vs off the shelf embedder

text: Off-the-shelf embeddings sometimes miss domain semantics. General-purpose embedding models are trained on web text - they know cat and dog are similar but have barely seen MI (myocardial infarction) or tPA (tissue plasminogen activator). On a medical corpus this shows up as low cosine similarity between an abbreviation and its full term, which wrecks retrieval. Cosine similarity on a medical corpus: 'MI' vs 'Myocardial infarction' - off-the-shelf 0.42, fine-tuned 0.95. 'tPA contraindicated' vs 'Tissue plasminogen activator should not be given' - off-the-shelf 0.51, fine-tuned 0.90. Recall@10 on a held-out medical query set: off-the-shelf embedder 0.62, fine-tuned on medical corpus 0.88 - a 26-point recall lift. That is the value of teaching the embedder your domain.
summary: Off-the-shelf embedders fail on specialized vocabulary, causing huge recall drops on domain corpora.
queries: why do general embedders fail on medical text
why off the shelf embedder bad for legal docs
how much does fine-tuning improve recall

text: Contrastive fine-tuning: pull positives close, push negatives away. We do not retrain the embedder from scratch - we take the existing model and nudge its vector space using triples: an anchor query, a positive document that should match, and a negative document that should not. Each training step pulls anchor closer to positive and farther from negative. Triplet loss formula: L_triplet = max(0, d(A, P) - d(A, N) + margin), where d = distance, A = anchor, P = positive, N = negative, margin = 0.2 typical. The loss is zero only when the negative is at least 'margin' farther from the anchor than the positive. If the negative is too close, the gradient pushes it away. The margin prevents the loss from going to zero just because positives are slightly closer than negatives.
summary: Triplet loss formula and contrastive learning mechanism that warps embedding space to favor true matches.
queries: what is the margin in triplet loss
why use a margin in triplet loss

text: Pair construction decides quality - the training data is the model. Each row is one triple: anchor (real user query), positive (chunk that should match), negative (chunk that should not). Example triples from a support corpus: anchor 'How do I reset my password?' / positive '[doc-1, chunk 1] - To reset your password, visit the account settings page and click Forgot Password' / negative '[doc-3, chunk 1] - Refunds are processed within 5-7 business days of approval'. Anchor 'I can't sign in' / positive '[doc-25] Login Troubleshooting: clear cookies, verify caps lock, and check for typos in your email' / negative '[doc-7] Dashboard tour: the home screen shows your usage metrics and recent activity'. Anchor 'Cancel my subscription' / positive '[doc-15] Account Deletion Flow: go to billing settings and click Cancel Plan to stop renewal' / negative '[doc-2] Email Change: update your contact email from the profile settings page'. Hard negatives teach the most: a hard negative sits NEAR the positive in vector space but is irrelevant - same vocabulary, wrong topic. Mining hard negatives (top-20 retrieval results minus the true positive) teaches the actual semantic boundary. Easy negatives (random docs from unrelated topics) waste compute because the gap is already obvious to the base model.
summary: How to construct anchor/positive/negative triples and why hard-negative mining matters more than random sampling.
queries: how to build training data for embedding fine-tune
examples of training triples for support corpus
mine hard negatives from top-20 retrieval
how to label query positive pairs

text: When is fine-tuning worth the cost? Fine-tuning takes labeled data, compute time, and a recurring re-training budget as your corpus drifts. Fine-tune when: highly specialized vocabulary (medical, legal, code); off-the-shelf recall below 80% on your golden set; you have 5k+ query-positive pairs labeled; budget for periodic re-training as embeddings drift. Skip and use off-the-shelf when: general English domain; common domain (e-commerce, support, news); fewer than 5k labeled pairs available; recall already above 80% on the golden set. The cleanest signal: build a small golden set (200-500 query-positive pairs) and measure recall with the off-the-shelf model. Below 80%, fine-tuning is usually worth it. Above 80%, the marginal gain rarely pays for the recurring training cost.
summary: Decision rubric for when to fine-tune embeddings vs sticking with off-the-shelf based on domain, data, and golden-set recall.
queries: fine-tune vs off the shelf decision

text: Before vs after on the support corpus: fine-tuning on 50k support-ticket triples on BGE-large. Recall and MRR jump, latency does not - fine-tuning changes vector geometry, not the index or query path. Recall@5: before 0.78, after 0.91. MRR: before 0.62, after 0.84. Latency: before 30 ms, after 30 ms. Training loss over 3 epochs (50k triples): epoch 0 loss 0.50, epoch 1 loss 0.22, epoch 2 loss 0.10, epoch 3 loss 0.05. Cost: ~$200 for a 50k-pair fine-tune on BGE-large via a managed service. Quality delta: usually 10-20 points on recall. Re-evaluate every 3-6 months as the corpus drifts and new query patterns appear.

Hybrid (RRF) bar at 0.86 (green). Caption: Recall@5 On 50 Held-Out Queries. Hybrid RRF is the tallest bar, showing +12 points over dense alone and +18 points over BM25 alone.

text: Chapter 21.3 - Hybrid Retrieval for RAG. Dense embeddings miss exact terms (API keys, error codes) while BM25 misses synonyms ("cancel" vs "Account Deletion"). Hybrid retrieval runs both and fuses rankings. Two fusion strategies: Reciprocal Rank Fusion RRF(d) = sum 1/(k + rank_r(d)) with k=60, parameter-free and robust; and weighted score Score(d) = alpha * bm25 + (1-alpha) * dense after min-max normalization. RRF worked example shows doc-25 wins because both retrievers placed it top-2. Empirically recall@5 jumps from 0.68 (BM25) and 0.74 (dense) to 0.86 (hybrid RRF) on a 50-query held-out set, costing only +30ms. Alpha can be tuned per query type: 0.7 factual, 0.3 conceptual, 0.5 default; a small LLM classifier picks alpha at runtime for 4-8 extra recall points. Decision rule: always start with RRF, add weighted-alpha and classifier only if metrics demand, re-evaluate quarterly.
summary: Hybrid retrieval fuses BM25 and dense rankings via RRF or weighted alpha, lifting recall@5 from ~0.7 to 0.86 with +30ms latency; default to RRF and tune alpha by query type only if needed.
queries: what is hybrid retrieval for RAG
RAG retrieval combining BM25 and embeddings
when to use RRF vs weighted score

text: Dense retrieval misses exact terms; BM25 misses synonyms. Hybrid search ships dense vectors and BM25 side by side and fuses their rankings. Example 1 - Query: "How do I get an API key?" Truth: doc-13 (API Keys). Dense Top-3: [doc-7, doc-12, doc-5] missed doc-13 because the embedding of "API key" sat closer to generic dev-tools docs than the actual API-Keys page. BM25 Top-3: [doc-13, doc-7, doc-12] found it. Example 2 - Query: "Subscription cancel" Truth: doc-15 (Account Deletion). Dense Top-3: [doc-15, doc-19, doc-3] found it. BM25 Top-3: [doc-3, doc-19, doc-25] missed it because the cancel page is titled "Account Deletion" and does not contain the token "cancel". Hybrid keeps both retrievers and fuses rankings - each catches what the other misses.
summary: Shows two concrete queries where dense and BM25 each fail, motivating hybrid retrieval.
queries: why does dense retrieval miss exact terms
why do we need hybrid retrieval
what retriever catches what other misses
dense top-3 vs BM25 top-3 comparison
why one retriever is not enough

text: Two fusion strategies dominate production RAG. Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF): RRF(d) = sum over retrievers r of: 1 / (k + rank_r(d)), where k = 60 (standard). Robust default. No weights to tune. Each retriever contributes a rank-reciprocal, so doc-1 always outweighs doc-2 regardless of underlying score scales. Weighted Score Fusion: Score(d) = alpha * bm25_score(d) + (1 - alpha) * dense_score(d), where alpha is in [0, 1]. Tunable per query type. Requires score normalization first - BM25 scores are unbounded positive while dense cosine sits in [-1, 1], so min-max scaling each list to [0, 1] is standard prep.
summary: Defines the two main fusion formulas - parameter-free RRF and tunable weighted score - with k=60 and alpha in [0,1].
queries: what is the constant k in RRF
how to combine BM25 and dense scores

text: RRF Worked Example. Query: "I can't sign in". BM25 Top-3: 1. doc-25, 2. doc-7, 3. doc-13. Dense Top-3: 1. doc-12, 2. doc-25, 3. doc-7. Compute RRF(d) with k = 60. doc-25: BM25 term = 1 / (60 + 1) = 0.01639, Dense term = 1 / (60 + 2) = 0.01613, RRF sum = 0.03252. doc-7: BM25 term = 1 / (60 + 2) = 0.01613, Dense term = 1 / (60 + 3) = 0.01587, RRF sum = 0.03200. doc-12: BM25 term = 0 (not in BM25 top-3), Dense term = 1 / (60 + 1) = 0.01639, RRF sum = 0.01639. doc-13: BM25 term = 1 / (60 + 3) = 0.01587, Dense term = 0 (not in dense top-3), RRF sum = 0.01587. Final Ranking: doc-25 (0.03252) > doc-7 (0.03200) > doc-12 (0.01639) > doc-13 (0.01587). doc-25 wins because both retrievers ranked it in their top-2. doc-7 is second because both kept it in top-3. Singletons trail behind.
summary: Walks through RRF math step by step for the query 'I can't sign in', showing doc-25 wins because it appears high in both lists.
queries: what happens when doc not in list RRF
fused ranking from BM25 and dense
doc appearing in both lists fusion

text: Complementary Recall: Hybrid catches what either misses. Recall@5 measured on a 50-query held-out set against the support corpus. BM25 Only: 0.68. Dense Only: 0.74. Hybrid (RRF): 0.86. Hybrid is not just an average of the two retrievers - the failures of BM25 and dense are largely uncorrelated, so fusion picks up the union of their wins. Hybrid is rarely worse than the best single method, often noticeably better. Complementary recall gain here is +12 points over dense alone. Latency Cost: +30 ms for the BM25 lookup on top of the dense search. For most RAG systems that is well below the LLM generation tail, so the recall lift is almost always worth it.
summary: Empirical recall@5 numbers show hybrid (0.86) beats BM25 (0.68) and dense (0.74) due to uncorrelated failures, at +30ms latency cost.
queries: hybrid recall improvement over BM25 dense
recall at 5 BM25 vs dense vs hybrid
how much better is hybrid retrieval
is hybrid worth the latency cost RAG
why hybrid is not just average

text: Tune Alpha By Query Type. One alpha for everything leaves recall on the table. Factual lookups (account IDs, error codes) lean BM25. Conceptual questions lean dense. A small classifier picks the right alpha per query before retrieval. Factual / Lookup: example "What is my account ID?", alpha = 0.7, Lean BM25, why: exact tokens like account IDs, error codes, and SKUs dominate the answer. Conceptual / How-Does-X-Work: example "How does our pricing model work?", alpha = 0.3, Lean Dense, why: synonyms and paraphrase dominate, the answer rarely uses the same words as the query. Mixed / Ambiguous (Default): example "Why is my charge wrong?", alpha = 0.5, Balanced, why: mix of keywords (charge, refund) and intent (something is wrong), hedge with 0.5. A simple LLM-based classifier (one call with a 3-class prompt) can label each query at runtime before retrieval. Cost: 1 extra LLM call (~$0.0002 with a small model). Adds ~150 ms but unlocks 4-8 points of recall on mixed query workloads.
summary: Per-query-type alpha tuning: 0.7 for factual lookups, 0.3 for conceptual, 0.5 default; a small classifier picks alpha at runtime for 4-8 points of recall.
queries: how to tune alpha hybrid search
examples of factual vs conceptual queries

text: Hybrid Defaults For RAG. Hybrid is the production default for RAG. Start simple with RRF and only add complexity if your evaluation metrics demand it. (1) Always start with RRF (no tuning needed). (2) Move to weighted-alpha only if you have query-type labels. (3) Add a classifier when your queries naturally split into factual vs conceptual. (4) Re-evaluate alpha quarterly as your corpus and query mix drift. The cheapest big win in RAG retrieval. If you only do one thing after picking an embedding model, ship hybrid with RRF first - it almost always beats either retriever alone with a tiny latency cost.
summary: Decision rules: start with RRF, upgrade to weighted-alpha + classifier only when metrics demand, re-evaluate quarterly.
queries: when to use RRF vs weighted alpha
should I use a query classifier
first thing to ship after embedding model
do I need to tune alpha

text: The Reranker Cascade chapter explains the 3-stage RAG retrieval pipeline: a cheap vector retriever shrinks 1M chunks to top-50, a cross-encoder reranker rescores those 50 down to top-10, and an LLM reads the top-3 to write the final answer. Each stage costs more per item but processes fewer items. End-to-end p50 latency is roughly 30 + 80 + 800 = 910 ms and cost is $0.0001 + $0.0003 + $0.0120 = $0.0124 per query, dominated by the LLM. The two tuning knobs are top-K retrieved and top-K reranked; 50 -> 10 -> 3 is the recall-per-millisecond sweet spot.
summary: Overview of the 3-stage RAG cascade with its latency, cost, and tuning knobs.

text: Each stage of a RAG cascade cuts the candidate set and pays for better quality. Rerankers (introduced in Section 17.6) re-score a candidate list using a cross-encoder that reads the query and the doc together. Applied to RAG: vector retriever is cheap and lossy, reranker is expensive and precise, LLM is most expensive and uses the smallest set. Each stage filters the work for the next. The 3-stage cascade: Vector top-50 (cheap) -> Cross-encoder rerank top-10 (medium) -> LLM reads top-3 (expensive).
summary: Why a cascade beats a single retriever: each stage filters work for the next, paying more compute per remaining item.

text: Stage 1: Vector Retrieval (Cheap And Lossy) - input 1M chunks, output top-50, cost ~30 ms / $0.0001. HNSW or IVF-PQ scan; approximate but blazing fast. Stage 2: Cross-Encoder Reranker (Medium Cost, Sharp Scores) - input 50 candidates, output top-10, cost ~80 ms / $0.0003. Reads query + each candidate jointly; 50 forward passes through a small transformer. Stage 3: LLM Reads Top-3 + Generates (Expensive, Final Answer) - input 10 reranked, output answer, cost ~800 ms / $0.0120. Top-3 chunks stuffed into the prompt; LLM does the reasoning and writes the answer. The pool shrinks ~5x at each stage, letting us spend more compute per remaining item.
summary: Detailed per-stage breakdown: vector top-50, cross-encoder top-10, LLM top-3 with their inputs, outputs, and costs.

text: Latency Budget: 30 + 80 + 800 = ~910 ms end-to-end p50 on a typical RAG deployment. Breakdown: Vector retrieval (HNSW, top-50): 30 ms. Cross-encoder reranker (50 -> 10): 80 ms. LLM generation (200 tokens, top-3): 800 ms. Total p50 latency: 910 ms. The bar is drawn to scale so you can see how the LLM swallows the budget. LLM generation dominates - optimizing reranker speed alone gives small wins. Caching the LLM call (covered in chapter 23.6) gives the biggest latency win.
summary: End-to-end RAG latency budget showing the LLM dominates at 800 ms of a 910 ms total.

text: Cost Per Query: $0.0001 + $0.0003 + $0.012 = $0.0124. Same shape as the latency budget, in dollars. Breakdown: Vector retrieval (managed HNSW): $0.0001. Cross-encoder reranker (50 cands): $0.0003. LLM generation (4000 in + 200 out): $0.0120. Total: $0.0124. LLM cost dominates; reranker is cheap, vector retrieval is essentially free. At 1M queries/day, daily cost = $12,400. Caching can cut this 50-90%.
summary: Per-query cost breakdown showing the LLM contributes $0.0120 of the $0.0124 total, dominating the bill.

text: The two knobs: Top-K Retrieved and Top-K Reranked. Three operating points on the running corpus: (1) 20 -> 5: recall 0.82, latency 850 ms, cost $0.011 - narrow, saves a touch of latency and cost but recall slips. (2) 50 -> 10 (Default): recall 0.89, latency 910 ms, cost $0.012 - default sweet spot, best recall per millisecond. (3) 100 -> 20: recall 0.92, latency 990 ms, cost $0.013 - wider, buys +3 points of recall for +80 ms, diminishing returns. Sweet spot is usually 50 -> 10 -> 3. Going wider buys diminishing returns; going narrower trades recall for latency.
summary: Tuning table comparing three top-K configurations and showing 50 -> 10 is the recall-per-millisecond sweet spot.

text: Chapter 21.5 explains why raw user queries are rarely optimal retrieval queries and previews four transformation strategies. Three failure modes: lexical mismatch (user and doc use different words like sign-in vs log-in), ambiguity (one query has multiple plausible intents like "How do I export?"), and multi-intent (one query asks two questions like cancel + refund). Four fixes: HyDE (21.6) for lexical mismatch, Multi-Query Expansion (21.7) for ambiguity, Query Decomposition (21.8) for multi-intent, and Query Routing (21.8) for picking the right index. Concrete failure: Cosine(user, best_doc) = 0.61, correct doc at rank 7.
summary: Chapter overview - three retrieval failure modes (lexical mismatch, ambiguity, multi-intent) and four query-transformation fixes (HyDE, multi-query, decomposition, routing).

text: User queries are rarely optimal for retrieval. The user types a sentence in their own words, the retriever embeds it and finds nearest chunks, but the user's wording is rarely the wording that lives in the index, so the right doc stays in the corpus while the retriever misses it. Concrete miss: user query "I can't sign in" vs best doc in corpus "Login Troubleshooting". Cosine(user, best_doc) = 0.61. Top-3 misses it. Correct doc lands at rank 7. Three failure shapes ruin naive retrieval and four query-transformation strategies fix them.
summary: Frames why raw user queries fail retrieval and motivates query-transformation strategies with a concrete cosine=0.61 / rank-7 miss.
queries: why do user queries fail retrieval
user query vs doc wording mismatch
why does the right doc rank low
what is query transformation in RAG
I can't sign in vs Login Troubleshooting

text: Lexical mismatch: the user and the doc use different words. Users and docs share an idea but not vocabulary; the retriever sees two strings of tokens that look unrelated and the right chunk falls out of top-k. Examples: user "I can't sign in" vs doc "Login Troubleshooting" (sign in vs log in); user "How do I cancel?" vs doc "Account Deletion Flow" (cancel vs delete); user "My screen is frozen" vs doc "Browser Performance Issues" (screen frozen vs browser performance). Dense embeddings help a bit (sign-in and log-in sit close in vector space) but only partially. HyDE (21.6) and multi-query (21.7) close the gap by rewriting the user query into wording that matches the doc.
summary: Defines lexical mismatch with three concrete user/doc word-pair examples and names HyDE and multi-query as fixes.
queries: what is lexical mismatch in retrieval
user and doc using different words example
sign in vs log in retrieval
screen frozen vs browser performance example
why dense embeddings only partially fix lexical mismatch
how does HyDE fix lexical mismatch

text: Ambiguity: the query has multiple plausible intents. One short question "How do I export?" has three different things the user might want. A naive retriever picks one direction and quietly drops the other two. Three branches from the same user query: "Export data to CSV" -> doc-9 (Export Formats); "Export user list for admin" -> doc-26 (Bulk Operations); "Cancel an export job that failed" -> doc-29 (Export Failures). A naive retriever picks one and misses the other two. Multi-query (21.7) generates paraphrases that cover each branch, and routing (21.8) sends each branch to the index that fits best.
summary: Shows ambiguity failure: one query splits into three plausible intents pointing at different docs, fixed by multi-query and routing.
queries: what is query ambiguity in RAG
How do I export query example
export to CSV vs bulk export vs failed export
Export Formats Bulk Operations Export Failures

text: Multi-intent: one query asks two or more questions. The user packs two asks into one sentence; the retriever embeds the whole thing and lands on docs that cover one ask, missing the other entirely. Example user query: "Cancel my subscription and get a refund". Sub-Intent 1: How to cancel subscription -> doc-15 (Account Deletion Flow). Sub-Intent 2: How to get a refund -> doc-4 (Refunds). Naive retrieval finds docs for one intent and misses the other. Decomposition (21.8) splits the query into two sub-queries, retrieves for each, then combines the results.
summary: Defines multi-intent failure where one sentence packs two asks, fixed by query decomposition into sub-queries.
queries: Cancel my subscription and get a refund example
how to handle two intents in one query

text: Four strategies map to three failures. Each transformation tackles a different failure shape. Strategies: HyDE (21.6) fixes lexical mismatch - ask an LLM to draft a hypothetical answer, then embed the answer instead of the question. Multi-Query Expansion (21.7) fixes ambiguity - generate N paraphrases of the query, retrieve for each, then merge results. Query Decomposition (21.8) fixes multi-intent - split a compound query into independent sub-queries, retrieve for each, then combine. Query Routing (21.8) picks the right index - classify the query and send it to the index, tool, or filter that best matches. Picking the right transformation is itself a routing decision; in production a small classifier or LLM call inspects the query and chooses which strategy (or combination) to run.
summary: Maps the four query-transformation strategies (HyDE, multi-query, decomposition, routing) to the three failure modes they fix.
queries: embed hypothetical answer instead of question

text: Five-box horizontal flow diagram showing the HyDE pipeline: user query, LLM generates hypothetical answer, embed hypothetical answer, vector retrieval, top-K real docs. Each box is colored differently (orange for User Query, purple for LLM Generates Hypothetical Answer, cyan for Embed Hypothetical Answer, yellow for Vector Retrieval, green for Top-K Real Docs) and connected by arrows showing left-to-right flow.
summary: Diagram of the five-stage HyDE pipeline from user query through LLM draft, embed, retrieve, to top-K real docs.
queries: What does the HyDE flow look like?

text: HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) flips standard RAG retrieval: instead of embedding the user's question, an LLM first drafts a plausible hypothetical answer, that answer is embedded, and the resulting vector is used to retrieve real documents. Because answers share shape and vocabulary with docs more than questions do, cosine similarity scores better even when the LLM's draft is factually wrong. HyDE helps long descriptive queries, lexical mismatch (sign-in vs log-in), and conceptual 'why' questions, but hurts short factual queries and latency-sensitive pipelines because it adds an LLM call (~+200-400 ms, ~$0.0005). Cache by query hash to amortize.
summary: HyDE embeds an LLM-drafted hypothetical answer instead of the raw question to improve retrieval recall.
queries: Why embed the answer instead of the question?
Does HyDE work if the LLM hallucinates?
Query rewriting with LLM for vector search
Why questions and answers live far apart in vector space

text: Don't Embed The Question - Embed The Hypothetical Answer. A user's question and the doc that answers it often live far apart in vector space. They use different words and have different shapes. HyDE flips the order: have an LLM draft a plausible answer first, then embed THAT, then retrieve. Normal RAG: Embed("Why is my dashboard slow?") -> retrieve. HyDE: LLM("Why is my dashboard slow?") = "The dashboard may be slow due to large data volumes, browser extensions, or network latency." -> Embed that ANSWER -> retrieve. The hypothetical answer is text-shape-closer to actual docs than the question is. Cosine similarity wins.
summary: HyDE's core insight: questions and answer-docs live far apart in vector space, so embed an LLM-drafted answer instead.
queries: What is the main insight behind HyDE?
Why don't questions match docs in vector space?
Why embed an answer instead of a question?
How does HyDE differ from normal RAG?
Question vs answer shape in embeddings
Why does cosine similarity prefer answers?
Flipping the order of embedding in RAG

text: The HyDE Flow: Query -> LLM -> Hypothetical Answer -> Embed -> Retrieve. HyDE adds one extra step before retrieval: an LLM call that writes a hypothetical answer. That answer is what gets embedded and sent to the vector index. The user never sees it directly. The five stages: User Query, LLM Generates Hypothetical Answer, Embed Hypothetical Answer, Vector Retrieval, Top-K Real Docs. The hypothetical answer is throwaway. Only its embedding goes to the index. The user gets the real top-K docs back, never the LLM's draft.
summary: Walks through HyDE's five-stage pipeline: query, LLM draft, embed, retrieve, top-K real docs.

Does the user see the LLM draft?
What goes into the vector index?

Why is the hypothetical answer throwaway?

text: Worked Example: 'Why Is My Dashboard Slow?' Three panels: what the user typed, what the LLM drafted, and what the retriever returned. User Query: "Why is my dashboard slow?" LLM Hypothetical Answer: "The dashboard may be slow due to large data volumes, browser extensions interfering with rendering, network latency, or background sync jobs consuming resources. Try refreshing, disabling extensions, or checking the network panel." Top-3 Retrieved Docs: doc-22 Slow Page Load Troubleshooting, doc-23 500 Errors, doc-27 Browser Compatibility Issues. The LLM's draft pulled in vocabulary like "page load", "browser extensions", and "network latency" - exactly the words that appear in the docs that actually help. Without HyDE, retrieval returned doc-7 (Dashboard Tour) - a generic doc that doesn't help. The question "why slow" embedded poorly; the answer embedded well.
summary: Concrete worked example shows HyDE rewriting 'why is my dashboard slow' into a vocabulary-rich answer that retrieves better docs.
queries: How does HyDE retrieve better docs?
What docs does HyDE return for dashboard slow?

Vocabulary pulled in by hypothetical answer
Why HyDE pulls page load vocabulary

text: Why HyDE Works. Three reasons the hypothetical-answer trick beats embedding the raw question. Even if the LLM's draft is factually wrong, the SHAPE of the answer matches the SHAPE of real docs - and that is what cosine similarity scores. (1) Different Shapes In Vector Space: questions look different from answers in vector space. (2) Borrows Doc Vocabulary: hypothetical answers borrow doc vocabulary even if the LLM doesn't know the actual answer. (3) Robust To Lexical Mismatch: robust to lexical mismatch because the LLM does the rewriting for you.
summary: Three reasons HyDE works: questions and answers have different shapes, answers borrow doc vocabulary, and rewriting handles lexical mismatch.
queries: Does HyDE work if LLM is wrong?
Why answer shape matters in retrieval
What does cosine similarity actually score?
How does HyDE handle lexical mismatch?
Does the LLM need to know the answer for HyDE?
Why hypothetical answers borrow doc vocabulary
Three reasons HyDE beats raw question embedding
Why questions are shaped differently than answers

text: When HyDE Helps Vs When It Hurts. HyDE is not free and is not universal. It earns its keep when the user's words are far from the doc's words. It hurts when the query is already short, factual, and embeds well. Helps: long descriptive queries; lexical mismatch (sign-in vs log-in); conceptual / 'why does X happen' questions; queries that don't share vocabulary with docs. Hurts: short factual queries with good embeddings (e.g., 'API key'); queries where the LLM might hallucinate the wrong answer shape; latency-sensitive pipelines (HyDE adds an LLM call: +200-400 ms). If recall on your golden set is already above 0.85, HyDE likely won't help and will add latency.
summary: Lists query types where HyDE helps (long, conceptual, lexical mismatch) vs hurts (short factual, latency-sensitive, hallucination risk).
queries: Should I use HyDE if recall is 0.85?

text: The HyDE Prompt Template. The exact prompt you fire at the LLM. The placeholder gets filled with the user's question. The LLM's response is the hypothetical answer that gets embedded. Prompt Template: "You are a helpful assistant. Write a single concise paragraph that answers the user's question as if you knew the answer. The paragraph will be used to search a documentation index, so include domain terms and likely doc vocabulary. Do not say 'I don't know' - guess plausibly. Question: {query}  Hypothetical Answer:" +1 LLM call per user query (~$0.0005 + ~250ms). Cache the hypothetical answer by query hash to amortize. Caching - covered in chapter 23.6.
summary: Provides the exact HyDE prompt template plus cost (~$0.0005, ~250ms) and a caching tip.

text: Multi-query expansion (also called RAG-Fusion) rewrites one user query into 3-5 LLM-generated variants, runs vector retrieval for each variant in parallel, then fuses the N rankings into one final list using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) with k=60. It helps on ambiguous, multi-intent, or complex queries where one wording would miss key docs, but wastes latency on short factual lookups. Adds about 300 ms (one LLM call) plus N cheap parallel retrievals. Step-back prompting is a specific variant style that asks a broader version of the question to catch docs that don't match the narrow wording.
summary: Chapter overview of multi-query expansion: generate variants, retrieve each, fuse with RRF.

text: One Query Becomes Many; Fuse The Results. If the user's exact wording missed the right doc, one of the variant wordings might catch it. Ask an LLM to rewrite the query into 3-5 variants, retrieve top-K for each, then fuse the rankings into one. RAG-Fusion is the same idea under a different name; this chapter absorbs it. The fan-out diagram shows User Query expanding into Variant 1, Variant 2, Variant 3, each producing its own Top-K Docs set, all converging into a single Fused Ranking via RRF.
summary: Core insight: one query fans out to many variants and the rankings are fused into one.

text: Three Steps: Generate Variants -> Retrieve Each -> Fuse. The whole pipeline fits in three stages: (1) LLM Generates 3-5 Query Variants, (2) Run Vector Retrieval For Each Variant, (3) Fuse Rankings With RRF. The LLM rewrites; the vector index runs N independent retrievals; RRF combines the N rankings into one final list. Stage 2 runs in parallel - N retrievals fire at once, not in sequence. Latency is dominated by stage 1 (the LLM call), not stage 2.
summary: Three-stage pipeline: LLM rewrite, parallel vector retrieval per variant, RRF fusion.

text: Worked Example: 'Cancel My Subscription And Get A Refund'. One query, two intents - a single vector lookup is forced to compromise. The LLM splits the wording into three variants: Variant 1: 'How to cancel my subscription'; Variant 2: 'How to get a refund after cancellation'; Variant 3: 'Stop billing immediately'. Top-3 retrieved docs per variant: Variant 1 -> doc-15, doc-7, doc-3. Variant 2 -> doc-4, doc-15, doc-3. Variant 3 -> doc-5, doc-15, doc-21. After RRF fusion, the final top-3 is: Rank 1 doc-15 (Covers Cancellation), Rank 2 doc-4 (Covers Refund), Rank 3 doc-3 (Subscription Tiers). Each variant brings a different doc to the top; fusion captures both intents.
summary: Worked example: cancel-and-refund query splits into 3 variants and RRF fuses their top-3 rankings.
queries: how does multi-query handle two intents in one query
what does the final fused ranking look like
why single retrieval fails on compound queries

text: Fuse With RRF (Same Formula As Chapter 21.3). Reciprocal Rank Fusion takes N rankings and produces one. Each doc's score is the sum of 1 / (k + its rank) across every ranking it appears in. Formula: RRF(d) = sum over variants v of: 1 / (k + rank_v(d)), where k = 60 (standard). The constant k = 60 dampens the influence of any single list so agreement across variants matters more than a top-1 appearance. Same fusion math as hybrid search; different inputs - here the rankings come from N variants of the same query rather than from BM25 + dense.
summary: RRF formula sums 1/(k+rank) across rankings with k=60 to fuse variant retrievals.
queries: how does RRF combine multiple rankings
what does k do in reciprocal rank fusion
RRF for multi-query expansion same as hybrid
why top-1 is dampened in RRF

text: Step-Back Prompting: Add A More General Variant. Step-back is a specific variant style: ask a broader, less specific version of the question. A doc might not match the user's exact narrow wording, but it almost certainly matches a more general phrasing of the same problem. Original (Very Specific): 'Why is the user role page failing on Chrome v124 with error E_PERM?'. Step-Back (More General): 'Why does the user role page fail?'. The original is too specific - the doc might not mention Chrome v124. The step-back catches more general docs. Reasoning models reduce the need for explicit step-back; include it when queries are unusually specific. One of many possible variant prompts; step-back is a niche addition.
summary: Step-back prompting adds a broader phrasing of the query as a variant to catch more general docs.
queries: broader version of user query for RAG
step-back prompting example specific to general
what kind of variant is step-back
is step-back necessary with new LLMs

text: When Multi-Query Helps. Multi-query expansion is not a default - it earns its keep on hard, ambiguous, or multi-intent queries; on short factual lookups it burns budget. Helps: ambiguous queries with multiple plausible intents; complex multi-clause queries; recall is the priority over latency; reranker can absorb the wider candidate set. Skip: short factual lookups ('What is my account ID?'); latency-critical paths (adds 1 LLM call + N parallel retrievals); when HyDE already solves the same failure. Adds about 300 ms (1 LLM call) plus N parallel retrievals (cheap). Often pairs well with HyDE for hard cases.
summary: Guidance on when multi-query expansion helps vs when to skip it, with latency cost and HyDE pairing.
queries: when should I use multi-query expansion
is multi-query worth it for factual lookups
latency cost of multi-query in ms
should I always use multi-query expansion

text: Diagram: Router decision tree with four leaves. The root node is labeled 'Classify Query Type' (cyan). Four branch lines drop to four leaf boxes laid out left-to-right: Account & Billing -> Account-Billing Index, Top-K = 5 (cyan); Product Features -> Product-Features Index, Top-K = 5 (purple); Troubleshooting -> Troubleshooting Index, Top-K = 8 (yellow); Chitchat / Off-Topic -> Skip Retrieval, Reply Directly (red). The tree visually conveys that classification routes each query to one of three indexes or skips retrieval entirely.
summary: Diagram of a four-leaf router decision tree mapping query types to indexes (or skip-retrieval) with Top-K values.
queries: what does the routing diagram show

text: Query Routing and Decomposition are two query-transformation strategies in RAG. Routing decides WHERE a query goes (which index or tool, or skip retrieval entirely for chitchat). Decomposition decides HOW MANY sub-queries are needed (split complex queries into 2-3 parts, retrieve each, then assemble). Routing implementations: semantic router (embedding-based, ~$0.0001/query, ~10ms, 85-90% accuracy) vs LLM classifier (~$0.0002/query, ~150ms, 92-97%). Decomposition fits comparison, multi-hop, and disjunction query shapes. The 2x2 decision: Route only (default for production), Decompose only (multi-hop heavy), Both (compose), Neither (simple lookups). Combined overhead ~300-450 ms / $0.0006-0.0007 per query.
summary: Chapter overview of query routing (where) and decomposition (how many) as composable RAG query-transformation strategies.
queries: what is query routing in RAG
when to split a query into sub-queries
how to route queries to the right index
should I route or decompose RAG queries
cost of query routing and decomposition

text: Routing decides WHERE; Decomposition decides HOW MANY. Two related query-transformation strategies share this chapter. Routing answers a where question - which index or tool should the query hit. Pick one target. Skip retrieval entirely for off-topic chitchat. Decomposition answers a how-many question - does the query need to be split into smaller sub-queries first. Split complex queries into 2-3 sub-queries; retrieve and assemble. Both rely on an LLM (or a cheaper classifier) to inspect the query and decide. They compose - a router can send a query to a decomposition step, or a decomposed sub-query can itself be routed to a different index.
summary: Introduces routing as a where-question and decomposition as a how-many-question, both LLM-driven and composable.
queries: what does routing decide in RAG
where vs how many query strategies
pick one target index for a query
how many sub-queries to split into
two strategies for query handling RAG

text: Routing sends each query to the right target. On the running support corpus, queries split cleanly into four buckets. A router classifies each query into one of them, then sends it to the matching index - or skips retrieval entirely for chitchat. The four leaves: Account & Billing -> Account-Billing Index, Top-K = 5. Product Features -> Product-Features Index, Top-K = 5. Troubleshooting -> Troubleshooting Index, Top-K = 8. Chitchat / Off-Topic -> Skip Retrieval, Reply Directly. Routing avoids retrieving from indexes that can't help. Skipping retrieval on chitchat saves latency and cost - and prevents the LLM from hallucinating about a doc it doesn't need to read.
summary: Shows a four-leaf router decision tree mapping each query type to a target index and Top-K, including a skip-retrieval branch for chitchat.
queries: four query buckets in RAG router
why route queries to different indexes

text: Two router implementations: Embedding-Based vs LLM Classifier. Semantic Router (Embedding-Based): embed the query, compare to embeddings of route prototypes ('Billing question', 'Troubleshooting question'). Pick highest cosine. Cost ~$0.0001 / query, Latency ~10 ms, Accuracy 85-90%. LLM Classifier: pass the query + route labels to a small LLM ('Classify this query: billing / feature / troubleshoot / chitchat'). Pick the label. Cost ~$0.0002 / query, Latency ~150 ms, Accuracy 92-97%. Embedding-based is ~15x faster and slightly cheaper; LLM classifier handles ambiguous wording better. Start with semantic; switch to LLM if routing accuracy stalls below your bar.
summary: Compares semantic (embedding) router vs LLM classifier across cost, latency, and accuracy with concrete numbers.
queries: how does a semantic router work
when to use LLM classifier for routing
which router implementation to start with

text: Decomposition splits 'Compare X and Y' into two sub-queries. User query: 'Compare the Pro and Enterprise plans.' LLM splits into sub-queries. Sub-Query 1: 'What's in the Pro plan?' retrieves doc-3 (Subscription Tiers) and doc-10 (Team Seat Management). Sub-Query 2: 'What's in the Enterprise plan?' retrieves doc-3 (Subscription Tiers), doc-10 (Team Seat Management), and doc-21 (Quota Exceeded - Enterprise-Only Feature). LLM assembly step: union of retrieved docs (doc-3, doc-10, doc-21) is passed to a single LLM call that writes the side-by-side comparison. Without decomposition, a single vector lookup gets pulled toward whichever subject has stronger phrasing and misses the other. Decomposition guarantees both sides of the comparison are retrieved before the LLM has to answer.
summary: Worked example: comparing Pro vs Enterprise plans is split into two sub-queries, retrieved separately, then unioned for one LLM assembly call.
queries: compare Pro and Enterprise plans RAG example
how to decompose a comparison query
union of retrieved docs for comparison
why single vector lookup misses comparisons
Pro plan vs Enterprise plan retrieval

text: When to decompose: Multi-Hop, Comparison, Disjunction. Three query shapes consistently benefit from being split before retrieval. Comparison example: 'Compare the Pro and Enterprise plans.' Split: Sub-1: What's in the Pro plan? + Sub-2: What's in the Enterprise plan? Multi-Hop example: 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' Split: Sub-1: How to recover email? + Sub-2: How to reset password? Disjunction (Mixed Types) example: 'Why is the dashboard slow and showing 500 errors?' Split: Sub-1: Dashboard slow troubleshooting + Sub-2: 500 error troubleshooting. If the query has 'and' / 'compare' / 'or' between two distinct subjects, decomposition usually helps. Single-subject queries don't benefit and just pay the extra LLM call for nothing.
summary: Lists the three query shapes (comparison, multi-hop, disjunction) that benefit from decomposition with concrete examples and a heuristic.

text: The decision: Route, Decompose, Both, or Neither. Four combinations cover every production case. Route Only: most production RAG systems start here. Cheap, easy, high-impact. Decompose Only: use when queries are consistently complex (multi-hop, comparisons). Adds 1 LLM call + N parallel retrievals. Both: route first to pick the right index, then decompose if the query is complex. They compose - they don't interfere. Neither: default for simple lookup queries. Don't add complexity you don't need. Cost summary: Routing adds +10-150 ms / +$0.0001-0.0002 per query. Decomposition adds +200-300 ms / +$0.0005 per query. Combined: ~+300-450 ms total query-transformation overhead.
summary: 2x2 decision grid for route/decompose/both/neither with cost-and-latency overhead numbers for each combination.
queries: should I use both routing and decomposition
when not to use query transformation
when to start with route only

Segment widths are proportional to token counts on a centered bar inside an 800x130 viewBox. Legend and numeric breakdown table show each slot's token count and percent share of the 8000-token total.

text: Context packing turns retrieved chunks into a final prompt that fits a token budget. The 8000-token window splits into system prompt, retrieved context, user question, reserved completion, and headroom. Chunks can be ordered relevance-first (best at position 1, default for single answers), chronologically (for time-sensitive docs), or deduplicated with MMR (lambda x sim(chunk, query) - (1 - lambda) x max(sim(chunk, already_picked))) to drop near-duplicates. The packed prompt combines a system instruction, citation-marked chunks, the question, and an allowed refusal phrase, fitting within budget.
summary: Chapter 22.1 covers token budgeting, chunk ordering strategies (relevance-first, chronological, MMR deduplication), and assembling the final packed prompt.

text: Every prompt has a token budget. An 8000-token context window splits across four jobs: the system prompt that frames the model (200 tokens, 2.5%), the retrieved context with the answer (5000 tokens, 62.5%), the user question (50 tokens, 0.6%), and the reserved completion tokens the model uses to write its answer (1500 tokens, 18.8%). Headroom (1250 tokens, 15.6%) is the slack you keep so a long answer never overflows. Slots: System Prompt 200, Retrieved Context 5000, User Question 50, Reserved Completion 1500, Headroom 1250. Total = 8000.
summary: An 8000-token context window splits across system prompt, retrieved context, user question, reserved completion, and headroom.

text: Pack Top-3 chunks for the password reset query. The running query 'How do I reset my password?' returned three chunks from doc-1 with cosine scores. The scores tell us how to order; the chunks tell us what to pack. [doc-1, chunk 1] Score 0.91: 'To reset your password, go to Settings then Security.' [doc-1, chunk 2] Score 0.84: 'An email with a reset link will be sent to your registered address.' [doc-1, chunk 3] Score 0.77: 'Click the link within 24 hours to set a new password.'

text: Ordering Option 1: Relevance-First. Sort by retrieval score, highest first. The most-relevant chunk lands at position 1 where the model attends best. Default for single-best-answer queries. Packed order: 1. [doc-1, chunk 1] (Score 0.91), 2. [doc-1, chunk 2] (Score 0.84), 3. [doc-1, chunk 3] (Score 0.77). Most relevant first. Cheap to do - just sort by score. Risk: tail-position chunks lose attention weight.
summary: Relevance-first ordering sorts packed chunks by descending retrieval score so the best chunk lands where the model attends most.
queries: how to order RAG chunks by score
should I sort retrieved chunks by score
where does the model attend best
how to pack chunks in prompt

text: Ordering Option 2: Chronological. Sort by publication date or document position. Useful for time-sensitive docs - changelogs, policy histories, incident timelines - where the natural reading order helps the model follow cause-effect. Packed order: 1. [doc-1, chunk 1] - Document Position 1, 2. [doc-1, chunk 2] - Document Position 2, 3. [doc-1, chunk 3] - Document Position 3. Time-sensitive ordering preserves the natural sequence so the model reads cause then effect.
summary: Chronological ordering packs chunks by date or document position so the model follows the natural cause-effect sequence.
queries: when to order RAG chunks by date
when not to sort by relevance

text: Ordering Option 3: Deduplicate with MMR. Maximal Marginal Relevance keeps high-score chunks while dropping near-duplicates. Same budget, more unique information. Before MMR (5 chunks, redundant): chunk 1, chunk 2, chunk 3, chunk 4 (duplicate - wastes budget, struck through), chunk 5. After MMR (4 chunks, diverse): [doc-1, chunk 1], [doc-1, chunk 2], [doc-1, chunk 3], [doc-9, chunk 1] 'MFA must be re-verified after a reset (different but related).' The MMR formula: MMR = lambda x sim(chunk, query) - (1 - lambda) x max(sim(chunk, already_picked)). Lambda = 0.7 weights relevance over diversity. Lambda = 0.3 weights diversity over relevance. Tune per workload.
summary: MMR drops near-duplicate chunks using a lambda-weighted formula that balances query relevance against similarity to already-picked chunks.
queries: how to balance diversity and relevance
sim chunk query minus sim chunk picked
how to pick diverse chunks for RAG

text:
The final packed prompt: system instruction, retrieved chunks with citation markers, user question, and an allowed refusal phrase. Prompt template:

You are a helpful support assistant. Use the documentation below to answer.
If the docs don't contain the answer, say "I don't have enough information".

Documentation:
[doc-1, chunk 1] To reset your password, go to Settings then Security.
[doc-1, chunk 2] An email with a reset link will be sent to your address.
[doc-1, chunk 3] Click the link within 24 hours to set a new password.

Question: How do I reset my password?
Answer:

Within budget: ~4,800 / 8,000 tokens (60%).

text: Chapter 22.2 covers the lost-in-the-middle problem from Liu et al. 2023: LLMs attend best to the start and end of a long context window and ignore the middle, producing a U-shaped accuracy curve over chunk position. Two cheap fixes are front-loading (sort chunks by retrieval score descending so the best lands at position 1) and sandwiching (top-2 at the front, next-best 2 at the back, leaving the dip for low-scored chunks). Three failure modes where reordering alone is not enough: truly long multi-fact queries needing hierarchical or multi-hop retrieval, tail-buried critical detail needing harder reranking with fewer chunks, and model-specific curve shapes that require benchmarking on your own model.
summary: Lost-in-the-middle problem, two reorder fixes (front-load, sandwich), and three failure modes.

text: Strategy 1: Front-load the most relevant chunk. Relevance-first ordering: sort by retrieval score descending. The top-scored chunks land in positions 1, 2, 3 - the high-attention region. Tail positions hold the lowest-scored chunks. Concrete scores across the 10 slots: pos 1 = 0.91 (Top-1), pos 2 = 0.88 (Top-2), pos 3 = 0.84 (Top-3), then descending 0.78, 0.71, 0.66, 0.59, 0.52, 0.45, 0.38 at pos 10. Cheap. Helps single-best-answer queries. Risk: tail context still ignored, but top-scored chunks land in the attention sweet spot.
summary: Front-load strategy sorts chunks by score descending so top hits sit at positions 1-3.
queries: What is front-loading chunks in RAG

text: Strategy 2: Sandwich the best chunks at start and end. Put the top-2 chunks at the front and the next-best two at the back. The middle dip on the U-curve becomes the least-relevant region. Same total tokens; reordering is negligible compute. Concrete arrangement across 10 slots: pos 1 = 0.91 (Top-1), pos 2 = 0.88 (Top-2), pos 3 = 0.66, pos 4 = 0.59, pos 5 = 0.52, pos 6 = 0.45, pos 7 = 0.38, pos 8 = 0.31, pos 9 = 0.84 (Top-3), pos 10 = 0.78 (Top-4). More robust for multi-fact queries. Same total tokens. Costs reordering compute (negligible).
summary: Sandwich strategy bookends top chunks at start and end so middle dip holds least-relevant content.
queries: What is sandwich strategy in RAG

text: When reordering isn't enough. Front-load and sandwich help most workloads but not all. Three failure modes need different fixes than position tricks. (1) Truly long multi-fact queries: 30 chunks all needed; even sandwich leaves middle facts ignored. Fix: hierarchical summarization or multi-hop retrieval (chapter 22.4). (2) Tail-buried critical detail: the killer fact lives at chunk 18; both strategies miss it. Fix: rerank harder, fetch fewer. (3) Model-specific curve shape: some models have flatter curves than others; benchmark on your own model. Lost-in-middle is a model fingerprint, not a universal law. Always benchmark on your own model and workload.
summary: Three failure modes where reordering fails: multi-fact queries, tail-buried facts, and model-specific curves.
queries: When reordering chunks does not help
Should I benchmark lost in middle on my model

text: Chapter 22.3 covers citations, refusal, and groundedness for production RAG. Without citations, users cannot verify answers and reviewers cannot audit them. Inline [doc-N] markers make every claim traceable. Citations can be requested inline in the system prompt or as structured JSON with doc_id, chunk_id, quote, and confidence fields. Refusal instructions give the model an authorized exit when documentation lacks the answer, with exact wording like "I don't have enough information to answer that." Faithfulness is the share of claims supported by retrieved chunks, measured via a 3-step audit: parse atomic claims, locate cited chunk, score with an LLM judge. A regex like \[doc-\d+\] parses markers back out for footnote rendering. The production template combines numbered RULES for citations and refusal because numbered constraints survive long context better than prose.
summary: Overview of citations, refusal instructions, faithfulness scoring, and the production prompt template combining both.
queries: how do I add citations to a RAG answer

text: Why citations are a production requirement. Without citations, the user cannot verify the answer and a reviewer cannot audit it. Hallucinations slip through silently. With inline [doc-N] markers, every claim is traceable. The same answer with citations becomes an artifact you can review, replay, and trust. Compare: No Citations - Query: "Why is my account locked?" Answer: "Your account may be locked due to too many failed logins or a billing issue." User cannot verify. Reviewer cannot audit. Hallucination has nowhere to land. With Citations - same query, answer: "Your account locks after 5 failed logins [doc-12]. Billing issues trigger a 24-hour hold [doc-7]." Every claim traces to a specific chunk. Audit-friendly. Hallucinations exposed.
summary: Citations turn opaque LLM answers into auditable artifacts where every claim traces back to a retrieved chunk.
queries: why are citations needed in production RAG
what happens without citations in RAG answers
how do citations help audit LLM answers
example RAG answer with and without citations

text: Tell the model to cite inline. The citation instruction lives in the system prompt. Use a literal [doc-N] marker the model can emit inline. Specify that N must come from the Documentation block, not be invented. Prompt template: "You are a helpful support assistant. Use the documentation below to answer the user's question. For every claim you make, cite the source as [doc-N], where N is the doc number from the Documentation section. Do NOT cite a doc you did not use. Documentation: {context} Question: {query} Answer:"
summary: System prompt template that instructs the model to emit inline [doc-N] citations tied to numbered docs.
queries: how to prompt LLM to cite sources
how to format Documentation block in prompt
what should the system prompt say about citations
Do NOT cite a doc you did not use

text: Or ask for structured citations. JSON output is machine-parseable and downstream audit pipelines can ingest it without regex. The schema pairs the answer with its citations and a confidence score. Example: { "answer": "Your account locks after 5 failed logins.", "citations": [ { "doc_id": "doc-12", "chunk_id": 3, "quote": "5 failed login attempts" } ], "confidence": 0.92 }. Structured output makes citations machine-parseable. Better for downstream audit pipelines.
summary: Structured JSON citation schema with doc_id, chunk_id, quote, and confidence for machine parsing.
queries: how to ask LLM for JSON citations
structured citation example with confidence score
what fields to include in citation JSON

text: Tell the model to refuse when context is missing. Without an explicit refusal instruction, models tend to invent. With one, the model has an authorized exit for "I don't know". The exact wording matters - models follow the most precise instructions in the prompt. Prompt template: "If the documentation does not contain enough information to answer, respond with EXACTLY this sentence and nothing else: 'I don't have enough information to answer that.' Do not invent facts. Do not guess. Do not cite a doc you didn't use." Comparison: Without Refusal Instruction - Query: "What's the refund window for monthly plans?" (no matching doc). Answer: "Monthly plans have a 7-day refund window." Hallucinated. The model guessed because it had no exit. With Refusal Instruction - same query, same missing doc. Answer: "I don't have enough information to answer that." Safe. The user knows the system was honest about its limit.
summary: Explicit refusal instructions give the model an authorized exit so it admits uncertainty instead of hallucinating.
queries: how to make LLM refuse to answer

text: Faithfulness: every claim must trace to a chunk. Faithfulness is the share of claims in an answer that are supported by the retrieved chunks. A 3-step audit converts citations into a measurable score. Step 1: Parse Answer Into Atomic Claims. Example: "5 failed logins triggers a lock" / "Billing issues hold for 24 hours". Step 2: Locate The Cited Chunk For Each Claim. Follow the [doc-N] marker back to the retrieved chunk text. Step 3: Score: claim_supported = LLMJudge(claim, chunk). Aggregate per-claim scores into a faithfulness number from 0 to 1. Output is a single number from 0 (none supported) to 1 (every claim has evidence in the retrieved chunks). Chapters 23.1-23.5 cover RAGAS faithfulness in depth.
summary: Faithfulness is a 0-to-1 score measuring the share of answer claims supported by retrieved chunks via a 3-step audit.
queries: how does LLM judge claim support
locate cited chunk for each claim
how to score RAG answer quality

text: Parse citations back out of the answer. After generation, parse [doc-N] markers out of the answer with a regex. Map each marker back to the retrieved chunk and render as footnotes or hover tooltips in the UI. Example model output: "Your account locks after 5 failed logins [doc-12]. Billing issues trigger a 24-hour hold [doc-7]." Regex: \[doc-\d+\]. Resolved citation table: Marker [doc-12] -> Doc doc-12 (Account Lockouts), Chunk 3, Quoted Span "5 failed login attempts". Marker [doc-7] -> Doc doc-7 (Billing Holds), Chunk 1, Quoted Span "24-hour hold after a failed charge". Render as footnotes or inline tooltips in your UI. Now the user can click a citation and see exactly what the model used to write that sentence.
summary: Use regex \[doc-\d+\] to extract citation markers from the answer and resolve each to its doc, chunk, and quote for UI footnotes.
queries: how to parse doc citations from LLM output
how to extract citations with regex

text: Production template: citations plus refusal. One combined template that does both. Numbered RULES make the contract explicit so the model treats citations and refusal as non-optional. Template: "You are a helpful support assistant. Use ONLY the documentation below to answer the user's question. RULES: 1. Cite every claim inline as [doc-N], where N is the doc number. 2. Quote 3-7 words from the cited chunk after each [doc-N]. 3. If the docs do not answer the question, respond with EXACTLY: 'I don't have enough information to answer that.' 4. Do not invent facts. Do not paraphrase what isn't in the docs. Documentation: {context} Question: {query} Answer:" Numbered rules survive long context better than prose instructions. The model attends to short structured constraints more reliably than to a paragraph.
summary: Production prompt combining citations and refusal using numbered RULES that survive long context better than prose.
queries: why numbered rules beat prose instructions
best system prompt for RAG production
Do not paraphrase what isn't in the docs
how to write production RAG system prompt

text: SVG diagram: Multi-hop retrieval control-loop flowchart showing User Query box at top flowing into Retrieve Top-K For Current Query box, then into a yellow diamond labeled 'Does Context Answer The Question?'. YES (green arrow) exits right to Generate Answer box. NO with Hops < max_hops (orange arrow) exits left to Reformulate Query box, then loops back up to Retrieve. NO with Hops >= max_hops (red arrow) exits down to Refuse box labeled 'I Don't Have Enough Information'. Colors: purple for query, cyan for retrieve, yellow for decision diamond, green for generate, orange for reformulate, red for refuse.
summary: Visual SVG flowchart of the multi-hop loop: retrieve, sufficiency diamond, reformulate loop-back, refuse on cap.
queries: user query to generate answer flow

text: Chapter 22.4 covers multi-hop retrieval in RAG: queries that need a chain of facts across multiple documents. Single-hop retrieval fails when one chunk only contains half the answer. The multi-hop control loop retrieves, checks sufficiency, then either generates the answer or reformulates the query and loops again, capped by max_hops (typically 3-5). A sufficiency-check prompt asks the model to respond SUFFICIENT or INSUFFICIENT with a follow-up query. Multi-hop is worth it for compound questions, cross-doc reasoning, and step-dependent flows, but each hop multiplies latency and cost (~3x for 3 hops). Failure modes: infinite loops, divergence from original intent, and overconfident sufficiency judges. Mitigations: hard cap max_hops, anchor original query, separate calibrated judge model.
summary: Overview of multi-hop retrieval: why, how the control loop works, sufficiency prompts, costs, and failure modes.
queries: what is multi-hop retrieval in RAG
when do I need more than one retrieval
how does a multi-hop RAG loop work
should I use multi-hop or single-hop
RAG when one retrieval isn't enough
how many hops should RAG do

text: Some queries need more than one retrieval. Single-hop retrieval works when the answer fits in one chunk. Multi-hop retrieval kicks in when the question depends on a chain of facts that live in different docs. The signal you need it: even a perfect retrieval returns only half of what is needed. Single-Hop Works example: Query 'How do I reset my password?' One retrieval finds doc-1 (password reset). Answer: 'Go to Settings then Security, click Reset Password.' Multi-Hop Needed example: Query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' One retrieval finds doc-1 (password reset) but the question also needs doc-3 (email change / recovery). One retrieval misses half. Multiple retrievals needed.
summary: Single-hop suffices for self-contained queries; multi-hop is required when answers depend on facts spread across multiple documents.
queries: why is one retrieval not always enough
RAG retrieval misses half the answer
how do I reset password if I forgot email RAG
RAG fetches only half the information
signal that a question needs chained retrievals

text: Two hops on the running query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?'. Hop 1: Query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?', Retrieved: doc-1 (password reset). Reasoning: Reset requires email access. The user says they forgot email - need email recovery info before answering. Decision: INSUFFICIENT -> Reformulate as: 'How do I recover access if I forgot my email?'. Hop 2: Query 'How do I recover access if I forgot my email?', Retrieved: doc-3 (email change / recovery). Reasoning: Now both pieces are in hand: the reset flow (doc-1) and the email-recovery path (doc-3). Decision: SUFFICIENT. Final answer combines doc-1 + doc-3 with citations. Each hop reformulates the query to fetch the missing piece.
summary: Walkthrough of a 2-hop trace showing how Hop 1 finds doc-1, reformulates, and Hop 2 fetches doc-3 to complete the answer.
queries: doc-1 password reset doc-3 email recovery
how LLM combines docs across hops
how does the model decide INSUFFICIENT

text: The multi-hop control loop. Loop structure: retrieve, check sufficiency, generate or reformulate. A hard cap on max_hops prevents infinite loops. If the cap is hit without sufficiency, the system refuses. Flow: User Query -> Retrieve Top-K For Current Query -> diamond 'Does Context Answer The Question?'. YES branch -> Generate Answer. NO branch with Hops < max_hops -> Reformulate Query -> loop back to Retrieve Top-K. NO branch with Hops >= max_hops -> Refuse: 'I Don't Have Enough Information'. max_hops typically 3-5. Each hop adds 1 retrieval + 1 LLM call latency. Tune based on workload and budget.
summary: Control-loop diagram for multi-hop retrieval with sufficiency check, reformulation loop-back, and refusal on max_hops.
queries: refuse when not enough information RAG
what happens if multi-hop hits max hops

text:
Asking the model: do you have enough? The sufficiency check is a small structured task. The model decides whether the retrieved text answers the question. SUFFICIENT means 'I can answer now'; INSUFFICIENT means 'fetch this follow-up'. Prompt Template - Sufficiency Check:

You are an assistant deciding whether you have enough information.

Question: {query}
Retrieved so far:
{context}

Respond with EXACTLY one of:
- SUFFICIENT (you can answer the question from the retrieved text)
- INSUFFICIENT: <one short follow-up query to retrieve more>

Do not answer the question yet.

Example Trace: Hop 1: 'INSUFFICIENT: How do I recover access if I forgot my email?'. Hop 2: 'SUFFICIENT'.
summary: Exact prompt template that asks the LLM to output SUFFICIENT or INSUFFICIENT plus a follow-up query, with an example trace.
queries: how to ask LLM if it has enough info
prompt to decide whether to keep retrieving
how does the model know to stop retrieving

text: When to reach for multi-hop. Multi-hop is not free. Each hop multiplies latency and cost. Use it only when the query genuinely needs chained facts, and always with a hard cap on hops. Worth It: compound questions (password reset if you forgot your email), cross-doc reasoning (compare Pro vs Enterprise plans), step-dependent flows (cancel subscription then refund). Not Worth It: single-doc lookups, simple FAQ-style queries, cost outweighs benefit when one hop already answers. Cost Multiplier: each hop = 1 retrieval + 1 LLM call; 3 hops = ~3x latency, ~3x cost vs single-hop; budget per query becomes a hard constraint. Mitigations: cap max_hops at 3, cache reformulated queries, use a smaller model for the sufficiency check.
summary: When multi-hop is worth it versus not, the cost multiplier per hop, and mitigations like caps, caching, and smaller judge models.
queries: FAQ queries multi-hop not worth it
cost of each hop retrieval plus LLM

text: Failure modes: infinite loops and divergence. Three known failure modes show up in production multi-hop systems. Each has a known mitigation that costs either an extra check or stricter caps. Infinite Loop: model never says SUFFICIENT. Each hop reformulates without making progress. Mitigation: hard cap on max_hops + log every reformulation. Divergence: each reformulation drifts farther from the original intent. 'How do I reset my password?' becomes 'How do I configure SSO?' by hop 3. Mitigation: keep the ORIGINAL query in the reformulation prompt as an anchor. Stuck Sufficiency Judge: model always says SUFFICIENT after hop 1 even when context is incomplete (overconfident). Mitigation: separate judge model, calibrate via a golden dataset.
summary: Three multi-hop failure modes (infinite loop, divergence, stuck overconfident judge) and their mitigations.
queries: how to prevent infinite multi-hop loops

text: Self-RAG token-emission timeline diagram: horizontal line with six markers showing the model alternating between special control tokens and answer text across the generation sequence. Markers in order: <retrieve> (model gates retrieval), Reformulate (tokens 2-3 produce search query), doc-1 In (retriever returns top doc), <isrel> (model judges doc-1 relevant), Answer (tokens 6-20 generate answer text), <issup> (model judges answer supported). Generation order left to right.
summary: Diagram showing the left-to-right order of Self-RAG control tokens interleaved with answer generation.
queries: what does Self-RAG timeline look like

text: Retrieve / no-retrieve decision diamond diagram: User Query box at top flows into yellow diamond labeled "Need external knowledge?". YES branch on right exits to green box "<retrieve> -> <isrel> -> <issup>". NO branch on left exits to red box "<no-retrieve> -> Answer". Below: "Trained via instruction tuning + RL. Reduces unnecessary retrievals ~40% (Asai et al. 2023)".
summary: Diagram of the Self-RAG gate diamond routing queries to either retrieve-plus-critique or direct-answer.

text: Self-RAG (Asai et al. 2023) trains the LLM to decide per-query whether to retrieve, then self-critique what comes back. Model emits four special control tokens inline: <retrieve>, <no-retrieve>, <isrel> (judges each retrieved doc's relevance), and <issup> (judges whether final answer is supported by kept docs). Runtime intercepts tokens and acts. Cuts unnecessary retrievals ~40%. Wins on mixed-knowledge corpora, latency-sensitive workloads, adaptive depth. Limits: needs a fine-tuned model, larger vocab, classifier calibration is the bottleneck.
summary: Self-RAG teaches the LLM to gate retrieval and self-critique results using four special control tokens.
queries: special tokens RAG retrieve isrel issup

text: Should the model retrieve, or just answer? Always-retrieve wastes tokens and latency. Never-retrieve risks hallucination. Self-RAG decides per-query. Example queries: "What is 2 + 2?" -> <no-retrieve> (factoid already known from pre-training). "How do I reset my password?" -> <retrieve> (custom-corpus knowledge model cannot answer without retrieval). "Summarize what we just discussed." -> <no-retrieve> (context already in conversation; no external lookup needed).
summary: Self-RAG gates retrieval per-query so the model only fetches when external knowledge is genuinely needed.

text: Self-RAG extends model vocabulary with four control tokens emitted inline during generation; runtime intercepts each. <retrieve>: retrieve a document now. <no-retrieve>: do not retrieve, answer from model's own knowledge. <isrel>: is this retrieved doc relevant? Emitted after each retrieved doc. <issup>: is the answer supported by the retrieved doc? Emitted with the answer.
summary: Four special tokens (<retrieve>, <no-retrieve>, <isrel>, <issup>) extend the model vocabulary to control retrieval and grade results.

text: Token emission timeline for query "How do I reset my password?": <retrieve> (model gates retrieval) -> Reformulate (tokens 2-3 produce a search query) -> doc-1 In (retriever returns top doc) -> <isrel> (model judges doc-1 relevant) -> Answer (tokens 6-20 generate answer text) -> <issup> (model judges answer supported). Each marker is a decision point the runtime watches. Generation order left to right.
summary: Walk through a real query showing the sequence of control tokens and answer text Self-RAG emits.

text: Retrieve / no-retrieve gate sits at top of every generation. User query enters decision diamond "Need external knowledge?" YES branch -> <retrieve> -> <isrel> -> <issup>. NO branch -> <no-retrieve> -> Answer. Trained via instruction tuning + RL on retrieval decisions. Reduces unnecessary retrievals ~40% in original Asai et al. 2023 paper.
summary: The gate diamond routes queries to retrieval-plus-critique or direct-answer paths, trained with instruction tuning plus RL.

text: Self-critique re-scores every retrieved doc. After retrieval, model emits <isrel> per doc to grade relevance. Per-doc critique example: doc-1 (Password Reset) RELEVANT -> Keep; doc-9 (Login Troubleshooting) PARTIALLY RELEVANT -> Keep but de-weight; doc-22 (Rate Limits) IRRELEVANT -> Drop. After answer, model emits <issup> final verdict: FULLY-SUPPORTED | PARTIALLY-SUPPORTED | NO-SUPPORT. Labels how grounded the answer is in the kept docs.
summary: After retrieval Self-RAG grades each doc with <isrel> and grades the final answer's groundedness with <issup>.

text: Self-RAG wins: mixed-knowledge corpora (some queries need retrieval, some don't); latency-sensitive workloads (skips retrieval for ~40% of queries); adaptive depth (decides how many docs to fetch). Limits: requires a model trained for Self-RAG (not plug-and-play); token vocabulary increases serialization cost; calibration of the special-token classifier is the bottleneck. Pick Self-RAG when fine-tuning is on the table and the workload mixes retrieval-needing and already-knew-it queries.
summary: Self-RAG wins on adaptive mixed-knowledge workloads but requires fine-tuning and classifier calibration.

text: CRAG three-branch decision tree: an evaluator score routes retrieved docs to a CORRECT path (use directly), an AMBIGUOUS path (combine internal + web), or an INCORRECT path (web-search fallback), all converging into knowledge refinement before generation.
summary: Diagram showing the CRAG evaluator routing docs into three branches that converge into knowledge refinement and generation.
queries: Visual showing CORRECT AMBIGUOUS INCORRECT branches

text: CRAG (Corrective RAG, Yan et al. 2024) inserts a retrieval evaluator between retrieval and generation. Each retrieved doc is scored and classified as CORRECT (use directly), AMBIGUOUS (combine internal docs with external web search), or INCORRECT (fall back to web search). All branches converge into a knowledge refinement step that decomposes docs into 100-150 token strips, drops off-topic strips, and packs only on-topic ones before generation. Threshold guidance: below 0.5 = INCORRECT, above 0.8 = CORRECT, between = AMBIGUOUS. Demonstrated on query 'What is our cancellation policy for monthly plans?' where doc-7 scored 0.92 (CORRECT), doc-22 scored 0.31 (INCORRECT), doc-15 scored 0.62 (AMBIGUOUS).
summary: CRAG adds an evaluator that routes retrieved docs into CORRECT, AMBIGUOUS, or INCORRECT branches and refines surviving docs into strips before generation.
queries: When does CRAG fall back to web search
How to handle bad retrieval in RAG
How to detect irrelevant retrieved docs
What threshold makes a doc CORRECT in CRAG

text: CRAG (Yan et al. 2024) adds a retrieval evaluator between retrieval and generation. Each doc is scored and classified: CORRECT (use it), AMBIGUOUS (combine with external sources), or INCORRECT (fall back to web). Running query: 'What is our cancellation policy for monthly plans?' Scored docs: doc-7 (Refunds + Cancellation) score 0.92 verdict CORRECT - snippet 'Customers can cancel monthly plans any time before the next billing cycle.' doc-22 (API Rate Limits) score 0.31 verdict INCORRECT - snippet 'Free tier allows 100 requests per minute; Pro tier 1000 per minute.' doc-15 (Subscription Tiers) score 0.62 verdict AMBIGUOUS - snippet 'Monthly and annual plans differ in pricing and feature access.' The evaluator can be a small classifier model or an LLM judge. Threshold below 0.5 = INCORRECT, above 0.8 = CORRECT, between = AMBIGUOUS.
summary: The CRAG retrieval evaluator scores each retrieved doc and labels it CORRECT, AMBIGUOUS, or INCORRECT using thresholds 0.5 and 0.8.
queries: What does the CRAG evaluator do
How are retrieved docs scored in CRAG
What is a CORRECT verdict in CRAG
What is an AMBIGUOUS verdict in CRAG
What is an INCORRECT verdict in CRAG
Can the CRAG evaluator be an LLM
Small classifier vs LLM judge for retrieval scoring
Example CRAG scores for cancellation policy query
How to classify retrieved doc relevance
Why score retrieved docs before generation
How to gate RAG generation on doc quality

text: CORRECT branch: highest-confidence path. Doc passes the evaluator; CRAG routes it through knowledge refinement and then to the LLM with citations. Flow: Retrieved Doc -> Knowledge Refinement -> Generate Answer With Citations. Same as baseline RAG once the docs pass the evaluator.
summary: On the CORRECT branch, passing docs go through knowledge refinement and into generation just like baseline RAG.
queries: What happens on the CRAG CORRECT branch
How does CRAG use docs that pass the evaluator
Is CRAG CORRECT branch same as baseline RAG
Flow after CORRECT verdict in CRAG
When evaluator gives high score what happens

text: INCORRECT branch: doc fails the evaluator. CRAG rewrites the query for web search, calls an external search API, and uses the web results in place of the internal doc. Flow: Failed Doc -> Rewrite Query -> External Web Search -> Use Web Results -> Generate Answer. Web fallback costs latency (300-800 ms) and requires a search API. Use when refusal is unacceptable. Trade-off versus admitting 'I don't know'.
summary: On the INCORRECT branch, CRAG rewrites the query and falls back to web search instead of using the failed internal doc.
queries: What does CRAG do when a doc is INCORRECT
Latency cost of web fallback in CRAG
When evaluator score is too low what happens
Query rewriting for web search in CRAG
Why use web search instead of refusal

text: AMBIGUOUS branch: doc partially answers. CRAG keeps it AND also fetches external web results, then merges both into the generation prompt. Flow: Partial Doc + Web Results -> Merge Into Context -> Generate Answer. The hedge bet: highest cost path, lowest miss rate. Use when refusal is unacceptable but confidence in internal docs is low.
summary: On the AMBIGUOUS branch, CRAG hedges by combining the partial internal doc with web results before generating.
queries: What does CRAG do on AMBIGUOUS verdict
CRAG combine internal docs and web
When CRAG merges internal and external sources
Why combine docs and web search

text: CRAG knowledge refinement splits each retrieved doc into 100-150 token strips and scores each strip individually. Off-topic strips are dropped; on-topic strips are packed. Doc-7 (Refunds) starts at 800 tokens; after refinement, only 3 of 6 strips are kept (~50% reduction). Strip breakdown: Strip 1 Refund Eligibility KEEP. Strip 2 Annual Plans Prorating KEEP. Strip 3 Refund Processing Time KEEP. Strip 4 Tax Handling DROP. Strip 5 Legal Disclaimers DROP. Strip 6 Chargeback Policy DROP. Final: 3 kept strips, ~50% of original tokens, all on-topic. Packed into the prompt for generation.
summary: Knowledge refinement splits each doc into 100-150 token strips, drops off-topic ones, and keeps only the on-topic strips for the prompt.
queries: How many strips are kept after CRAG refinement

text: The full CRAG decision tree: Query + Retrieved Docs feed into an Evaluator Score diamond, which branches three ways - CORRECT -> Use Docs (green), AMBIGUOUS -> Combine (yellow), INCORRECT -> Web (red). All three branches converge into a Knowledge Refinement (Strips) step, which feeds Generate Answer. One evaluator. Three branches. All converge into knowledge refinement and generation (Yan et al. 2024).
summary: Full CRAG pipeline diagram: one evaluator routes to three branches that all converge into knowledge refinement and final generation.
queries: From evaluator to generation in CRAG
Where does knowledge refinement sit in CRAG

Five nodes highlighted in orange - Smith v Jones, Doe v Acme, Roe v Tech, Brown v Board, Garcia v State - with their connecting edges thickened, while the other 10 nodes stay dimmed. Civil Rights community (6 nodes, red) on top, Contract Law community (5 nodes, cyan) in the middle, IP Law community (4 nodes, orange) on the bottom. Cross-community edges visible.
