text: Chapter 22.7 introduces GraphRAG (Microsoft 2024), a retrieval pattern that replaces flat vector search with a knowledge graph built from entity and relation extraction. The chapter switches corpora from customer-support docs to a 15-node legal-citation network (cases as nodes, citations as directed edges) because GraphRAG needs rich entity-relationship structure. Offline pipeline: LLM extracts entities (Plaintiff, Defendant, Court, Statute) and relations (sued, decided-in, applies, holding) at ~$0.02 per doc, builds the 15-node 27-edge graph, then runs community detection (Leiden or Louvain) to cluster the graph into communities (Civil Rights, Contract Law, IP Law) and generate per-community LLM summaries. At query time, local queries retrieve a 5-node subgraph; global queries retrieve community summaries and map-reduce them. GraphRAG is worth it for entity-rich corpora with frequent global queries; not worth it for self-contained FAQ docs.
summary: Chapter-level summary of GraphRAG: knowledge graph from LLM entity extraction, community detection, subgraph retrieval for local queries, community summaries for global queries.
queries: entity and relation extraction for RAG
global vs local queries in GraphRAG
how to answer thematic queries with RAG

text: Why this chapter uses a different corpus. The customer-support corpus is mostly self-contained how-to pages; relationships are sparse, so GraphRAG over it would build a thin graph. This chapter switches to a 15-node legal-citation network because the customer-support corpus lacks rich entity-relationship structure. Customer Support Corpus - Won't Fit: 30 docs, mostly self-contained how-to pages, relationships sparse, a graph here would have few edges and demonstrate nothing. Legal Citation Network - Fits: 15 case decisions where each cites 2-5 prior cases, rich entities (Plaintiff, Defendant, Court, Statute) and a real graph topology. The customer-support corpus returns in chapter 22.8; this chapter only uses the legal-citation secondary corpus.
summary: Explains why GraphRAG demo swaps the customer-support corpus for a 15-node legal-citation network with richer entity-relationship structure.
queries: why not use FAQ docs with GraphRAG

text: Offline: extract entities and relations with an LLM. Each case runs through an LLM extraction prompt once at index time. The LLM emits entities (people, organizations, statutes) and relations (sued, decided-in, applies, holding). Cost is ~$0.02 per doc. Example: Smith v Jones (2018). Entities: Smith (Plaintiff), Jones (Defendant), Northern District Court, Title VII (Statute), 'Discrimination Found' (Holding). Relations: Smith --sued--> Jones; Smith v Jones --decided-in--> Northern District Court; Smith v Jones --applies--> Title VII; Smith v Jones --holding--> Discrimination Found.
summary: Shows the offline LLM extraction step that turns each doc into typed entities and relations at ~$0.02 per doc.

structured output from LLM for graph

text: The 15-node citation graph. Built once offline. Each node is a case; each directed edge is a citation from the citing case to the cited case. Updated incrementally as new cases come in. 15 cases, 27 citation edges. Built once offline; updated incrementally as new cases arrive. Nodes include Smith v Jones, Doe v Acme, Roe v Tech, Brown v Board, Garcia v State, Miller v EEOC, Apex v Beta, Cosmos v Stellar, Delta v Epsilon, Falcon v GoTech, Hera v Olympus, Aurora v Beacon, Cipher v Decode, Eagle v Falcon IP, Sigma v Tau.
summary: Describes the 15-node, 27-edge directed citation graph built offline as the GraphRAG corpus.

text: Query time: retrieve a subgraph. Query: 'What is the precedent chain for workplace discrimination cases?' The retriever finds a 5-node subgraph (Smith v Jones, Doe v Acme, Roe v Tech, Brown v Board, Garcia v State) and sends only those nodes + their edges to the LLM. Entity-aware retrieval; the graph topology is the signal. 5 nodes highlighted in orange. The LLM reads only the highlighted subgraph - not the whole 15-node corpus.
summary: Walks through a local query that retrieves a 5-node precedent-chain subgraph instead of the full corpus.
queries: how does GraphRAG retrieve at query time
what does LLM see in GraphRAG
Smith v Jones Brown v Board subgraph

text: Communities: cluster the graph for global queries. Community detection (Leiden or Louvain) groups densely-connected nodes into clusters. Each community gets an LLM-generated summary at index time. Global queries hit the community summaries; local queries hit the subgraph. Civil Rights (6 nodes): cases established protected-class precedents under Title VII and the Equal Protection Clause. Contract Law (5 nodes): cases interpreted breach-of-contract remedies, specific performance, and damages caps. IP Law (4 nodes): cases shaped fair-use boundaries, trademark dilution, and software copyrightability.
summary: Introduces community detection (Leiden/Louvain), per-community LLM summaries, and the three example communities Civil Rights, Contract Law, IP Law.
queries: what is community detection in GraphRAG
why cluster the graph in RAG
Civil Rights Contract Law IP Law communities
what is a community in a graph

text: Global queries use communities, local queries use subgraphs. Two query patterns, two routing paths. Local queries need a specific subgraph; global queries need an overview that no single subgraph contains. Local Query: 'How did the court rule in Smith v Jones?' Plan: retrieve the Smith v Jones node + 1-hop neighbors (5 nodes total). Output: direct answer from the subgraph. Global Query: 'What are the main themes across this corpus?' Plan: retrieve all 3 community summaries; map-reduce summarize them. Output: high-level themes answer the question without retrieving every doc. Global queries are GraphRAG's killer feature. Naive RAG cannot answer them without retrieving everything; GraphRAG answers from the community summaries.
summary: Contrasts local vs global query routing in GraphRAG with concrete plans and outputs, highlighting global queries as the killer feature.
queries: what is a global query in RAG
what is a local query in RAG
how does naive RAG fail global queries
Smith v Jones local query example
answering corpus-wide questions without full retrieval
why naive RAG fails on summarization

text: When to reach for GraphRAG. GraphRAG is expensive to build and maintain. Use it when the corpus has rich entity-relationship structure and global queries are common. Worth It: rich entity-relationship corpus (legal, biomedical, code); global queries common (themes, summaries, comparisons); long-term value (build the graph once, query many times). Not Worth It: mostly self-contained docs (FAQ, support knowledge base - like our primary corpus); only local / factoid queries; schema churn (entities change weekly). Cost: per-doc LLM extraction at index time (~$0.02 per doc); re-extract on doc changes; graph storage + community detection compute. Mitigations: cache extractions; run community detection nightly, not per-query; hybrid: GraphRAG for global queries, naive RAG for local.
summary: Decision card listing when GraphRAG is worth it vs not, its cost drivers, and mitigations like caching and hybrid routing.
queries: build graph once query many times
decision criteria for knowledge graph RAG

text: Tool-call loop flowchart for agentic RAG: User Query enters the Model node. Model output flows into a 'Need A Tool?' decision diamond. YES branch leads right to a tool_call(...) box; tool_response is fed back to the Model, completing the loop. NO branch leads left to the Final Answer box. Label at bottom: max_iterations = 10. Hard Cap Prevents Runaway Loops.
summary: SVG flowchart of the agentic RAG tool-call loop with user query, model, tool decision, loopback, final answer, and max_iterations cap.
queries: yes no branches agent tool decision

text: Chapter 22.8 covers Tool-Augmented and Agentic RAG: treating retrieval as one of several tools the LLM can call alongside SQL queries, calculators, web search, and internal APIs. The model uses a function-calling pattern, emitting structured tool_call tokens that the runtime executes and feeds back in a loop until the model produces a final answer or hits max_iterations (typically 5-10). A worked multi-tool trace shows compare-plans-and-estimate-cost answered in four turns: vector_search, sql_query, calculator, then final answer. Loop control combines hard caps, soft divergence signals, cost tracking, and mitigations like caching tool responses and using a smaller orchestrator model. Frameworks like LangGraph are optional - DIY while-loops work and are framework-agnostic.
summary: Overview of agentic RAG: function-calling loop, multi-tool traces, loop control, and orchestration trade-offs.
queries: how does LLM pick a tool
cost tracking for agent tool calls

text: Retrieval Is One Tool. Add More. Tool-augmented (agentic) RAG treats retrieval as one of several tools the LLM can invoke. SQL, calculators, web search, and internal APIs sit alongside it. The model picks the right tool per query. Four example tools: Vector Search - semantic retrieval over your embedded chunks, the RAG we have been building. SQL Query - structured lookups over a relational store, pricing tables, user accounts. Calculator - precise arithmetic; models are bad at math, calculators are not. Web Search - fresh or out-of-corpus info, used as a fallback or for time-sensitive queries. The LLM decides which tool to call. Function-calling pattern. Each tool returns text the model can read.
summary: Agentic RAG treats vector retrieval as just one tool among SQL, calculator, and web search that the LLM dispatches.

vector search SQL calculator web search comparison
what is a tool in agentic RAG
tools that return text to LLM

text:
Function-Calling: How The Model Picks A Tool. The model emits structured tool_call tokens; the runtime intercepts, executes the tool, and feeds the response back. The loop continues until the model emits a final answer or hits max_iterations. Function-Calling Trace:

User: Compare the Pro and Enterprise plans.

Model: I need the pricing table. Calling tool.
  tool_call: sql_query(table="plans", filter="name in ('Pro','Enterprise')")
  tool_response: [{"plan":"Pro","price":29,"users":10},
                  {"plan":"Enterprise","price":199,"users":"unlimited"}]

Model: I also need feature comparison docs. Calling tool.
  tool_call: vector_search(query="Pro vs Enterprise features", k=3)
  tool_response: [doc-15 chunk 1, doc-15 chunk 2, doc-19 chunk 1]

Model: Now I can answer. Generating response with citations.
summary: Concrete function-calling trace showing the model emitting tool_call tokens, the runtime returning tool_response, and the loop continuing until a final answer.
queries: what does a tool_call look like
agent trace with two tool calls
how model chains SQL then vector search
LLM emits tool_call then final answer

text: The Tool-Call Loop. The control loop runs until the model declares done or max_iterations is reached. A loopback path lets the model chain multiple tool calls. Flow: User Query -> Model -> decision diamond 'Need A Tool?'. YES branch -> tool_call(...) -> tool_response fed back to Model (loopback). NO branch -> Final Answer. Hard cap: max_iterations = 10 prevents runaway loops.
summary: Flowchart of the tool-call loop: model decides whether to call a tool, executes it, loops back, or emits a final answer, with max_iterations as hard cap.
queries: how does the tool calling loop work
tool response fed back to model
when does agent stop calling tools

text: Worked Example: Multi-Tool Trace. Query: 'Compare Pro and Enterprise plans, then estimate cost for 25 users on Pro.' Four turns, three tools, one final answer. Turn 1: vector_search("Pro Enterprise comparison") -> 3 chunks (doc-15 + doc-19). Turn 2: sql_query(SELECT price FROM plans WHERE name='Pro') -> $29 per seat. Turn 3: calculator(25 x 29) -> 725. Turn 4: Final Answer -> Pro is $29/seat. 25 users => $725/month. Pro includes features X, Y, Z [doc-15]; Enterprise adds SSO + audit logs [doc-19].
summary: Four-turn worked trace combining vector_search, sql_query, and calculator to compare plans and estimate cost for 25 Pro users.
queries: compare Pro Enterprise SSO audit logs

text: Loop Control: Termination Criteria. Termination is a layered defense. Hard caps stop runaway loops; soft signals catch loops that are technically progressing but diverging; cost tracking catches expensive flows; mitigations cut the rate. Hard Caps: max_iterations (typically 5-10); total tool calls per query (rate-limit by tool); wall-clock budget (timeout in seconds). Soft Signals: model emits 'final answer' marker; same tool called with same args twice in a row -> divergence flag; tool error rate above threshold -> abort and refuse. Cost Tracking: each tool call has a fixed cost, sum across iterations; alert if over per-query budget; bill chargebacks to feature owners. Mitigations: cap iterations strictly; cache tool responses by exact args (especially vector_search); use a smaller orchestrator model.
summary: Layered loop termination: hard caps, soft divergence signals, cost tracking, and mitigations like caching and smaller orchestrator models.
queries: same tool same args twice signal
chargeback agent costs to feature owners
soft signals vs hard caps agents

text: Orchestration: Frameworks Like LangGraph. Pick orchestration last. The patterns above work without any framework. Frameworks are a productivity choice, not a correctness one. DIY (Custom While Loop): you implement the function-calling parse, tool dispatch, max_iterations check, response merge yourself. Full control. More code. LangGraph And Similar Frameworks: graph-shaped state machine for tool flows. Nodes are tool calls, edges are transitions. Built-in checkpointing, retries, human-in-the-loop. Less code. Framework lock-in. Pick a framework when it saves more time than it costs. Patterns above are framework-agnostic.
summary: Compares DIY custom while-loop orchestration with graph frameworks like LangGraph - frameworks are a productivity, not correctness, choice.
queries: tool dispatch parse function calling DIY

text: Chapter 22.9 Long-Context vs RAG. Compares three strategies on a 200-page (~120k token) product manual that fits in a 200k context window. Long-Context Only: stuff all 120k tokens, $0.36/query, 5-8s latency, lost-in-the-middle risk. RAG-Only: 240 chunks of 512 tokens, retrieve top-5 (2,560 tokens), $0.008/query (~45x cheaper), ~1.5s, risks missing the right chunk. Hybrid: retrieve top-30 (~15k tokens), optional rerank, pack 50k with front-load or sandwich ordering, $0.15/query, ~3s, highest accuracy (92% golden recall). Hybrid is the production default; RAG-only for high-volume cost-sensitive workloads; long-context-only for single-doc, low-volume cases with no retrieval infra.
summary: Compares RAG-only, hybrid, and long-context-only on cost, latency, and accuracy for a 120k-token manual, with hybrid as the production default.

text: This chapter switches from the 30-doc customer-support corpus to a single 200-page product manual to compare retrieval against just stuffing the prompt. Customer Support Corpus: 30 docs at 500-2000 tokens each, total ~50k tokens, already fits in modern context windows but distributed across 30 distinct docs so retrieval is the natural pattern. 200-Page Product Manual: a single ~120k token reference that fits in a 200k context window. This is the 'do I even need RAG?' test case. When the entire knowledge base is one long document and fits in the context window, the retrieval question changes shape.

text: RAG Only: Chunk, Retrieve, Pack Small. Chunk the 200-page manual into 240 chunks of 512 tokens. At query time, embed the query, retrieve top-5 (2,560 tokens), pack into prompt. Pipeline: Chunk Manual Into 240 Chunks -> Retrieve Top-5 -> Pack ~2,560 Tokens -> LLM Reads -> Answer. Cost: ~$0.008 per query, ~45x cheaper than long-context-only. Latency: 50ms retrieval + 1-2s generation = ~1.5s. Risk: if relevant chunk missed by top-5, the answer is wrong. Massively cheaper. Massively faster. Risk: if the relevant chunk isn't in top-5, you get the wrong answer.

text: Hybrid: Retrieve Top-30, Pack 50k Tokens. The pragmatic middle. Retrieve top-30 chunks (15,000 tokens), optionally rerank, then pack 50k tokens into the prompt. Reorder using lost-in-middle strategies (front-load or sandwich, from chapter 22.2). Pipeline: Retrieve Top-30 -> Optional Rerank -> Pack 50k Tokens -> Front-Load Or Sandwich -> LLM -> Answer. Cost: ~$0.15 per query (5x cheaper than long-context-only). Latency: 80ms retrieval + 3s generation = ~3s. Accuracy: highest. More candidates = lower miss rate. The pragmatic middle. Most production systems land here.
summary: Hybrid retrieves top-30 (~15k tokens), optionally reranks, packs 50k with front-load/sandwich ordering for ~$0.15, ~3s, and highest accuracy.

text: Decision: When Each One Wins. Three strategies, three sweet spots. Pick by which trade-off your workload cares about most. RAG Only: cost-critical at scale (~$0.008/query), latency-critical (~1.5s p50), large multi-doc corpus, citations required (chunk-level provenance). Hybrid: default for production, best accuracy on accuracy-critical workloads, acceptable cost + latency tradeoff, citations still feasible. Long-Context Only: single long document corpus, low query volume (cost not pressing), citations not required (or post-extracted), no retrieval infrastructure available. Hybrid is the production default. Long-context-only is a niche. RAG-only is for high-volume cost-sensitive systems.
summary: Decision card: pick RAG-only for cost/latency at scale, hybrid for production accuracy, long-context only for single-doc low-volume cases.

text: Chapter 23.1 introduces the RAG Eval Triangle: three layers of evaluation that let any wrong final answer trace to a specific root cause. Layer 1 (Retrieval) asks 'did we find the right docs?' using Recall@k, MRR, Precision@k, and nDCG@k. Layer 2 (Generation) asks 'did the model use them faithfully?' using Faithfulness and Answer Relevancy. Layer 3 (End-to-End) asks 'did the final answer help the user?' using Correctness (binary vs gold) and Helpfulness (1-5 Likert). Three failure scenarios demonstrate how a wrong answer traces to retrieval, generation, or product. Word-overlap metrics BLEU, ROUGE, and METEOR are deprecated for RAG because they measure n-gram proximity to a reference, not faithfulness or groundedness; use RAGAS and LLM-as-judge instead.
summary: Overview of the three-layer RAG evaluation triangle (retrieval, generation, end-to-end), the metrics at each layer, and why BLEU/ROUGE are deprecated.

The triangle gives one named layer for each possible root cause of failure.

text:
Layer 1: Retrieval Metrics. Retrieval metrics measure whether the search step found the right docs. Four metrics dominate the literature:

- Recall@k: Recall@k = relevant_retrieved / total_relevant. What fraction of all the relevant docs were retrieved?
- MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank): MRR = (1/N) * sum( 1 / rank_of_first_relevant ). Mean reciprocal rank of the first relevant doc per query.
- Precision@k: Precision@k = relevant_retrieved / k. What fraction of the top-k retrieved are actually relevant?
- nDCG@k: nDCG@k = DCG@k / IDCG@k. Normalised discounted cumulative gain - rank-weighted relevance.

Example: top-3 returned for 'How do I reset my password?': doc-1 (relevant), doc-23 (not), doc-7 (relevant) -> Precision@3 = 2 / 3 = 0.667.
summary: Four retrieval metrics for RAG with their formulas: Recall@k, MRR, Precision@k, and nDCG@k, plus a Precision@3 worked example.
queries: what is MRR mean reciprocal rank
metrics for evaluating search step in RAG
how to measure if RAG retriever found right docs

text:
Layer 2: Generation Metrics. Generation metrics measure whether the model used the retrieved context to produce a grounded answer. Faithfulness is the headline metric here, paired with answer relevancy.

- Faithfulness: Are the claims in the answer supported by the retrieved context? Example: answer claims '24-hour expiry'; retrieved doc-1 says 'the link expires after 24 hours' -> claim supported -> score 1.0.
- Answer Relevancy: Does the answer actually address the question? Example: Question 'How do I reset my password?' Answer 'Click forgot password and check your email' -> relevancy 0.9.
summary: Generation-layer metrics Faithfulness and Answer Relevancy with worked examples scoring claims against retrieved context and question alignment.
queries: how to measure if model used the context
does answer address the question metric

text:
Layer 3: End-To-End Metrics. End-to-end metrics measure whether the user actually got what they needed. Correctness compares to a gold answer; helpfulness asks the user on a 1-5 Likert scale.

- Correctness: Did the final answer match the gold answer? (binary). Example: Gold: 'Click forgot password and check email.' Generated: 'Click forgot password link and check your registered email.' -> 1.
- Helpfulness: How useful was the answer to the user? (1-5 Likert). Example: user rated this answer 4/5: 'Solved it but had to scroll'.
summary: End-to-end metrics Correctness (binary vs gold answer) and Helpfulness (1-5 Likert from user) with worked examples.
queries: what is correctness in RAG eval
how to measure user-facing RAG quality

text:
Why Three Layers? Failures Trace To A Layer. With three layers, a wrong final answer locates to a specific root cause. Walk the multi-hop query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' through three failure scenarios:

- Scenario A - Retrieval Fault: Top-k returns doc-4 (refunds) for a password-reset query; doc-1 never surfaces. Recall@10 drops from 0.95 to 0.30. Root cause: retrieval. Fix in the retriever (21.1-21.8).
- Scenario B - Generation Fault: Retrieval returns doc-1 + doc-7 correctly. The model ignores them and hallucinates a phone-support step. Faithfulness drops to 0.50. Root cause: generation. Fix the prompt / model / refusal logic (22.3).
- Scenario C - Product Fault: Retrieval and generation both score above 0.9. Yet user reports 'this did not help'. Helpfulness drops to 2/5. Root cause: product / prompting / answer style. Fix online via 23.5.
summary: Three failure scenarios show how Recall, Faithfulness, and Helpfulness drops each point to a different root cause (retrieval, generation, product).

text: Deprecated For RAG: BLEU & ROUGE (and METEOR). These are word-overlap metrics: they count how many n-grams in the generated answer also appear in a reference answer. They were designed for machine translation and summarisation, where wording proximity matters. They do not measure faithfulness or groundedness. A perfectly faithful answer written in different words scores 0; a hallucinated answer that recycles reference words scores high. Use RAGAS (chapter 23.3) and LLM-as-judge (chapter 23.2) instead.
summary: BLEU, ROUGE, and METEOR are word-overlap metrics built for translation/summarisation and are wrong for RAG because they ignore faithfulness; use RAGAS and LLM-as-judge instead.
queries: why is BLEU bad for RAG
is ROUGE good for RAG evaluation

After curve (green): rises from 0.78 at low percentile up to 0.92 at high percentile, crossing above the 0.85 target. Labels: 'Before: ~60%' under the red curve, 'After: ~92%' above the green curve. The after curve was produced by refining the rubric and applying position-bias and verbosity-bias mitigations, showing how calibration narrows the gap to human judgement.

text: Chapter 12.32 covers LLM-as-judge: using one LLM to evaluate another LLM's outputs. It contrasts human annotation (slow, expensive, high quality), heuristics like BLEU/ROUGE (fast, free, low quality), and LLM-as-judge (fast, medium cost, near-human when calibrated, agreement 0.85+). It walks through the judge prompt artifact (framing, 1-5 criteria, JSON output), the rubric design that anchors integer scores to behaviours for Faithfulness, three biases (position bias - first slot wins; verbosity bias - longer answers preferred even when wrong; self-preference bias - judges favor their own model family), each with mitigations (randomize/swap order, weight conciseness or length-normalise, cross-family judging). Closes with calibration: spot-check 50-100 human-labeled cases until agreement with human rises from ~60% baseline to 0.85+ target (after curve hits ~92%). Use a stronger or cross-family judge model.
summary: Overview of LLM-as-judge for RAG eval: trade-offs vs human/heuristics, judge prompt, 1-5 rubric, three biases with mitigations, and human-spot-check calibration to 0.85+ agreement.

text: Why use an LLM to judge an LLM? Human annotation is the gold standard but does not scale. Word-overlap heuristics like BLEU and ROUGE are fast but miss faithfulness. LLM-as-judge sits between the two: fast, medium cost, and near-human quality on well-rubric'd tasks. Three methods compared: Human Annotation - speed Very Slow, cost Expensive, quality High, 1-2 minutes per example, costs scale with team size. Heuristic (BLEU / ROUGE) - speed Instant, cost Free, quality Low, n-gram overlap that misses faithfulness and groundedness entirely. LLM-As-Judge - speed Fast, cost Medium, quality Near-Human, seconds per example; agreement with human reaches 0.85+ when calibrated.
summary: Compares three eval methods - human annotation, BLEU/ROUGE heuristics, and LLM-as-judge - on speed, cost, and quality to justify LLM-as-judge as the practical middle path.

text: The judge prompt is a text artifact, not a code block. It frames the LLM as an evaluator, names three criteria with a 1-5 scale, and asks for a JSON response. Variable placeholders are filled in at eval time. Prompt text: 'You are an expert evaluator. You will be given a question, the retrieved context, and the candidate answer. Score the answer on three criteria, each 1 (terrible) to 5 (excellent), with one short justification per criterion. Faithfulness: are all claims in the answer supported by the retrieved context? Answer Relevancy: does the answer address the question directly? Helpfulness: would this answer actually help the user reach their goal? Question: {question} Retrieved Context: {retrieved_context} Candidate Answer: {generated_answer} Return JSON: { "faithfulness": <1-5>, "answer_relevancy": <1-5>, "helpfulness": <1-5>, "justification": "<one short paragraph>" }'
summary: Shows the actual judge prompt artifact - role framing, three criteria (faithfulness, answer relevancy, helpfulness) on a 1-5 scale, variable placeholders, and required JSON output schema.

text: Rubric design: 1-5 per criterion. The rubric is the actual calibration tool. Prose framing in the prompt sets tone; the rubric anchors every integer score to a concrete behaviour. Faithfulness rubric: Score 5 - All Claims Supported, None Missing: every factual statement in the answer is verifiable in the retrieved context. Score 4 - Mostly Supported, Minor Unsupported Detail: core claims grounded; one peripheral detail not in context. Score 3 - About Half Supported: a roughly even mix of supported and ungrounded statements. Score 2 - Mostly Unsupported; Some Grounded: most claims are made up; one or two are anchored in the context. Score 1 - Hallucinated; Nothing In Context Supports It: answer is a confident hallucination with no grounding at all. Rubric design beats prompt prose. The rubric is what makes scores reproducible.
summary: Defines a 5-point Faithfulness rubric where each integer score is anchored to a concrete behaviour, showing why the rubric (not the prose) is what produces reproducible judge scores.
queries: How to design a judge rubric?

How to make judge scores reproducible

text: Bias 1: Position Bias. In pairwise judging, the order of the two candidates affects the verdict even when their content is identical. The first slot wins more often. Order 1 (A, B) - judge picks position 1 (A): same two answers, same judge model, the first slot wins. Order 2 (B, A) - judge picks position 1 (B): swap the order, the judge's pick swaps too. Identical content. Mitigation: Randomize Or Swap. Run both orders for every pair and average; or randomize position per query before passing to the judge.
summary: Explains position bias in pairwise LLM judging - the first slot wins regardless of content - and mitigates by swapping/averaging both orderings or randomizing position.
queries: What is position bias in LLM-as-judge?
Why does the first answer win in pairwise judging?
Does order matter in LLM judging?
Swap order trick for LLM judge
Why swap A and B in judge prompt

text: Bias 2: Verbosity Bias. Judges (especially weaker models) prefer longer answers, mistaking length for thoroughness. The longer answer can be wrong and still score higher than a tight correct one. Answer A - 50 Words, Accurate: 'Go to Settings, click Forgot Password, check your email, use the link within 24 hours.' All claims supported. No padding. Answer B - 200 Words, One Hallucination: 'To begin the secure password recovery process, navigate to your account dashboard... You may also call our 24/7 support line.' (Note: phone support claim is unsupported.) Longer feels thorough. Judges (especially weaker ones) score it higher. Mitigation: Weight Conciseness Or Length-Normalise. Add an explicit conciseness criterion to the rubric, or normalise by answer length before averaging scores.
summary: Demonstrates verbosity bias - judges prefer longer answers even when they hallucinate - using a 50-word accurate vs 200-word hallucinated example, and mitigates by adding conciseness criteria or length normalisation.
queries: What is verbosity bias in LLM judges?
Why do LLM judges prefer longer answers?
Do longer answers win in LLM eval?

text: Bias 3: Self-Preference Bias. Judges score outputs from their own model family higher than human-aligned scores would. The bigger the model, the bigger this effect typically gets. Examples: Judge = GPT-4o, Candidate = GPT-4o -> +12% vs Human (same family). Judge = GPT-4o, Candidate = Claude 3.5 -> -3% vs Human (different family). Judge = Claude 3.5, Candidate = Claude 3.5 -> +9% vs Human (same family). Mitigation: Third-Party / Cross-Family Judging. Pick a judge from a different model family than the candidates. Or average across multiple judge families to wash out single-family bias.
summary: Shows self-preference bias - judges score their own model family +9 to +12% above human-aligned scores - and mitigates with cross-family judging or averaging across families.
queries: What is self-preference bias in LLM judges?
Do LLM judges prefer their own model family?
How much does self-preference bias inflate scores?
Bias when judge and candidate are same model
Why not use the same model to judge itself

text: Calibrate: Spot-Check 50-100 Cases. A judge is a hypothesis. Before trusting its scores in production, verify agreement against 50-100 human-labeled cases. Refine the rubric and mitigations until correlation lands above 0.85. Calibration chart: x-axis is Judge Score Percentile (Low To High); y-axis is Agreement With Human. A horizontal target line is drawn at 0.85. Before curve (red) sits flat around 0.55-0.60 across all percentiles - labeled 'Before: ~60%'. After curve (green) rises from 0.78 at low percentile to 0.92 at high percentile - labeled 'After: ~92%'. The after curve crosses above the 0.85 target line. Judge Model Selection: use a stronger judge model than the candidate (e.g., judge with GPT-4 a candidate from a 7B model). Or use the same model with documented caveats. Cross-family judging is the strongest setup.
summary: Calibrate the judge against 50-100 human-labeled cases until agreement crosses the 0.85 target - the before-curve sits at ~60% agreement, the after-curve (rubric refined plus bias mitigations) rises to ~92%.
queries: How to calibrate an LLM judge?
How many human-labeled cases for calibration?
What agreement should an LLM judge have with humans?
Before and after calibration of LLM judge
When to trust LLM judge in production

text: Chapter 23.3 covers RAGAS, a 2023 reference-free RAG evaluation suite that uses an LLM-judge to compute four metrics: Faithfulness (verifiable_claims / total_claims, 4/5 = 0.8 password reset example), Answer Relevancy (mean cosine between original query and n=3 round-trip-generated questions, 0.913 example), Context Precision (relevant top-k / k, 2/3 = 0.667 example; also called ContextualRelevancyScore in DeepEval/TruLens), and Context Recall (retrieved relevant / all relevant in corpus, 2/2 = 1.0 with a 1/2 = 0.5 counter-example). Combined into a per-query report card averaging 0.845. Contrasts with BLEU/ROUGE n-gram metrics which target translation/summarisation and miss faithfulness.
summary: Chapter-level overview of the four RAGAS metrics, their formulas, worked examples, the per-query report card, and why BLEU/ROUGE are unfit.
queries: How to evaluate a RAG system end to end

text: RAGAS (Retrieval-Augmented Generation Assessment) is a 2023 metric suite that uses an LLM under the hood to compute eval scores without needing a reference answer. It has four core metrics, two for retrieval and two for generation: Faithfulness (Are the claims supported?), Answer Relevancy (Does it answer the question?), Context Precision (Are top results actually relevant?), and Context Recall (Did we retrieve everything?).
summary: Introduces RAGAS as a reference-free, LLM-judged eval suite with four metrics split across retrieval and generation.
queries: How to evaluate RAG without a ground truth answer?
How does RAGAS work without reference answers?
Faithfulness Answer Relevancy Context Precision Context Recall

text: Faithfulness asks whether every claim in the generated answer can be verified in the retrieved context. An LLM-judge extracts atomic claims from the answer and labels each as supported or not. Formula: Faithfulness = |verifiable_claims| / |total_claims_in_answer|. Worked example: retrieved context from doc-1 says 'To reset your password, go to Settings > Security. Click Forgot Password. An email is sent to your registered address. The link expires after 24 hours.' Generated answer: 'Go to Settings > Security, click Forgot Password, check your email, and use the link within 24 hours. You can also call support.' Five atomic claims extracted: (1) Go to Settings > Security - Supported, (2) Click Forgot Password - Supported, (3) Check your email - Supported, (4) Use the link within 24 hours - Supported, (5) You can also call support - Not Supported. Faithfulness = 4 / 5 = 0.8.
summary: Defines Faithfulness as the fraction of generated claims supported by retrieved context, with a 4/5 = 0.8 password reset worked example.
queries: What is the Faithfulness metric in RAGAS?
How to detect hallucinations in RAG?
What does it mean for a claim to be unsupported?
Does the answer match the retrieved context?

text: Answer Relevancy is a round-trip check: the LLM-judge reads the generated answer and writes n new questions that the answer would best answer. Each generated question is embedded and compared with cosine similarity to the original query. The average is the relevancy score. Formula: Answer Relevancy = (1/n) * sum( cosine( embed(q), embed(q_i_generated) ) ). Worked example with n = 3: original query 'How do I reset my password?'. The judge generates Q1 'What are the steps to reset my password?' (cos 0.94), Q2 'How can I change my password if I forgot it?' (cos 0.91), Q3 'How do I update my account password?' (cos 0.89). Answer Relevancy = (0.94 + 0.91 + 0.89) / 3 = 0.913.
summary: Defines Answer Relevancy as average cosine similarity between the original query and n round-trip questions generated from the answer, scoring 0.913 in the example.
queries: What is Answer Relevancy in RAGAS?
How does the round-trip relevancy check work?
Why generate questions from the answer?
Does the answer address the question?
How is n chosen for relevancy?
Comparing generated questions to original query

text: Context Precision asks what fraction of the top-k retrieved chunks were actually relevant. RAGAS adds a rank-aware weighting in the full version, but the simplified ratio is taught here. Formula: Context Precision = relevant_chunks_at_top_k / k. Worked example for top-3 on the password reset query: Rank 1 'doc-1 chunk-1 - Password Reset Intro' Relevant, Rank 2 'doc-23 chunk-2 - 500 Error Troubleshooting' Not Relevant, Rank 3 'doc-1 chunk-2 - Password Reset Email Step' Relevant. Context Precision = 2 / 3 = 0.667. Naming note: DeepEval and TruLens call this ContextualRelevancyScore - same idea under a different framework name. Older RAGAS shipped a separate 'Context Relevancy' metric; it was deprecated in favor of Context Precision plus Context Recall.
summary: Defines Context Precision as fraction of top-k retrieved chunks that are relevant, illustrated with a 2/3 = 0.667 example and a DeepEval/TruLens naming note.
queries: Difference between Context Relevancy and Context Precision

text: Context Recall asks what fraction of all the relevant chunks in the corpus were retrieved. It is the dual of precision: precision says 'are top results clean?', recall says 'did we miss anything?'. Formula: Context Recall = relevant_chunks_retrieved / all_relevant_chunks_in_corpus. Worked example: corpus has 2 chunks relevant to 'How do I reset my password?': doc-1 chunk-1 and doc-1 chunk-2. Both retrieved -> Context Recall = 2 / 2 = 1.0. Counter-example: if retrieval returned only doc-1 chunk-1 and missed doc-1 chunk-2, Context Recall = 1 / 2 = 0.5.
summary: Defines Context Recall as fraction of corpus-relevant chunks retrieved, with a 2/2 = 1.0 case and a 1/2 = 0.5 missed-chunk counter-example.
queries: What is Context Recall in RAGAS?
Did retrieval miss any relevant chunks?
Precision vs recall in RAG eval

text: Putting the four metrics together as a per-query report card. One query produces four scores. The retrieval pair (precision + recall) covers the search step; the generation pair (faithfulness + answer relevancy) covers the model. Example scores: Faithfulness 0.80, Answer Relevancy 0.91, Context Precision 0.67, Context Recall 1.00. Average = (0.80 + 0.91 + 0.67 + 1.00) / 4 = 0.845 - the headline score per query. How RAGAS computes the scores: an LLM-judge extracts atomic claims for faithfulness, generates round-trip questions for relevancy, and labels each retrieved chunk as relevant or not for precision and recall.
summary: Combines all four RAGAS metrics into a per-query report card averaging to 0.845, splitting retrieval (precision/recall) from generation (faithfulness/relevancy).
queries: What does an LLM-judge do in RAGAS?

text: BLEU and ROUGE are not RAGAS. They compute n-gram overlap between the generated answer and a reference answer. They were designed for machine translation and summarisation, where wording proximity matters. They do not measure faithfulness or groundedness: a perfectly faithful answer in different wording scores low, and a hallucinated answer that recycles reference words scores high. Use RAGAS or LLM-as-judge instead.
summary: Explains why BLEU and ROUGE n-gram metrics from translation/summarisation are unsuitable for RAG and should be replaced by RAGAS or LLM-as-judge.
queries: Why not use BLEU for RAG?
Why ROUGE fails for grounded answers

The upper lane shows the LLM generating question-answer pairs grounded in a corpus document; the lower lane shows a human reviewer accepting, editing, or rejecting each pair. Illustrates how a small hand-built seed scales into a 500-2000 reviewed set without losing human oversight. LLM lane segments: Corpus Doc -> LLM Generates Q-A Pair -> Pair Candidate. Bridge arrow down to Human lane segments: Pair Candidate -> Reviewer Accept/Edit/Reject -> Golden Record.
summary: Diagram of the two-lane LLM-generate then human-review pipeline that scales golden datasets while keeping human oversight.

text: Chapter 12.34 Golden Datasets explains how to build and maintain the human-verified ground-truth set every other RAG eval metric depends on. Covers the structure of a golden record (question, expected answer, expected docs, refusal flag), starting with a 30-100 hand-written seed across query types, the five edge cases that must be covered (multi-hop, empty-context, ambiguous, refusal-required, time-sensitive), the regression set that captures every production bug as a permanent test, LLM-bootstrapped generation with mandatory human review to scale to 500-2000 records, and a monthly review cadence with coverage, freshness, and pass-rate health metrics.
summary: Overview of building, scaling, and maintaining golden datasets for RAG evaluation.

text: Golden Datasets are the ground truth every other eval metric depends on. RAGAS, LLM-as-judge, and recall@k all need human-verified labels. The golden dataset is the held-out, human-verified set of records run against every eval. Without it you have no signal; with a flaky one you have noise. One golden record has four fields: Question ('How do I reset my password?'), Expected Answer ('Click Forgot Password and check your registered email.'), Expected Docs ([doc-1]), Refusal (No). Multiply by 30-2000 records and you have a defensible eval set.
summary: A golden record is a tuple of question, expected answer, expected docs, and refusal flag - the ground truth for every other RAG metric.

text: Start with 30-100 hand-written examples. The goal of the first batch is breadth across query types, not volume. Three curation steps: (1) List query types from logs or product spec - mine a week of production queries (or the product spec) for the categories you must support. (2) Write 5-10 examples per type by hand - cover normal cases plus edge cases. (3) Write expected answer + expected docs per example - each tuple becomes one golden record. Week 1 tally by query type: Single-Doc Lookups 20, Multi-Hop 15, Aggregation 10, Refusal-Required 10, Empty-Context 10. Total Week 1 = 65.
summary: Curate the first 30-100 records by hand across query types from logs, aiming for breadth not volume.
queries: how to start a golden dataset
first batch of RAG eval cases

mine production queries for eval categories

where to source first eval queries

query type distribution in eval seed

text: Five edge cases that break naive RAG and must be covered in the first hand-written batch: (1) Multi-Hop - answer requires combining 2+ docs - example: 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' (2) Empty-Context - no doc in the corpus answers this, must refuse - example: 'What is your stock price?' (3) Ambiguous - two docs conflict, newest-wins or refusal - example: 'What is the refund window?' (doc-A: 14 days; doc-B: 30 days). (4) Refusal-Required - out-of-scope or unsafe query - example: 'Give me another customer's email.' (5) Time-Sensitive - answer depends on today's date - example: 'Is feature X live yet?' Skip any and you ship blind to a class of failures.
summary: Five edge cases - multi-hop, empty-context, ambiguous, refusal-required, time-sensitive - must be covered or you ship blind.
queries: five edge cases for golden dataset
what test cases must I cover

text: Regression Set: every production bug becomes a golden case. Anything that failed in production once must never silently fail again. The regression set is the self-healing memory of your eval suite. Four-step workflow: (1) Production user reports a bad answer. (2) Engineer reproduces; captures query, docs, answer, expected answer. (3) Add tuple to the regression-golden set. (4) Every future eval includes it; failure cannot silently re-emerge. Example regression tuples: 'Reset password forgot email' -> multi-hop missed, bot suggested support phone; 'Cancel and refund' -> refused incorrectly, user blocked from valid request; 'Pro plan SSO' -> hallucinated SSO inclusion in Pro tier.
summary: Every production failure is captured into the regression set so the same bug cannot ship twice.
queries: what is a regression set in RAG eval
how to handle production bugs in RAG
workflow for adding failures to golden set
capture production bug to test case
how to track production RAG failures

text: LLM-Bootstrapped pipeline: generate then human-review. To scale past the hand-written seed, generate question-answer pairs with an LLM grounded in corpus docs, then human-review each pair. Acceptance rate is 60-80% for clear docs. A 30-100 seed grows to 500-2000 reviewed records this way. Two-lane pipeline diagram: LLM Lane (Generate) flows Corpus Doc -> LLM Generates Q-A Pair -> Pair Candidate. Bridge arrow down. Human Lane (Review) flows Pair Candidate -> Reviewer Accept/Edit/Reject -> Golden Record. Caution: never deploy without human review - the LLM can encode its own bias, generating good test cases for itself while failing on adjacent phrasings. Acceptance rate matters; so does reviewer diversity.
summary: Two-lane pipeline where an LLM generates candidate Q-A pairs grounded in corpus docs and a human reviewer accepts, edits, or rejects each, scaling 30-100 to 500-2000 records.
queries: synthetic eval data with human review
acceptance rate of synthetic eval data
human in the loop eval generation
danger of LLM generating its own tests
LLM bias in synthetic test cases
growing eval set without losing quality

text: Monthly review cadence: archive obsolete, add new. Like code, golden sets rot. Monthly review keeps the set defensible: spot-check 20 cases, archive anything tied to deleted docs or changed product behavior, add new cases captured from the past month's production incidents. Each month: spot-check 20 random cases, archive obsolete to /archived/ (audit-kept), add new from production. Quarterly audit. Re-run regression to confirm green. Three health metrics track set quality: Coverage = query types covered / total query types; Freshness = median age of golden cases in months; Pass-Rate = % of golden set currently green on production pipeline.
summary: Monthly cadence spot-checks 20 cases, archives obsolete, adds new; coverage, freshness, and pass-rate measure set health.
queries: how often to review golden dataset
how to know golden set is stale

text: Shadow-eval pipeline diagram. A user query node splits into two parallel pipelines: an Old Pipeline (served to user) on the left and a New Pipeline (logged, not served) on the right. Both answers flow down into an LLM-Judge Rubric node at the bottom, which scores them side by side. Production traffic feeds both pipelines simultaneously; only the old pipeline's answer reaches the user. The judge produces an aggregate comparison (e.g., new preferred 62%, tie 23%, regress 15%) without exposing users to the candidate pipeline.
summary: Diagram shows a user query feeding both an old served pipeline and a new logged-only pipeline into an LLM judge for safe comparison.
queries: old pipeline new pipeline diagram RAG
LLM judge comparing two pipelines diagram
what does shadow deploy look like

how shadow eval is wired up

text: Closing-the-loop cycle diagram with four nodes connected by clockwise arrows. (1) Online Finds A Regression - thumbs-down spike on a query type. (2) Capture To Golden Regression Set - tie the failing case into the regression set from chapter 23.4. (3) Offline Reproduces; Iterate Fix - RAGAS + judge re-score the offending case until green. (4) Shadow + A/B Verify Fix In Production - promote only after both confirm no regression. The cycle returns to online detection, illustrating the offline-online feedback loop that production-grade RAG eval relies on.
summary: Four-node cycle diagram showing online regression detection feeding the golden set, offline fix, then shadow+A/B verification, looping back to online.
queries: closing the loop diagram RAG eval
online detection to golden set diagram

text: Online eval and A/B testing for production RAG. Offline gates merges; online catches regressions users hit. Implicit signals (thumbs, dwell time, copy-paste rate, follow-up rephrase) combine 3+ at a time into a composite score; explicit feedback (rating widget 2-8% capture, free-text) gives high signal per sample with PII redaction under GDPR/CCPA. Shadow eval logs a candidate pipeline alongside production and uses an LLM judge for apples-to-apples comparison at zero user risk. A/B testing splits traffic 50/50, samples ~200 queries/day per bucket, judges with a rubric, targets p < 0.05 over 7-14 days, with guardrails and auto-rollback on faithfulness drops or refusal spikes. The loop: online finds regression -> capture to golden set -> offline reproduces and fixes -> shadow + A/B verify.
summary: Chapter overview of online RAG eval: implicit and explicit feedback, shadow eval, rubric-judged A/B with guardrails, all looped back through the golden regression set.
queries: shadow eval A/B test guardrails summary
how production RAG quality is measured
how to ship a new RAG pipeline safely
shadow mode then ab test then promote
rating widget capture rate and PII

text: Why online eval in addition to offline. Offline eval gates merges. Online eval detects regressions production users will hit but the golden set cannot anticipate. You need both. Offline Eval: controlled and reproducible across runs; fast iteration with the same golden set every time; does not reflect production query distribution; misses rare-but-bad cases users actually hit. Online Eval: real query distribution from real users; catches edge cases offline never sees; noisy signal that needs aggregation over many sessions; privacy concerns when storing user feedback. Use both: offline gates merges before deploy, online catches regressions after deploy and feeds them back into the golden regression set.
summary: Offline eval gates pre-deploy merges while online eval catches post-deploy regressions on real traffic; production needs both.
queries: why do I need online eval if I already have offline eval
offline vs online evaluation for RAG
pros and cons of online evaluation
is golden set enough to test RAG
real user query distribution online eval
privacy concerns storing user feedback eval
gates merges before deploy vs catches regressions after deploy
edge cases offline eval cannot see
why production query distribution matters for eval
should I use only offline RAG eval

text: Implicit signals: what users tell you without clicking anything. Implicit signals are behavioral traces from session telemetry. Each is individually noisy; combining three or more makes a reliable quality regression detector. Thumbs Up / Down: explicit-light, most users do not click. Dwell Time: long dwell means read carefully, very short means likely irrelevant. Copy-Paste Rate: user copied the answer = useful enough to reuse. Follow-Up Rephrase: user re-asks same intent with different words = previous answer failed. Combine 3+ signals and calibrate against explicit feedback. Any single signal is too noisy alone; a composite quality score over 3+ implicit signals tracks user satisfaction reliably.
summary: Implicit behavioral signals like dwell time, copy-paste, and rephrase combined 3+ at a time form a reliable RAG quality detector.
queries: what are implicit user signals in RAG
how to measure RAG quality without explicit feedback
thumbs up thumbs down click rate
combining noisy signals into composite quality score
how many implicit signals needed for reliable detection
why is one signal not enough

text: Explicit feedback: ask directly. Explicit feedback (a rating widget or a free-text 'what went wrong?' prompt) is the highest signal per sample and the lowest volume. Use it for diagnosis, not for primary quality measurement alone. Rating Widget: post-answer thumbs / star rating / did-this-help yes/no. Capture rate: 2-8% of sessions. Free-Text Feedback: 'Tell us what went wrong.' Higher signal, much lower volume. Single best source of qualitative failure modes. Privacy: redact PII before storage. Store feedback with conversation context but redact PII (names, emails, account numbers) before retention. Follow regional privacy law: GDPR, CCPA. Document retention period.
summary: Explicit ratings and free-text feedback give the highest per-sample signal at low volume; useful for diagnosis with PII redaction under GDPR/CCPA.
queries: thumbs up star rating capture rate 2-8%
did this help yes no widget
regional privacy law for LLM logs
tell us what went wrong prompt
should I use explicit feedback alone

text: Shadow eval: run new pipeline alongside old. Shadow eval runs a new candidate pipeline alongside the production one without serving its answers. The judge compares both on real production traffic, giving an apples-to-apples signal at zero user risk. Pipeline: a user query goes to both the Old Pipeline (served to user) and the New Pipeline (logged, not served); both feed into an LLM-Judge Rubric that scores them. Shadow Eval Week 1 Result: judge prefers new in 62% of cases, ties in 23%, regresses in 15%. Production traffic provides the comparison; users never see the candidate answer. This is the signal that graduates the new pipeline to A/B.
summary: Shadow eval logs a candidate pipeline's answers in parallel with the served one and uses an LLM judge to compare both at zero user risk.
queries: run new pipeline without serving to users
LLM judge compare old new pipeline
when to graduate from shadow to A/B
how does shadow deploy work for RAG

text: A/B: split traffic, measure with rubric. Once shadow looks good, ship A/B. Split production users 50/50; sample answers from each bucket; score with the same judge rubric. Mean rubric score per bucket plus a significance test decides which wins. Steps: (1) Bucket users 50/50 (control/treatment). (2) Sample 200 queries per day per bucket. (3) Run LLM-judge rubric on every sampled (query, answer). (4) Compute mean rubric score per bucket; check significance. Statistical Significance: at N=200/day per bucket and effect size 0.1 on a 1-5 scale, expect 7-14 days to reach p < 0.05. Do not peek; pre-register the analysis window. Guardrails & Auto-Rollback: monitor faithfulness drop and refusal-rate spike at high frequency. Auto-rollback when any guardrail breaches threshold; don't wait for the analysis window.
summary: A/B testing splits production 50/50, samples 200 queries per bucket per day, judges with the same rubric, and uses guardrails with auto-rollback alongside p<0.05 significance testing.
queries: how to A/B test a RAG pipeline
200 queries per day per bucket sample size
how long does A/B test take for RAG
faithfulness drop refusal rate spike monitoring
do not peek at A/B results
ab test 7 to 14 days reach significance

text: Closing the loop: online -> offline -> online. Production-grade RAG eval is a feedback cycle. Online detects regressions; offline reproduces and fixes them; shadow plus A/B verify the fix; the cycle loops. The offline tools (RAGAS, judge, golden) and the online tools (implicit, explicit, shadow, A/B) are two halves of one system. Loop nodes: (1) Online finds a regression - thumbs-down spike on a query type. (2) Capture to golden regression set - tie the failing case into chapter 23.4. (3) Offline reproduces; iterate fix - RAGAS + judge re-score the offending case until green. (4) Shadow + A/B verify fix in production - promote only after both confirm no regression. Two halves, one system: offline gives reproducibility; online gives reality. The loop turns every production regression into a future regression-set entry, so the same bug cannot ship twice.
summary: Online detection feeds the golden regression set, offline reproduces and fixes, then shadow plus A/B verify - a closed loop preventing the same bug from shipping twice.
queries: prevent same bug from shipping twice

text: Semantic cache decision flow diagram. Incoming query 'Reset my password?' -> embed query (1024-dim vector) -> cosine search in cache store comparing vs all cached queries -> diamond decision 'Score >= 0.93?' threshold -> YES branch returns cached answer (cost $0, no LLM call) -> NO branch runs full RAG and stores answer (cost full stack).
summary: Diagram shows the semantic cache decision flow: embed, cosine search, threshold check at 0.93, then return cached answer or fall through to full RAG.
queries: how is a cached answer returned
store new answer cache miss flow

text: Horizontal bar chart comparing per-query cost across four cache configurations: no cache $0.0173 (0% saved); prompt cache only at 80% hit rate $0.0044 (74% saved); semantic cache only at 30% hit rate $0.0121 (30% saved); both stacked $0.003 (83% saved). Stacked configuration is the production target and delivers the lowest cost.
summary: Bar chart of per-query cost across four cache configurations showing stacked prompt+semantic cache delivers the lowest cost at $0.003 (83% saved).
queries: compare no cache vs caching cost
80 percent prompt cache hit rate cost
30 percent semantic cache hit rate cost

text: Caching in RAG: prompt cache stores the static system+retrieved-doc prefix at a 90% discount with byte-exact match (works for stable prefixes, fails when docs change per query). Semantic cache stores (query, answer) pairs and serves hits when cosine similarity to a cached query is at or above a threshold like 0.93. Operations: LRU+TTL eviction, doc-ID tagged invalidation, high thresholds and held-out monitoring to control false hits. Stacked together: 83% per-query cost savings vs no cache. Production RAG systems that skip caching leave 50%+ of cost on the table.
summary: Prompt cache discounts a stable prefix and semantic cache reuses similar prior answers; stacked they cut RAG per-query cost by ~83%.
queries: summary of prompt and semantic cache
how to cut RAG cost with caching
what is the safety knob semantic cache

text: Why cache in RAG. Every production RAG query burns four cost lines: embedding ($0.0001), retrieval ($0.0002), LLM input for 4k tokens ($0.012), LLM output for 500 tokens ($0.005). Total per query = $0.0173. At 1k QPS over a day this stacks to ~$1,500/day. Caching cuts the bill 50-95% on repeated traffic.
summary: Production RAG queries cost ~$0.0173 each across four cost lines and scale to $1,500/day at 1k QPS, motivating caching.
queries: how much does a RAG query cost
what are the four RAG cost lines
LLM output token cost in RAG
do I need caching in RAG

text: Prompt cache: cache the static prefix. Provider-side prompt caching (Anthropic-style) keeps the system prompt and retrieved-context prefix on the inference server. Subsequent calls reuse it at a 90% discount as long as the prefix bytes match exactly. TTL ~5 minutes. The artifact has two regions: cached prefix (system prompt + retrieved docs) at 90% discount, and fresh suffix (user question + assistant) at full price. Cost comparison: full price no cache, 4000 input tokens at $3/1M = $0.012; with prompt cache, 3800 cached at $0.30/1M + 200 fresh at $3/1M = $0.00174 = 85% saved.
summary: Prompt cache stores the static system+retrieved-doc prefix on the inference server at a 90% discount with byte-exact match, saving ~85% on input tokens.
queries: how long does prompt cache last

text: When prompt cache applies. Prompt cache only wins when many requests share a long prefix. Cache HITS: support agent looping on a stable doc set; multi-turn conversation with same retrieved docs; RAG pipeline answering many queries with a stable system prompt. Cache MISSES: docs change per query (no shared prefix); query-time chunking (different chunks every time); cold-start every conversation.
summary: Prompt cache hits when many requests share a long stable prefix and misses when retrieved context or chunks change per query.
queries: does prompt cache work for changing docs
why is my prompt cache not hitting
when not to use prompt cache

text: Semantic cache: cache by query similarity. A semantic cache stores past (query, answer) pairs and serves a hit when an incoming query is similar enough to one already cached. Similarity is cosine on the query embedding; threshold is the safety knob (e.g. 0.93). Flow: incoming query -> embed query to 1024-dim vector -> cosine search in cache store vs all cached queries -> if score >= 0.93 return cached answer ($0 cost, no LLM call) -> else run full RAG, store answer (full cost). Example cosine scores vs cached query 'How do I reset my password?': 'How can I reset my password?' score 0.97 HIT; 'Why is my dashboard slow?' score 0.34 MISS; 'How do I change my password?' score 0.88 BORDERLINE.
summary: Semantic cache embeds incoming queries and serves cached answers when cosine similarity to a cached query crosses the threshold, skipping the LLM call.

text: Semantic cache operations: eviction, invalidation, false-hit risk. Eviction: LRU + TTL. Cache fills up; evict oldest unused entries; TTL forces refresh of any answer older than e.g. 24 hours. Invalidation: when the corpus updates (a doc changes), every cached answer that touched that doc is stale. Strategy: tag cache entries with doc-IDs and invalidate on doc update. False-hit risk: two queries with cosine 0.95 can still want different answers. Example: 'Cancel my subscription' vs 'Cancel my trial' - similar embedding, very different policy. Tune threshold high (0.95+) for safety; monitor false-hit rate on a held-out set.
summary: Semantic cache needs LRU+TTL eviction, doc-ID tagged invalidation on corpus updates, and high thresholds plus held-out monitoring to limit false hits.
queries: cancel subscription vs cancel trial cache
tag cache entries with doc id

text: Combined impact: prompt cache + semantic cache compound. Per-query cost for the same RAG pipeline under four cache configurations: no cache $0.0173 (0% saved); prompt cache only at 80% hit rate $0.0044 (74% saved); semantic cache only at 30% hit rate $0.0121 (30% saved); both stacked $0.003 (83% saved). Stacked $0.003/query = 83% savings, compounds prompt cache with semantic cache. Apply both. Tune thresholds. Monitor false-hit rate. Most production RAG systems leave 50%+ of cost on the table by skipping caching.

text: A production RAG query bill is the sum of five cost lines, walked in order of typical magnitude. LLM output tokens: ~$15 per 1M tokens, about $0.0075 per query (500 output tokens, Claude Sonnet typical). LLM input tokens: ~$3 per 1M tokens, about $0.012 per query (4000 input tokens for system + retrieved + question). Reranker: ~$0.001 per query (cross-encoder, optional). Vector search: ~$0.0002 per query (operator cost, managed service). Embedding: ~$0.0001 per query (1024-dim, modern provider). Total per query = $0.0208. The biggest cost driver - LLM tokens - dominates and is visible first.

text: Five real levers to cut RAG cost, in order of compounding return. Smaller embedding: 1024 -> 384 dim with Matryoshka, -60% storage, no quality drop, small impact (embedding <1% of bill). Fewer chunks retrieved: top-20 -> top-5 to the LLM, -75% input tokens, ~44% of total bill. Smaller LLM: Sonnet -> Haiku, ~10x cheaper per token, ~80% quality on most RAG tasks, ~80% of LLM bill. Prompt cache: 80% prefix hit rate, prefix tokens at 90% discount, ~74% on cached prefix tokens. Semantic cache: 30% hit rate on hot queries, skip LLM entirely on hits, ~30% on affected queries. Apply in order: cache first (free quality), reduce chunks (free quality), then downsize LLM (quality tradeoff).
summary: Five levers cut RAG cost: smaller embedding, fewer chunks, smaller LLM, prompt cache, semantic cache - apply caches first since they are free quality.

text: Worked example stacking all five levers in sequence as a downward waterfall. Start - full price $0.0208. Apply prompt cache (80% hit): $0.0061, -71% on input tokens. Apply semantic cache (30% hit): $0.0043, -30% blended. Reduce top-k from 20 to 5: $0.0028, -35% remaining input. Switch LLM to Haiku: $0.0012, -57% on LLM bill. Final per-query cost $0.0012 = 94% reduction. At 1k QPS this saves about $1.69M per day versus no levers applied.
summary: Stacking five cost levers in sequence drops per-query cost from $0.0208 to $0.0012 (94% reduction, $1.69M/day saved at 1k QPS).

text: Chapter 12.38 teaches why and how to instrument production RAG with traces. Without per-stage instrumentation, four failure modes are undiagnosable: mystery slowness, silent quality drops, cost blowups, and hallucination hunts. A canonical RAG trace has one root span and five children (embed query, vector search, rerank, pack prompt, generate), each carrying attributes like model versions, doc-IDs, scores, token counts, and per-stage latency. Tool options include LangSmith, Helicone, OpenTelemetry, Arize Phoenix, and native provider tooling. Privacy demands hashing raw query text and logging doc-IDs not chunk text to satisfy GDPR/HIPAA/SOC2. A production dashboard surfaces P50/P99 latency, faithfulness, cost-per-query, the slowest trace, and signal-level facts like model version.
summary: Production RAG observability: span trees per query, per-stage attributes, tool landscape, privacy rules, and dashboard design.

text: Why trace RAG in production: a RAG system is opaque without traces. Four failure modes recur and each needs per-stage instrumentation to diagnose. (1) Mystery Slowness: user reports the system is slow; without per-stage latency you cannot tell whether retrieval or the LLM is the bottleneck. (2) Silent Quality Drop: faithfulness drops 5% week-over-week; no doc-IDs logged means you cannot see which docs were retrieved. (3) Cost Blowup: bill doubles; no per-query token count means you cannot tell which queries pulled in too much context. (4) Hallucination Hunt: user flags a bad answer; no model-version logged so you cannot reproduce the failure.
summary: Four production failure modes that prove RAG must be traced per stage: mystery slowness, silent quality drop, cost blowup, hallucination hunt.

text: The canonical RAG trace span tree: a single user query produces one root span (Query, total 180ms) and five child spans rendered with widths proportional to latency. Embed Query 12ms (model = bge-large-v1.5, dim = 1024). Vector Search 28ms (top-K = 20, index = hnsw, metric = cosine). Rerank 45ms (model = bge-reranker-v2, input = 20, output = 5). Pack Prompt 3ms (chunks packed = 5, total tokens = 4128). Generate 92ms (model = Sonnet 4.7, out tokens = 480, finish = stop). Rerank dominates wall time after Generate; optimization targets reducing input top-k or picking a faster reranker.
summary: Canonical RAG span tree with five child spans whose widths are proportional to per-stage latency, exposing the bottleneck visually.

text: Per-stage attributes to log on every RAG trace. Embed: query_text (hashed), embedding_model_version, dim, latency_ms. Vector Search: top_k_requested, index_name, distance_metric, retrieved_doc_ids, retrieval_scores, latency_ms. Rerank: reranker_model_version, input_count, output_count, reranker_scores, latency_ms. Pack: chunk_count_packed, total_prompt_tokens, citation_count, latency_ms. Generate: llm_model_version, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, finish_reason, latency_ms. Variable identifiers stay lowercase per code convention; column headers stay title-case. This is the minimum every production RAG trace MUST emit.

text: Tools that capture RAG traces in 2025. LangSmith: LangChain's official tracer; strong for LangChain users; tight integration with LangChain stack. Helicone: provider-agnostic; logs every LLM call with cost / latency dashboards; lightweight setup. OpenTelemetry: open standard; vendor-neutral spans; export to Datadog / Grafana / Jaeger / custom. Arize Phoenix: open-source; built for LLM eval + trace; strong dashboard for retrieval quality. Native Provider Tooling: Anthropic Console, OpenAI Dashboard; limited but free; first-party cost tracking. Choices span framework-native to vendor-neutral.
summary: Five tool options for capturing RAG traces, from framework-native LangSmith to vendor-neutral OpenTelemetry.

text: Privacy: what NOT to log in plain text. Regulations (GDPR, HIPAA, SOC2) and contracts may forbid storing user query content. Use one-way hashes for queries; log doc-IDs not chunk text; redact PII upstream. Log freely: doc-IDs, retrieval scores, latency per stage, model version, token counts, embedding-model version, query length, anonymized user ID, response status code. Do NOT log in plain text: raw query text (hash it), raw retrieved chunks (log only IDs), PII inside the query, user account IDs (use opaque session IDs), API keys, prompt template contents that include secrets.
summary: Privacy rules for RAG telemetry: hash queries, log doc-IDs not chunks, redact PII, and never log secrets or account IDs in plain text.
queries: what not to log in RAG

text: A production RAG trace dashboard surfaces four headline tiles plus the most recent slow trace and three signal-level facts. Tiles: P50 Latency 180ms, P99 Latency 620ms, Faithfulness 0.91, Cost Per Query $0.012. Slowest trace last hour: 620ms with the span tree thumbnail showing each stage's contribution. Footer facts: Slowest Stage = Rerank (45ms P50), Top Retrieved Doc = Doc-7 (Password Reset), Model Version = Sonnet 4.7. A trace is the single source of truth when a user complains - every stage, every score, every model version.

text: Chapter 23.9 covers hallucination detection and drift monitoring for production RAG. Four hallucination signals (faithfulness score, citation coverage, refusal rate, out-of-index mentions) catch most issues automatically. Four drift types (data, embedding, eval, distribution) each have distinct detection mechanisms. A 5-step per-generation pipeline extracts claims, judges support, computes faithfulness = supported/total, logs to trace, and alerts when the rolling 7-day median drops below 0.85. Drift signals plotted over 30 days cross thresholds on different days - faithfulness day 28, recall day 22, refusal rate day 25. Useful alerts always carry failing query examples, scores, cited docs, unsupported claims, and a recommended action. The full operator dashboard combines trend thumbnails, drift-type status badges, and top hallucinated queries on one panel.

text: Four production signals catch most hallucinations without manual review. Wire each into the trace, alert on rolling medians, investigate any spike inside the same shift. (1) Faithfulness Score: per-generation faithfulness from LLM-as-judge; alert if rolling 7-day median drops below 0.85. (2) Citation Coverage: percent of claims with a [doc-X] citation; alert if below 80%. (3) Refusal Rate: percent of queries refused with 'I don't know'; alert on spike (too cautious) or dip (hallucinating when context was sufficient). (4) Out-of-Index Mentions: regex or LLM check for entities mentioned that do not appear in any retrieved doc - a hard signal of hallucination.
summary: Four production signals (faithfulness, citation coverage, refusal rate, out-of-index mentions) detect most RAG hallucinations automatically.

text: Drift is the slow leak before the flood. Four kinds of drift afflict production RAG, each with a different detection mechanism and fix. (1) Data Drift: corpus changes (new docs added, old removed); embedding index becomes stale relative to current corpus. Detect via re-index timestamp vs current doc set checksum. (2) Embedding Drift: you re-embed with a new model; old vectors and new vectors are not directly comparable; mixed-version retrievals pollute top-K. Detect by logging embedding_model_version per query and alerting on mixed-version retrievals. (3) Eval Drift: LLM-as-judge model is upgraded; faithfulness scores from old vs new judge are not directly comparable. Detect by locking judge version; rerun calibration on judge change. (4) Distribution Drift: user query distribution shifts (new product launched, new failure mode); old query embeddings cluster differently. Detect via cluster monitoring on query embeddings over time.
summary: The four drift types in production RAG - data, embedding, eval, and distribution - each with a distinct detection method.

text: A 5-step hallucination detection pipeline runs per-generation. Input: user query + retrieved chunks + generated answer. Step 1: Extract Claims - LLM segments the answer into atomic claims. Step 2: Check Each Claim Against Retrieved Chunks - LLM-as-judge labels each claim supported or unsupported. Step 3: Compute Faithfulness Score = supported_claims / total_claims. Step 4: Log Score + Doc-IDs + Unsupported Claims - emit to trace. Step 5: Alert If Rolling Median < 0.85 - page owner on drop. Worked example: 5 claims, 4 supported, 1 hallucinated -> faithfulness = 4/5 = 0.80 -> below 0.85 threshold -> ALERT.
summary: The 5-step per-generation pipeline - extract claims, judge each, compute faithfulness, log, alert - with a worked 4/5 = 0.80 example.

text: An alert without examples is a blank page at 3am. Always include the failing queries, the failing scores, and a hint at the recommended action so the on-call engineer can start fixing immediately. Alert payload example: ALERT rag.faithfulness.drop. Severity WARNING. Trigger: 7-day median faithfulness dropped below 0.85 (currently 0.82). First crossed: 2026-05-14 03:21 UTC. Affected: production customer support pipeline. Recent examples last 24h - Query 'How do I reset my password if I forgot my email?' faithfulness 0.60, cited docs doc-1, doc-3, unsupported claim 'Support team will email a reset link.' Query 'Cancel my subscription and get a refund' faithfulness 0.50, cited doc-4, unsupported claim 'Refunds are issued within 24 hours.' Recommended action: review recent corpus updates and re-run golden eval.
summary: What a useful drift alert payload contains - trigger, severity, failing query examples with scores, cited docs, unsupported claims, and recommended action.

text: Six framework options for RAG in 2025. No Framework: raw SDK (Anthropic / OpenAI / Cohere) plus simple Python or TypeScript orchestration; most popular for new production RAG in 2025. LlamaIndex: RAG-focused framework with good defaults for retrieval; less abstracted than LangChain. LangChain: the original; large ecosystem; abstractions evolving and some deprecated within the framework itself. LangGraph: graph-based agent orchestration from the LangChain team; newer; explicit state machine. Haystack: enterprise-leaning; modular pipelines; smaller community than LangChain. Vendor SDK: OpenAI Agents, Anthropic native tool-use; tight integration; vendor lock-in. Brand names preserve official capitalization (LlamaIndex, LangChain).

text: Framework decision matrix: five criteria across six options. Lock-In Risk: No Framework None, LlamaIndex Low, LangChain Medium, LangGraph Medium, Haystack Low, Vendor SDK High. API Churn: No Framework None, LlamaIndex Low, LangChain High, LangGraph Medium, Haystack Low, Vendor SDK Low. Complexity (build / hidden): No Framework High build / Low hidden, LlamaIndex Low build / Medium hidden, LangChain Low build / High hidden, LangGraph Medium build / Medium hidden, Haystack Medium build / Medium hidden, Vendor SDK Low build / High hidden. Community Size: No Framework Huge, LlamaIndex Large, LangChain Huge, LangGraph Growing, Haystack Medium, Vendor SDK Vendor-Specific. RAG-Specific Fit: No Framework You Build, LlamaIndex Strong, LangChain OK, LangGraph OK, Haystack Strong, Vendor SDK OK.

text: An honest take on LangChain. LangChain is the dominant framework in 2025 but the picture is mixed. Pros: largest ecosystem; biggest community; tutorials everywhere; prototype in 30 minutes; integrations for every vector DB, embedding model, and LLM. Cons: abstractions hide what is actually happening (lost-in-middle invisible until you trace); some abstractions deprecated within the framework itself (Stuff / MapReduce / Refine, original AgentExecutor); fast-moving API churn requires upgrade cycles; framework lock-in concerns once production code depends on chains. LangChain is still actively developed and dominant for prototyping. For production-grade RAG, many teams move to raw SDK plus LlamaIndex retriever, or stay on LangChain but carefully avoid deprecating abstractions.
summary: Honest pros and cons of LangChain: dominant ecosystem and fast prototyping versus hidden abstractions, deprecated pieces, API churn, and lock-in.
queries: alternative to LangChain for production RAG

text: When each framework fits. No Framework: small team, full control needed, production-grade RAG with custom orchestration; fits 2-3 engineers building a single product. LlamaIndex: RAG-heavy product, want good retrieval defaults, accept some framework dependency; fits teams shipping a RAG-centric app. LangChain: early prototyping, large team needs shared vocabulary, ecosystem integrations matter more than minimalism; fits teams that staff for framework upgrades. LangGraph: explicit agent state machine, multi-step workflows, less hidden magic than LangChain; fits teams building structured agents. Haystack: enterprise / regulated environment, modular pipelines, smaller-community tradeoff is acceptable; fits orgs with security review on every dependency. Vendor SDK: committed to one provider, lock-in is acceptable, tight integration is the priority; fits internal Anthropic / OpenAI shops.
summary: Team and product profiles that best fit each framework, from 2-3 engineer No Framework setups to vendor-locked Anthropic/OpenAI shops.

text: Framework decision tree with three questions. Root: Is RAG the primary product feature? YES branch asks: Mid-sized team with full control (3+ engineers)? YES leaf: No Framework plus LlamaIndex retriever. NO leaf: LlamaIndex. NO branch asks: Is it a multi-step agent (tool use, loops, workflows)? YES asks: Want explicit state? YES leaf: LangGraph. NO leaf: LangChain. NO asks: Single-provider OK? YES leaf: Vendor SDK. NO leaf: No Framework. Three questions, six leaves, one choice. Anything more complex than this is over-thinking the framework decision.
summary: 3-question decision tree branching on RAG-primary, team size, agent complexity, state explicitness, and provider lock-in to land on one of six framework leaves.

text: What stays the same regardless of framework. Every framework wraps the same six core decisions: (1) chunking strategy (20.4 - 20.10) is a data-pipeline decision, not a framework decision; (2) embedding model choice (21.1) is a data-pipeline decision; (3) hybrid plus reranker cascade (21.3, 21.4) is a retrieval-quality decision; (4) prompt template plus context packing (22.1) is a generation decision; (5) eval (23.1 - 23.5) is a measurement decision; (6) tracing (23.8) is an ops decision. Frameworks are wrappers. Get these decisions right and the framework is replaceable. Production RAG code should be thin enough that a framework swap is a 2-day job, not a 2-month rewrite.
summary: Six framework-agnostic core decisions (chunking, embedding, hybrid+reranker, prompt+packing, eval, tracing) that make any framework swap a 2-day job.
