chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Chapter 23.11 is the capstone of Section 23 (RAG in Production). It introduces a nine-row decision framework covering every choice in production RAG: (1) Should I use RAG, (2) Chunking, (3) Embedding + Index, (4) Query transformation, (5) Context packing + Generation, (6) Advanced retrieval, (7) Evaluation, (8) Production operations, (9) Framework choice. The framework is then walked end-to-end on a new capstone use case: Q&A over case law for a legal research firm with 200,000 published court cases across 50 US jurisdictions, partner-lawyer >= 4/5 quality bar, 5-10 second latency budget, $0.05 per query cost budget, and mandatory citations + jurisdiction filter. The chosen stack: Hierarchical + Contextual chunking; domain-adapted Legal-BERT embedding; HNSW + BM25 hybrid with jurisdiction metadata; multi-query expansion + decomposition (HyDE skipped); 16-30k token sandwich packing with mandatory citations; GraphRAG + multi-hop for citation queries (long-context skipped); partner-curated golden set of 500 pairs + LLM-as-judge + RAGAS + online A/B; prompt cache enabled, semantic cache disabled, full tracing, legal-fact-check post-process. Framework choice: No Framework + LlamaIndex retriever + raw Anthropic SDK. Result: ~$0.04 per query, ~6 second latency, every answer cited.
summary: Capstone chapter that walks the full nine-decision RAG framework on a legal case-law Q&A use case end to end.

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Capstone Use Case: Q&A Over Case Law. Customer is a legal research firm. They need a RAG system for searching across published court cases, a fully-different domain from a customer support corpus. Design Brief: Corpus = 200,000 published court cases across 50 US jurisdictions. Metadata = jurisdiction tag, year, citation tree. Quality Bar = partner-lawyer rating >= 4/5 on legal research rubric. Latency Budget = 5-10 seconds (legal research is patient). Cost Budget = $0.05 per query. Audit Requirements = every answer cited, jurisdiction filter mandatory. Example queries: 'What's the precedent for tortious interference in California state court?', 'Cases citing Smith v Jones about negligence in medical contexts', 'How has the standard for qualified immunity evolved 2010-2025?'. Every choice in the framework will point back to the chapter that taught it.

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 1: Chunking Strategy (Chapters 20.5, 20.6). Choice: Hierarchical Chunking (20.5) + Contextual Retrieval (20.6) - per-paragraph chunks with parent (case-level) metadata and an LLM-injected 'This paragraph is from Case X about Y' preamble. Why: Case opinions have natural hierarchy (intro, facts, reasoning, holding). Hierarchical preserves parent-child relationships. Contextual Retrieval injects the per-chunk preamble that disambiguates citation-level retrieval. Plain fixed-size chunking would mid-sentence-split a court's analysis. Tradeoff: More storage (each chunk now has parent metadata). Higher index build cost (contextual augmentation is an LLM call per chunk). Worth it because case-law precision demands citation-level retrieval.
summary: Picks hierarchical + contextual chunking for case opinions, accepting higher storage and indexing cost for citation-level precision.
queries: Best chunking strategy for legal documents
Why not fixed-size chunking for case law
Chunking court opinion intro facts reasoning holding
Per-paragraph chunk with parent case metadata

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 2: Embedding + Index (Chapters 21.2, 21.3, 21.4). Choice: Domain-adapted embedding (21.2) on top of a base Legal-BERT or Cohere Embed Multilingual. HNSW index with hybrid retrieval (BM25 + dense, 21.3). Jurisdiction stored as filter metadata. Cross-encoder cascade rerank (21.4). Why: General-purpose embeddings underweight legal jargon ('tortious', 'qualified immunity'). Hybrid catches both exact-citation lookups ('Smith v Jones') and semantic queries ('medical negligence cases'). Jurisdiction filter eliminates 96% of irrelevant cases before reranking. Cross-encoder lifts precision on legal-language matching. Tradeoff: Domain-adapt requires labeled training pairs (expensive but one-time). Cross-encoder adds 50-100ms per query. Latency budget allows it.
summary: Picks domain-adapted Legal-BERT embedding, HNSW hybrid BM25+dense, jurisdiction metadata filter, and cross-encoder rerank.
queries: Why hybrid retrieval for legal queries
Smith v Jones exact citation lookup
How much does jurisdiction filter eliminate

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 3: Query Transformation (Chapters 21.5, 21.6, 21.7, 21.8). Choice: Multi-Query Expansion (21.7) + Decomposition for complex multi-hop queries (21.8). HyDE (21.6) SKIPPED. Why: A query like 'Cases citing X about Y' naturally decomposes into 'find X' -> 'find cases citing X' -> 'filter for cases about Y'. Multi-Query rewriting (legal synonyms: 'negligence' / 'duty of care' / 'breach of duty') improves recall on lexically-narrow legal language. HyDE is skipped because case law is precise; fake hypothetical documents hurt more than help. Tradeoff: More retrievals per query (3-5x). Cost goes up linearly. Mitigated by aggressive deduplication after RRF merge.
summary: Picks multi-query expansion and decomposition, skips HyDE, accepts 3-5x retrieval cost mitigated by RRF dedup.

summary: Picks 16-30k token sandwich packing with mandatory [Case Name, Year] citations and explicit refusal clause, with full prompt template.

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 5: Advanced Retrieval Patterns (Chapters 22.4, 22.6, 22.7, 22.9). Choice: GraphRAG (22.7) for the case-citation graph (cases citing X / cases cited by X). Multi-Hop Retrieval (22.4) for 'cases citing X about Y' decomposition. Long-Context (22.9) SKIPPED. Why: Legal citation is a graph; GraphRAG indexes the relationships. Without it, 'cases citing X' requires querying every case in the corpus. With it, follow the citation edges. Multi-hop chains the steps for complex precedent queries. Long-context skipped because 200k cases exceeds any context window so RAG is mandatory. Tradeoff: GraphRAG indexing is 10x slower than vector-only. Re-indexing the citation graph on corpus update is a nightly batch. Multi-hop is slower per query but precision gain on multi-hop queries is large.
summary: Picks GraphRAG for the citation graph and multi-hop retrieval, skips long-context because 200k cases exceed any window.
queries: Cases citing X cases cited by X

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 6: Evaluation (Chapters 23.2, 23.3, 23.4, 23.5). Choice: LLM-as-Judge (23.2) with a legal-specific rubric. Golden Dataset (23.4) of 500 partner-curated query/expected-answer pairs. RAGAS metrics (23.3): faithfulness, citation precision, jurisdiction match. Online A/B (23.5) on partner-rated 4/5 score. Why: General-purpose RAG eval misses legal correctness. Partner-curated golden set is the ground truth because no automated metric reaches partner-quality. RAGAS faithfulness catches hallucinations. Citation precision (% of cited cases that are real and on-point) is critical. Tradeoff: Golden dataset is expensive to build (partner hours). 500 examples covers ~80% of query distribution; remaining 20% caught by online feedback. Rerun golden eval weekly on prod.
summary: Picks LLM-as-judge with legal rubric + 500-pair partner-curated golden set + RAGAS + online A/B, weekly reruns.
queries: Why partner curation for ground truth

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Decision 7: Production Operations (Chapters 23.6, 23.8, 23.9). Choice: Semantic cache DISABLED. Prompt cache ENABLED for system prompt + few-shot examples (23.6). Full tracing per query (23.8). Hallucination detection with legal-fact-check post-process (23.9). Drift monitoring on case-law updates. Why: Semantic cache fails when 'negligence in CA' and 'negligence in NY' cosine to 0.96 - the answers differ entirely. Prompt cache safe because system + few-shot are constant. Tracing every query is non-negotiable for audit. Tradeoff: No semantic cache = higher per-query cost. Mitigated by prompt cache. Drift monitoring catches when judicial reasoning shifts (rare but high-impact).
summary: Disables semantic cache (false-hit risk), enables prompt cache, requires full tracing, legal fact-check, and drift monitoring.
queries: Why disable semantic cache for legal RAG

chapterTitle: The Complete RAG Decision Framework + Capstone
text: Putting It All Together: The Capstone Stack. Five layers: 1. Ingest - hierarchical + contextual chunking; domain-adapted embedding; HNSW + BM25 hybrid index; jurisdiction metadata; case-citation graph index. 2. Retrieve - multi-query expansion; jurisdiction filter; hybrid retrieval; cross-encoder rerank; GraphRAG for citation queries. 3. Generate - 16-30k token budget; sandwich ordering; mandatory citations; I-don't-know refusal if no jurisdiction-matching cases. 4. Eval - partner-curated golden set (500 pairs); LLM-as-judge with legal rubric; RAGAS faithfulness; online A/B. 5. Ops - prompt cache (semantic cache disabled); full tracing per query; hallucination detection with legal-fact-check post-process; drift monitoring on case-law updates. Framework Choice (23.10): No Framework + LlamaIndex retriever for the RAG core + raw Anthropic SDK for generation. Cost ~$0.04 per query (under budget). Latency ~6 seconds. Every answer carries [Case Name, Year] citations. Every choice on this stack maps to a chapter you have worked through.
summary: Final five-layer capstone stack (ingest/retrieve/generate/eval/ops) with framework choice, $0.04 per query, 6s latency, full citations.

text: Chapter 24.1 Anatomy of an LLM Call. Breaks one LLM call into its parts: (1) Messages list with System/User/Assistant roles, sent in full each turn. (2) Tokens are the real unit - sub-word chunks billed and counted against the context window (e.g. 200k tokens ~ 150k words). (3) Sampling knobs - temperature, top-p (nucleus), top-k - shape the next-token probability distribution. (4) Generation ends on one of four stop conditions producing finish_reason values: length, stop_sequence, end_turn, tool_use. (5) Response object returns content, stop_reason, and usage with input_tokens + output_tokens. (6) The complete six-step cycle: build messages, set parameters, send request, stream tokens, reach stop reason, return final response.
summary: Chapter-level overview of all parts that make up a single LLM API call: messages, tokens, sampling, stops, response, and cycle.

text: Every LLM call is an ordered list of messages, each tagged with a role. You don't 'chat' with the model - you send the whole list and the model writes the next assistant turn. Three roles: System sets the role, rules, and tone of the assistant (e.g. 'You are Alice's customer-support assistant. Be concise. Cite docs when possible.'). User is the end-user's turn, the thing the model has to respond to (e.g. 'I can't log in - reset my password for alice@example.com.'). Assistant is the model's reply that goes back into the messages list for the next turn (e.g. 'I'll help you reset it. I just sent a one-time link to alice@example.com.'). Every later request includes the prior assistant replies, so the model always sees the full conversation.
summary: Introduces the three message roles (System, User, Assistant) that make up every LLM API call.

text: The model doesn't see characters or words - it sees tokens, sub-word chunks from the tokenizer. Both input and output are billed and limited in tokens. User message 'I can't log in - reset my password' is 9 tokens: I, can, 't, ' log', ' in', ' -', ' reset', ' my', ' password'. Assistant reply 'I'll help you reset it.' is 7 tokens: I, 'll, ' help', ' you', ' reset', ' it', '.'. Context window = total tokens across all messages (system + user + assistant history + new reply). A 200k context window holds roughly 150k English words. Cross it and the oldest messages get dropped or the call fails.
summary: Explains tokens as the unit of input/output and how the context window limits the total tokens per call.

text:
Three sampling knobs shape how the model picks the next token from its probability distribution. Lower = safer and more repeatable. Higher = more varied.

Temperature - sharpness of the distribution. Stops: 0.0 Deterministic, 0.3 Focused, 0.7 Default, 1.0 Creative, 2.0 Wild. At T=0 the model always picks the most likely next token. At T=2 unlikely tokens get a real chance.

Top-P (Nucleus) - keep tokens until cumulative probability hits P. Example distribution: reset p=0.42 cum=0.42, change p=0.21 cum=0.63, update p=0.16 cum=0.79, fix p=0.09 cum=0.88, recover p=0.05 cum=0.93, send p=0.04 cum=0.97, create p=0.03 cum=1.00. With top_p = 0.9, sample only from the smallest set whose probabilities add to 0.9. Here 4 tokens stay in, 3 are cut.

Top-K - keep the K most likely tokens. Candidates: reset 0.42, change 0.21, update 0.16, fix 0.09, recover 0.05 (kept); send 0.04, create 0.03 (cut). With top_k = 5, everything past the 5th most-likely token is discarded before sampling.
summary: Walks through temperature, top-p (nucleus), and top-k sampling with concrete probability distributions.

text:
One LLM call doesn't 'just stop' - it stops for a specific reason and the response tells you which. Four stop conditions and four finish reasons.

1. Max Tokens Reached - config: max_tokens: 1024. Hard ceiling on how many tokens the model is allowed to generate this call. finish_reason: length.

2. Stop Sequence Hit - config: stop: ['</answer>']. The model produced one of the configured stop strings. Useful for structured output. finish_reason: stop_sequence.

3. End-Of-Turn Token - model decides on its own. The model emitted its special end-of-turn token. The natural end of a reply. finish_reason: end_turn.

4. Tool Call Requested - only with tool use enabled. The model paused to ask for a tool. Generation halts so your code can run the tool. finish_reason: tool_use.

Always check finish_reason. A length stop means the answer got cut off mid-thought - retry with a higher max_tokens.
summary: Lists the four ways an LLM call can end and the matching finish_reason values to inspect in the response.

text:
Every single call your agent makes follows this exact six-step cycle:

1. Build Messages - combine system + history + new user turn into the messages list. Arrow: Roles assigned.
2. Set Parameters - pick temperature, top-p, top-k, max_tokens, stop. Arrow: Sampling locked.
3. Send Request - POST messages + params to the model endpoint. Arrow: HTTPS POST.
4. Tokens Stream Back - model emits one token at a time over the wire. Arrow: SSE chunks.
5. Stop Reason Reached - one of: end_turn, length, stop_sequence, tool_use. Arrow: end_turn fired.
6. Final Response - content + stop_reason + usage delivered to your app.

Typical latency: ~1.5s median for a 7-token response. Long replies stream tokens as they're generated. Messages in, parameters in, request out. Tokens stream back until a stop reason fires. Response object lands with content, stop_reason, and usage.
summary: Summarizes the full six-step lifecycle of one LLM call from building messages to receiving the final response.

text: Chapter 24.2 'System Prompts - The Role Contract' teaches that the system prompt is the contract the model re-reads every turn. It has four parts: persona (who am I, voice, role), capabilities (which tools and data are available), constraints (what must never be done), and output rules (format, citations, structure). The chapter walks through each part with a customer-support assistant example for Alice, lists four tools (search_kb, lookup_customer, process_refund, escalate_human), four 'never' rules (e.g. never promise refunds over $200 without escalation), four output rules (greet by name, cite KB, wrap tool calls in <thinking>, end with follow-up or escalation), and ends with a full ten-line system prompt template.
summary: Chapter-level summary of the four-part system prompt contract: persona, capabilities, constraints, output rules.

text: The system prompt is the contract. It is the constitution every later message is interpreted under. The model re-reads it on every single turn, so anything not in it does not exist for the agent. The contract has four moving parts: Persona (Who am I? What is my voice and role?), Capabilities (What tools and data am I allowed to use?), Constraints (What must I never say, promise, or do?), and Output Rules (How must my reply be formatted, cited, and structured?). Get all four right and the agent behaves predictably. Skip one and it goes off-script.
summary: The system prompt is a contract re-read every turn, made of four parts: persona, capabilities, constraints, output rules.

text: Persona answers 'who am I, what is my voice, and what is my role?' A vague persona produces a generic chatbot. A sharp persona produces an assistant that sounds like it works at your company. BEFORE - VAGUE: 'You are a chatbot.' No role, no voice, no domain - the model fills the gap with a generic helpful tone. AFTER - PERSONA-SPECIFIC: 'You are Alice's customer-support assistant for the customer support knowledge base. You are friendly, concise, and you never invent policies.' Names the user (Alice), names the role (customer-support assistant), names the voice (friendly, concise), and one guardrail (no invented policies). Three rules of thumb: name the user, name the role, name one thing the assistant must never do.
summary: Persona names who the agent is, its voice, and role - vague vs sharp persona examples for Alice's support assistant.

text: Capabilities tell the model which tools are available, what each one does, and when to use it. Without this section the model either refuses or hallucinates tool calls. The four tools for the support agent: search_kb (searches the customer support knowledge base at docs.example.com), lookup_customer (fetches the profile for a customer id like c-9924), process_refund (issues a refund up to a configured dollar limit), escalate_human (hands the conversation off to a human support agent). Inline declaration: 'Tools available to you: search_kb, lookup_customer, process_refund, escalate_human.' The system prompt declares the tools. The API schema (chapter 25.2) is what actually wires them up. Both sides have to agree.
summary: Capabilities list available tools (search_kb, lookup_customer, process_refund, escalate_human) so the model neither refuses nor hallucinates calls.

text: Constraints are the negative-space rules. Every refund-gone-wrong, every leaked customer record, every invented policy traces back to a missing line here. Be explicit about the 'never's. The four NEVER constraints for the support agent: Never Promise Refunds Over $200 Without Escalation, Never Share Another Customer's Data, Never Claim Policies Not In The Knowledge Base, Never Call Tools Without First Looking Up The Customer. The $200 line and the escalation line live in the system prompt, not in code. The model checks them on every reply, so a policy change is a one-line edit, not a deploy.
summary: Constraints are explicit 'never' rules (e.g. no refunds over $200 without escalation) enforced every turn without redeploys.
queries: what are constraints in a system prompt

how to prevent leaked customer records

text: Output rules shape the surface of every reply: tone, structure, citations, and what each turn must end with. They are what makes the assistant feel consistent across thousands of conversations. The four numbered output rules: (1) Always Greet The Customer By Name, (2) Cite The Knowledge Base Doc When Quoting Policy, (3) Wrap Tool Calls In <thinking> Tags First, (4) End With A Follow-Up Question Or Escalation Offer. Citations and follow-up offers are not polish - they are how you keep the agent honest and the conversation moving. Without them, replies dead-end and trust erodes.
summary: Output rules dictate greeting, citations, <thinking> wrapping, and ending each reply with a follow-up or escalation.

how to format LLM replies consistently
cite knowledge base doc when quoting policy
wrap tool calls in thinking tags
escalation offer at end of reply

text:
All four parts woven into one artifact. This is exactly what gets sent as the system message on every turn for the customer-support agent. PROMPT TEMPLATE:

You are Alice's customer-support assistant for the customer
support knowledge base at docs.example.com. You are friendly,
concise, and you never invent policies.

Tools available to you:
  search_kb, lookup_customer, process_refund, escalate_human.
Always call lookup_customer (e.g. c-9924) before any other tool.

Never promise refunds over $200 without escalating to a human.
Never share another customer's data. Never claim a policy that
is not in the knowledge base.

Always greet the customer by name. Cite the knowledge base doc when quoting
policy. Wrap any tool call in a <thinking> block first. End every
reply with a follow-up question or an escalation offer.

Ten lines, four parts, zero ambiguity. The agent's behavior in production is mostly the quality of this one block of text.
summary: Full ten-line customer-support system prompt template combining persona, tools, constraints, and output rules.

example of weaving four parts into one prompt

text: Chapter 24.3 covers few-shot prompting plus structured output for reliable LLM classification. Few-shot replaces English instructions with 3 to 5 worked input-output examples so the model pattern-matches the format. JSON schemas with enums, required fields, and numeric ranges lock the response shape so the API rejects anything malformed. The two techniques complement each other: examples teach values and style, schemas enforce structure. Production rules: 3-5 examples, diverse not similar, include edge cases, span all enum values, put the hardest case last. The chapter ends with a complete reusable ticket-classifier template (system line + three examples + schema + ticket slot) returning JSON like { "category": "billing", "urgency": "high", "confidence": 0.85 }.
summary: Combining few-shot examples with JSON schema constraints produces reliable, drift-free structured LLM output suited for production classification.
queries: how to build reliable LLM classifier

text:
Show, don't just tell. Zero-shot prompts describe the task in English. Few-shot prompts demonstrate the task with 3 to 5 worked examples first. The model is far better at pattern-matching examples than parsing English instructions.

Zero-shot example: 'Classify this ticket as billing, product, or troubleshooting: "Charged twice for last month."' Model output is unpredictable - it returns prose like 'This looks like a billing issue, possibly a duplicate charge...' That's the wrong shape, not a label.

Few-shot example: three labeled pairs then the query.
Input: "I can't log in." → billing
Input: "Charged twice." → billing
Input: "500 errors." → troubleshooting
Input: "Charged twice for last month." →
Model snaps to the pattern and outputs one token: 'billing'. Same model, same task, but examples raise classification accuracy from wobbly to reliable.
summary: Few-shot prompts demonstrate the task with worked examples, producing reliable structured output where zero-shot prompts produce unpredictable prose.
queries: how to make LLM output predictable
what does few-shot mean for LLMs

text:
Three examples, same format. Anatomy of a few-shot block: a list of input-output pairs in identical format. The format is the lesson - the model learns the shape from these three then applies it to the real query.

Example 1: Input 'I can't log in - reset my password.' → Category: billing, Urgency: low.
Example 2: Input 'Charged twice for last month.' → Category: billing, Urgency: high.
Example 3: Input 'Dashboard shows 500 errors.' → Category: troubleshooting, Urgency: medium.

Three categories (billing, product, troubleshooting) and three urgency levels (low, medium, high). Every example uses the same labels in the same slots. Consistency is the whole point.
summary: Three labeled input-output examples in identical format teach the model the exact shape and vocabulary to reuse.
queries: what does a few-shot block look like
how model learns shape from examples

summary: JSON schema with enums, required fields, and numeric ranges forces the model's output into a fixed shape the API can validate.
queries: how to lock LLM output format

text:
Together they eliminate drift. Few-shot and structured output do different jobs. Examples teach VALUES and STYLE. Schemas enforce STRUCTURE. Run them together and the model produces the same shape with the same vocabulary every single call.

Few-shot teaches values + style: which categories exist, what urgency means here, how a typical ticket maps to a label.
Schema enforces structure: field names, allowed enum values, types, required keys. API rejects anything that doesn't match.
Together = zero drift: same shape, consistent labels, every call. Safe to feed straight into a database or downstream tool.

Final output: { "category": "billing", "urgency": "high", "confidence": 0.85 }

Belt and suspenders. Each technique catches a different failure mode, so together they leave almost nothing to chance.
summary: Few-shot teaches values and style while schemas enforce structure - combined they eliminate drift and produce identical-shape outputs every call.
queries: why use both examples and schema
what does few-shot teach vs schema

text:
What actually matters in few-shot. Five rules from production:

Tip 1: Use 3 to 5 examples. More than 5 often hurts more than helps. Diminishing returns set in fast.
Tip 2: Diverse examples beat similar ones. Three near-duplicates teach one pattern. Three different cases teach three.
Tip 3: Edge cases improve robustness. A tricky example moves the needle more than a typical one. Include cases that break naive rules.
Tip 4: Span all enum values. Every category and urgency level should appear at least once across your examples.
Tip 5: Put the hardest case last. The last example sets the strongest pattern. End on the case you most want the model to remember.

Diverse, edge-case-heavy, enum-spanning, hardest-last. The ordering rule is the one teams forget most - and it matters more than count of examples.
summary: Five production rules for few-shot: 3-5 examples, diverse, edge-case-heavy, span all enum values, hardest case last.

text:
The ticket-classifier we'll reuse. All pieces stitched into one prompt: system line on top, three few-shot examples in middle, output schema, then ticket slot.

Template:
SYSTEM: You classify support tickets for Alice's customer-support team. Return JSON only, matching the provided schema.

EXAMPLES:
Input:  "I can't log in - reset my password."
Output: { "category": "billing",         "urgency": "low",    "confidence": 0.91 }

Input:  "Charged twice for last month."
Output: { "category": "billing",         "urgency": "high",   "confidence": 0.95 }

Input:  "Dashboard shows 500 errors."
Output: { "category": "troubleshooting", "urgency": "medium", "confidence": 0.88 }

OUTPUT SCHEMA:
{ "category": enum["billing", "product", "troubleshooting"], "urgency": enum["low", "medium", "high"], "confidence": number in [0, 1] }

TICKET TO CLASSIFY: "{{ ticket_text }}"

Reusable: one template, thousands of tickets per day, identical output shape. Auditable: every field logged, can review wrong calls and tune examples. Tunable: swap one example, redeploy, measure - no model retraining required.
summary: Complete ticket-classifier template combining system instruction, three labeled examples, output schema, and a ticket slot for the live query.
queries: system instruction plus examples plus schema
how to build a classifier with prompts

text: Chain Of Thought + Self-Consistency. Chain of thought (CoT) makes the model show its reasoning so multi-step errors get caught. Zero-shot CoT uses a trigger phrase like 'Let's think step by step' or 'Think carefully, show your work'. Few-shot CoT goes further by including full reasoning traces in examples to transfer reasoning style. Self-consistency samples N CoT runs at temperature 0.7 and takes the majority vote to collapse variance. Costs: CoT is 5x tokens and latency for +15% on math; self-consistency N=5 is 25x tokens for +20%, with parallel sampling holding latency at 5x. Use CoT for multi-step math, multi-hop reasoning, ambiguous questions. Skip it for lookups, simple classification, single-fact Q&A. Reasoning models reason internally, often making explicit CoT prompts redundant.
summary: Chapter overview of chain of thought prompting, zero-shot and few-shot variants, self-consistency voting, cost-accuracy tradeoffs, and when to use or skip CoT.
queries: chain of thought and self consistency summary
zero shot vs few shot CoT
when to use chain of thought prompting

text: Reasoning In The Open. Some questions look simple but hide multi-step math. Asking for the final number alone gives the model nowhere to recover when it slips. Chain of thought forces the work onto the page - and the work catches the mistake. Question: Ticket T4: customer on annual plan cancels in month 7. Annual plan is $120/year. Cancellation fee is 10%. What is the refund? Direct Answer (WRONG): $45 with no reasoning shown - the model guessed and stopped. Chain Of Thought (CORRECT): 1) Annual plan = $120 for 12 months. 2) 5 months remain (12 minus 7 used). 3) Prorated refund = 120 x 5 / 12 = $50. 4) Cancellation fee = 10% of $50 = $5. 5) Final refund = $50 minus $5 = $45. Final: $45. Same model, same question - only difference is whether model shows its work. CoT exposed prorating and fee; direct call had nothing to anchor to.
summary: Side-by-side refund example showing how chain of thought exposes the prorating and fee math while a direct answer hides reasoning.
queries: what is chain of thought prompting
why does chain of thought help LLMs
direct answer vs chain of thought example
how does CoT catch model mistakes
ticket T4 refund 120 dollar annual plan
chain of thought multi step math example
LLM step by step refund problem
model slips on math without reasoning
how to make model show work

text: The 'Let's Think Step By Step' Trick. Zero-shot CoT is the cheapest reasoning upgrade you can buy: one extra sentence on the user turn. No examples, no system-prompt changes. Two phrases work reliably. Classic Trigger: 'User: Ticket T4 refund - annual plan cancelled month 7. What is the refund? User: Let's think step by step.' Stronger Trigger: 'User: Ticket T4 refund - annual plan cancelled month 7. What is the refund? User: Think carefully before answering. Show your work.' Zero examples, just a trigger phrase. Model has seen so much 'step by step' reasoning in training that this single phrase flips it into reasoning mode for free.
summary: Zero-shot CoT uses one trigger phrase like 'Let's think step by step' or 'Think carefully, show your work' to unlock reasoning without examples.
queries: what is zero-shot chain of thought
lets think step by step prompt
cheapest way to add reasoning to LLM
why does step by step work for LLMs
how to trigger reasoning without examples

text: Show The Reasoning In Examples. Few-shot CoT raises the bar. Each example includes the reasoning trace, not just the answer. This transfers the reasoning STYLE, not just the answer format - so the model imitates how you think, not just what you output. Example 1: Question: Ticket T1: annual plan $120, cancelled month 3. No fee. What is the refund? Reasoning: 1) 9 months remain out of 12. 2) Prorated refund = 120 x 9 / 12 = $90. 3) No fee applied. Answer: $90. Example 2: Question: Ticket T4: annual plan $120, cancelled month 7. 10% cancellation fee. What is the refund? Reasoning: 1) 5 months remain out of 12. 2) Prorated refund = 120 x 5 / 12 = $50. 3) Fee = 10% of $50 = $5. Answer: $45. Example does the work. Model now knows: list months remaining, prorate, apply fee, then state answer. Few-shot CoT teaches that exact chain.
summary: Few-shot chain of thought shows full reasoning traces in examples so the model imitates the style of thinking, not just the answer format.
queries: what is few-shot chain of thought
how to teach LLM reasoning style

text: Run N Times, Vote. Self-consistency is a simple trick: ask the same CoT question N times with temperature 0.7, collect the answers, and take the majority vote. Variance shrinks. Wrong runs get out-voted. Question: Ticket T4 refund - annual plan $120, cancelled month 7, 10% fee. Sample 5 times with temperature = 0.7. Run 1: Prorated $50 minus 10% fee = $45 - $45 correct. Run 2: 5/12 x 120 = $50. Fee $5. Final $45 - $45 correct. Run 3: Refund $50 prorated. Forgot the fee - $50 wrong. Run 4: $50 prorated, $5 fee, $45 refund - $45 correct. Run 5: Months left 5. Refund $50. Fee 10% - $45 correct. Majority Vote: $45 x 4 vs $50 x 1. Winner: $45. One run can slip and forget the fee. Five runs disagree, the correct answer wins by majority. Variance collapses without retraining anything.
summary: Self-consistency samples N reasoning chains at temperature 0.7 and takes the majority vote so wrong runs get out-voted.
queries: how to reduce LLM variance without training

text: What CoT Buys And What It Costs. Reasoning isn't free. CoT generates extra tokens. Self-consistency multiplies that by N. Accuracy gain is real but the bill is real too. Tradeoff table - Direct Answer: 1x tokens, 1x latency, Baseline accuracy. CoT Alone: 5x tokens, 5x latency, +15% on math. Self-Consistency (N=5): 25x tokens, 5x (parallel) to 25x (serial) latency, +20% on math. Self-consistency tradeoff: 5x to 25x the cost for ~5% extra accuracy over CoT alone. Run samples in parallel and latency drops back to 5x. Cost stays at 25x. Only worth it when accuracy is critical - billing, refunds, dosage, legal answers.
summary: Tradeoff table comparing token cost, latency, and accuracy of direct answers, CoT alone (5x cost +15%), and self-consistency N=5 (25x cost +20%).
queries: chain of thought cost vs accuracy
when is self consistency worth it
billing refunds dosage legal use cases

text: When Chain-Of-Thought Wins. CoT is a Decision Matrix problem. Technique helps when there is real reasoning to do. It hurts when the task is a lookup or one-step classification - extra tokens, extra latency, no payoff. Use CoT (Reasoning Required): Multi-Step Arithmetic - refunds, prorating, tax breakdowns, discount stacking - any math with two or more steps. Multi-Hop Reasoning - questions that chain facts: who did X for the customer that signed up in 2023 and is in tier B? Ambiguous Questions - cases where user's intent isn't obvious from words alone; reasoning surfaces the assumption. Skip CoT (Direct Answer Is Better): Simple Classification - map ticket text to one of three labels; few-shot already nails it, CoT just adds latency. Lookup Tasks - fetch customer's email or plan; no reasoning needed, it's a retrieval. Single-Fact Q&A - what time does support open? CoT will hallucinate a derivation where a direct answer suffices. Reasoning Models Change The Calculus: reasoning models like ones from Section 14.4 Thinking do CoT internally before replying. You no longer prompt for 'step by step' - the model thinks first, then emits the answer. CoT prompts still help on non-reasoning models, but with a reasoning model the technique often becomes redundant. Match the technique to the task. Multi-step math gets CoT. Category lookup gets direct answer. When the model already reasons internally, save the tokens.
summary: Decision matrix for CoT - use it for multi-step math, multi-hop reasoning, ambiguous questions; skip for classification, lookups, single-fact Q&A; reasoning models make CoT prompts redundant.
queries: when to use chain of thought
do reasoning models need step by step prompt

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Chapter 24.5 contrasts the four main ways to build with LLMs - Prompting, Fine-Tuning, RAG, and Agent - across four axes: data freshness, capability gap, latency, and cost. Prompting changes nothing in model or data and runs as 1 LLM call. Fine-Tuning bakes new behavior into weights. RAG retrieves fresh chunks at call-time via vector search. Agent uses tools in a loop. A decision tree picks the right one: model already knows it -> Prompting; knowledge must stay current -> RAG; specialized skill missing -> Fine-Tuning; need real-world actions -> Agent. Production systems usually STACK all four (fine-tuned base + RAG + agent loop + prompting). Anti-patterns: fine-tuning for fresh facts, RAG for missing behavior, agent loops for simple lookups, massive prompts instead of fine-tuning a small model.
summary: Overview of how Prompting, Fine-Tuning, RAG, and Agent differ across freshness, capability, latency, and cost axes, and how production systems stack them.

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Four Tools In The Toolbox. Every customer-support feature is one of four things: a prompt change, a weight change, a retrieval change, or a tool loop. Prompting: changes nothing in model or data. Write better messages. Same base weights, same world knowledge. At inference: 1 LLM call. Fine-Tuning: model weights change, data is baked in. Train on examples of the new behavior; the new skill lives inside the parameters. At inference: 1 LLM call (specialized model). RAG: data is retrieved at call-time. Vector search pulls fresh chunks into the prompt. Same base model, new context per call. At inference: 1 retrieval + 1 LLM call. Agent: model uses tools in a loop. LLM decides what to do, calls a tool, reads the result, loops until done. At inference: multiple LLM calls + tool calls. These aren't competitors - they solve different problems.
summary: Defines the four approaches by what each changes and what runs at inference time.

how many LLM calls does RAG need
how many LLM calls does an agent need

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Does The Data Change? Data freshness is the first filter. If the answer depends on facts that move - prices, inventory, the ticket just filed - those facts cannot live inside the model weights. Freshness axis from Never to Real-Time: Fine-Tuning = Never to weekly (retraining is slow and expensive). Prompting = Never (whatever you wrote into the prompt is frozen). RAG = Hourly (re-index docs and the model sees them next call). Agent = Real-time per query (tools hit live APIs at the moment of the question). Rule of thumb: data that moves fast belongs OUTSIDE the model. Prompt and weights are frozen between calls; retrieval and tools are not.
summary: Ranks the four approaches by how quickly each can refresh the data it relies on.
queries: how fresh is data in prompting
how fresh is data in fine-tuning
where do fresh facts belong in an LLM system
are model weights frozen between calls

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Is The Capability Missing? Second axis is the capability gap. Ask: does the model already know how to do this, or is the skill itself missing? Prompting (position 0.1) = already in base model: trigger the skill that's already there, cheapest path. RAG (position 0.4) = knows the skill, missing the facts: reading-comprehension is in the model, private docs are not. Agent (position 0.65) = knows the skill, missing the actions: reasoning is in the model, side-effects need tools. Fine-Tuning (position 0.95) = specialized skill never seen: style, domain jargon, structured output the base model can't imitate. Summary: RAG fills factual gaps. Agent fills action gaps. Fine-tuning fills skill gaps. Prompting only works when the skill is already inside the model and you just need to point at it.
summary: Maps each approach to the kind of gap it fills - factual, action, skill, or none.

why use an agent instead of RAG

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Latency And Cost Budget. Third and fourth axes are budgets. Real-time chat has a latency budget; nightly batch jobs have a cost budget. Quadrants: Low Latency, Low Cost = Prompting (1 call, small prompt; cheapest and fastest by default). Low Latency, Higher Cost Upfront = Fine-Tuning (training is expensive, inference after is short prompt + 1 call). Higher Latency, Low Cost = RAG (add vector search before the call; tokens stay small, freshness is free). Highest Latency, Variable Cost = Agent (each loop is another call; cost scales with tool steps). Order: Latency Prompting ~ Fine-Tune < RAG << Agent. Cost per call same order, but Agent loops can balloon. Budgets break ties - when two approaches both work technically, the one fitting latency floor and cost ceiling wins.
summary: Places each approach in a latency-cost quadrant and gives a tie-breaking rule.

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Which One? Walk The Tree. Combine the axes into one decision tree, top to bottom. First Yes is the answer - or the starting point if you need to stack. Question 1: Does the model already know it well enough? Yes -> Prompting alone. Question 2: Knowledge needs to stay current? Yes -> RAG. Question 3: Need a specialized skill the model doesn't have? Yes -> Fine-Tuning (plus RAG if data also moves). Question 4: Need to take actions in the world via tools? Yes -> Agent. Need to take actions via tools always points at an Agent. Decision tree, not flowchart. Each leaf is a single technique. If two questions answer Yes, the next sub-step shows how to combine them.
summary: A four-question decision tree that maps each Yes to the right LLM approach.

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Most Production Systems Stack Them. A real customer-support assistant rarely uses one technique. The shipping pattern is a stack, each layer covers what the layer below cannot. Layer 1: Fine-Tuned Base Model - teaches the support voice and the refund-policy JSON shape. Layer 2: + RAG Layer - pulls today's order history, today's billing record, today's playbook. Layer 3: + Agent Loop - reads tickets, issues refunds, sends emails. Real side-effects. Layer 4: + Prompting / Few-Shot Per Task - system prompt + examples shape each individual call. Stack pattern: fine-tune + RAG + agent loop + prompting combine into one system. These aren't either/or. Knowing each layer's job is how you debug the stack when something breaks.
summary: Shows the production stack: fine-tuned base + RAG + agent loop + prompting layered together.

chapterTitle: Prompt vs Fine-Tune vs RAG vs Agent
text: Common Misuses. Four anti-patterns show up over and over. Each picks the wrong tool for an axis the team didn't think about. (1) Fine-Tuning To Add Fresh Facts - Wrong move: retraining every time the pricing page changes. Right fix: use RAG - facts that move belong outside the weights. (2) RAG For Behavior The Model Doesn't Know - Wrong move: stuffing style guides into the prompt and hoping the tone sticks. Right fix: fine-tune - behavior lives in weights, not retrieved chunks. (3) Agent Loop For A Simple Lookup - Wrong move: multi-step planning to answer 'what's the refund window?' Right fix: direct call + 1 tool - loops are for tasks that branch. (4) Massive Prompt Instead Of Fine-Tuning A Small Model - Wrong move: sending 30 examples on every call to a frontier model. Right fix: fine-tune a small model once - cost runaway stops, latency drops. Pattern under every misuse: someone picked their favorite tool instead of walking the four axes.
summary: Four LLM anti-patterns and the right fix for each.

text: Context engineering is the meta-skill of packing the fixed 128k token window of a frontier LLM. Every call must fit five layers (system prompt, few-shot examples, conversation history, retrieved RAG chunks, user query) inside the hard cap. Order matters because models read top-to-bottom and exhibit lost-in-the-middle recall (peaks at start and end, dips to ~45% near the middle). Strategies trade history for retrieval or output. Eviction follows a ladder: drop older retrieved chunks first, summarize mid-conversation turns, never evict the system prompt or last user message.
summary: Chapter overview of context engineering: 128k window, five-layer assembly stack, lost-in-the-middle, budget tradeoffs, and eviction ladder.

text: Tokens In, Tokens Out, Hard Cap. Every model has a context window: a hard token budget the call has to live inside. A frontier model today is 128k tokens. Every prompt is a packing problem against that ceiling. The 128k window is broken into reserved zones: System Prompt 4k (rules + persona, always present), Conversation History 50k (prior turns, grows every turn), Retrieved Docs 69k (RAG chunks, refilled per query), User Input 1k (the new question, capped at 1k), Reserved For Output 4k (headroom the model writes into). Anything past 128k gets dropped or summarized. Context engineering is the meta-skill of deciding what survives the cap on every single call.
summary: Introduces the 128k context window as a hard cap split into five reserved zones with specific token budgets.
queries: how big is the context window
what is the token budget for an LLM
how are tokens split in a prompt
how many tokens for conversation history

text: What Goes In, In Which Order. The model reads top-to-bottom. Order is not cosmetic - it changes what the model attends to. The assembly stack is the same every call: Layer 1 System Prompt (persona, rules, tone, refusal policy, read first, anchors everything below); Layer 2 Few-Shot Examples (2-5 input + output pairs that pin the format, read before any real content); Layer 3 Conversation History (prior turns of this thread, older turns nearest the top of the section); Layer 4 Retrieved Chunks (RAG hits for this query, reranked closer-to-bottom = closer-to-query attention); Layer 5 User Query (the thing to actually answer, sits at the very bottom for maximum recency). Recency = bottom. Whatever you want the model to weight most goes nearest the user query. Same five layers every turn.
summary: Defines the fixed five-layer prompt assembly stack from system prompt at top to user query at bottom, ordered for recency.

what goes at bottom of context
how does prompt ordering affect attention

text: Same Window, Different Strategies. 128k is a fixed pie. Every call is a tradeoff between more retrieval, more history, or more output. Three strategies for the same long support ticket: (1) Cram Everything - System 4k, History raw 50k, Retrieved top-20 70k, User 1k, Output 3k. Verdict: at-risk, lost-in-middle kicks in. (2) Trim History - System 4k, History summary 8k, Retrieved 80k, User 1k, Output 35k. Verdict: wins for fact-heavy tickets, keeps room for retrieval. (3) Trim Retrieval - System 4k, History raw 50k, Retrieved top-3 8k, User 1k, Output 65k. Verdict: wins for long multi-turn convos, keeps room for output. Rule: summarize what you can compress, evict what you cannot, never starve the output. Trim and summarize are the two levers; which wins depends on the ticket type, not on taste.
summary: Compares three concrete budget allocations (cram, trim history, trim retrieval) for the same 128k window and gives verdicts.
queries: should I summarize history or trim retrieval
summarize vs evict in context window

text: Models Forget The Middle. Even with a 128k window, the model does not attend evenly. Recall drops in the middle and spikes at the start and end. This is the lost-in-the-middle effect, and it is the single biggest reason long prompts fail. Sample recall accuracy by position: Start (0) 93%, 15% in: 78%, 30% in: 60%, 45% in: 48%, Middle (50%) 45%, 55% in: 48%, 70% in: 62%, 85% in: 82%, End (100%) 95%. Rule: put the most important info at the START or END of context, never in the middle. Buried in the middle is buried full stop. If a fact has to land, place it next to the user query or inside the system prompt - those are the two recall peaks.
summary: Explains the lost-in-the-middle effect with a U-shaped recall curve and the rule to place critical info at start or end.
queries: what is lost in the middle effect
why do LLMs forget the middle
recall accuracy by position in context
best position for important info in prompt
do models attend evenly to context
recall peaks at start and end
where to put critical facts in prompt
why models miss middle of context

text: Relevance-First vs Recency-First. Inside the retrieved-chunks and history layers, ordering is its own choice. Two strategies cover the common cases. Relevance-First: when to use - factual queries, one-shot lookups; rule - rerank retrieved chunks by similarity, top-3 sit closest to the user query; example - Ticket 'What's the refund window for plan Pro?' -> top-3 RAG hits next to the query. Recency-First: when to use - multi-turn conversations, long debugging threads; rule - latest history at the bottom (near query) for conversational coherence; example - Ticket 6-turn back-and-forth -> last 2 user / assistant turns sit nearest the query. Factual lookup -> relevance-first. Long conversation -> recency-first. Hybrid -> rerank then recency-bias the last 2 turns. Same chunks, different order, different answer.
summary: Contrasts relevance-first and recency-first chunk/history ordering and maps each to factual vs conversational query shapes.
queries: relevance first vs recency first ordering
should I rerank by similarity or recency

text: Eviction Order: Cheapest Loss First. When the packed prompt exceeds 128k, something has to leave. Eviction is not random - there is a ladder. #1 Older Retrieved Chunks (Beyond Top-3) - Evict First - rerank surfaced them but top-3 carry the answer almost always, drop the long tail. #2 Mid-Conversation History (Raw Turns) - Summarize Then Evict - compress middle 5+ turns into one summary line, keep newest 2 turns raw. #3 Few-Shot Examples Beyond The First Two - Evict Soon - two examples pin the format, examples 3-5 are insurance and cost tokens. #4 Top-3 Retrieved Chunks - Rarely Evict - these ARE the answer to this query, only drop if user input is huge. #5 Latest Assistant Response - Rarely Evict - removing it loses the model's own context for what it just said. #6 System Prompt + Last User Message - Never Evict - the rules of the game and the question itself, evicting either breaks the call. Summarize before you evict.
summary: Lists the six-rung eviction ladder from drop-first older retrieval to never-evict system prompt and last user message.
queries: what to evict from prompt first
what should I drop when prompt too long

Right box (green): Result - 'Flows back to the model'. Arrows labeled Tool_use (Model -> Runtime) and Execute (Runtime -> Result). A dashed return arrow loops the Result back to the Model labeled 'Return: tool_result becomes the next input'. Title: 'One tool call: Decide -> Execute -> Return'.

Bottom-left (green): Observe - 'Read result'. Arrows go Reason -> Act -> Observe -> Reason. Center label: 'Loop until done'. Cycle repeats until the model decides it has enough information to answer.

text: Chapter 25.1 introduces tool use as the bridge between a pure LLM and a real agent. A tool is a declared function: name, description, parameters. The model decides when to call and emits a tool_use block; the runtime executes the real function and returns a tool_result. This Decide -> Execute -> Return split is the safety boundary. A single tool call is the atom; placed in a Reason-Act-Observe loop it becomes an agent that can do multi-step work. The running example is a support agent with 8 tools, 3 read-only and 5 side-effectful, with process_refund's $200 escalation threshold as the canary case.
summary: Tool use connects LLMs to real systems via declared functions, with the model deciding and the runtime executing inside a Reason-Act-Observe loop.
queries: overview of tool use as bridge

text: Pure LLM vs LLM with tools using support ticket T5: 'What's the difference between Pro and Enterprise?'. Pure LLM relies on training data, may give outdated or hallucinated answers like 'Pro has more features than Enterprise, I think...' - risky, no source. LLM + Tools calls search_kb('Pro vs Enterprise'), fetches live doc, answers 'Pro: 10 seats, $49/mo, email support. Enterprise: unlimited seats, SSO, dedicated CSM.' - grounded and traceable. Same model, same prompt; the only difference is access to search_kb. That one hook turns a chatty box into a working agent.
summary: Tools let an LLM ground answers in live data instead of guessing from stale training memory.

text: A tool is exactly one function the model is allowed to call. You declare the signature like lookup_customer(email: string) -> Customer. The model decides WHEN to call. The runtime executes the real function. The result flows back into the conversation as the next input. Flow: Model emits tool_use -> Runtime executes -> Result returns as tool_result and re-invokes the model. Model = brain, Runtime = hands. The LLM never touches the database directly; it only emits a tool_use block. The runtime does the actual call. This boundary is what makes tool use safe enough to ship.
summary: A tool is a declared function the model chooses to call while the runtime executes it, keeping decision and side effects separate.
queries: what is a tool in LLM agents

text: Every tool definition has exactly three parts. PART 1 Name: process_refund - the identifier the model emits when calling. PART 2 Description: 'Refund a paid invoice. Refunds over $200 require human approval.' - plain-English contract the model reads to decide WHEN to call. PART 3 Parameters: { invoice_id: string, reason: string } - arguments the model must produce, validated before the runtime executes. Description is the most important field; spend most editing time there. The model has never seen your codebase; the description is its only manual. Vague description -> model guesses; specific -> model rarely misfires.
summary: A tool spec has three parts - name, description, parameters - and the description is the model's primary contract.

text: Two distinct roles: model decides which tool and arguments, runtime handles side effects (DB reads, card charges, emails). Six steps: 1) User sends 'Refund my last invoice, the charge was wrong.' 2) Model Reads - compares message to every tool's description. 3) Model Decides - 'User asked for a refund, I should call process_refund' or chooses no tool. 4) Model Emits tool_use - generation halts, response is { name: 'process_refund', input: {...} }. 5) Runtime Executes - your code validates inputs and calls real function. 6) Result Returns - runtime appends tool_result and re-invokes the model. The model may also choose NOT to call any tool. The runtime is the single chokepoint where auth, logging, and rate limits live.
summary: A tool call is six steps where the model halts at tool_use and the runtime is the chokepoint for all real-world side effects.

text: A single tool call is the atom. An agent loop is the molecule: Reason -> Act -> Observe -> Reason again until done. Reason: read messages so far, decide if a tool is needed and which one. Act: emit a tool_use block, runtime calls function for real. Observe: read the tool_result, did it succeed, was info enough to answer. One ticket might be a single search_kb call. Another might need lookup_customer, then lookup_subscription, then process_refund - three turns of the loop before answering. Loop formalized in 26.3.
summary: An agent is one tool call placed in a Reason-Act-Observe loop that repeats until the model has enough to answer.

text: Canonical 8-tool inventory for the support agent reused across the rest of the section. search_kb(query) - search customer support knowledge base. lookup_customer(email) - find customer by email. lookup_subscription(customer_id) - get current plan and status. reset_password(customer_id) - send password-reset email. change_email(customer_id, new_email) - update email on record. process_refund(invoice_id, reason) - refund invoice; over $200 requires escalation. escalate_human(transcript, urgency) - hand off to human agent. send_email(template_id, vars) - send templated email. Split: 3 read-only (search, lookup, lookup) cheap to retry; 5 side-effectful (reset, change, refund, escalate, send) need auth, validation, approvals. process_refund is the canary - any refund over $200 escalates instead of executing.
summary: The chapter's running support agent has 8 tools, split into 3 cheap-to-retry read-only and 5 guarded side-effectful operations.

text:
Descriptions Are Read By The Model. The description field is plain language for the model. It is the most important text in your whole tool definition. Treat it like a manual entry the model will skim every turn. Five rules turn a bad description into a great one.

RULE 1: State what the tool does in plain language. One sentence the model can match against the user's request.
RULE 2: State the side effects. Does it mutate state? Send email? Cost money? The model needs to know before calling.
RULE 3: State when NOT to use the tool. Negative examples prevent over-eager calls. "Do not use for refunds over $200" saves real dollars.
RULE 4: Use the same vocabulary the user would use. If the user says cancel, do not name the tool terminate_subscription. Match the word.
RULE 5: Mention any constraints. Rate limits, dollar thresholds, retry semantics, idempotency - put them all in the description.

Description is the model's manual. Write it like you would write a runbook for a new hire. Spend ten times longer on the description than on the parameter names. A great description prevents wrong-tool calls, prevents the model from inventing arguments, and tells the model exactly when to call escalate_human instead of process_refund.
summary: Five rules for writing effective tool descriptions: state what it does, state side effects, state when not to use it, match user vocabulary, and mention constraints.

text:
One Sentence Changes Everything. Same tool, same parameters. The only difference between a refund that goes wrong and one that goes right is the description string. Read both versions of process_refund below.

BAD (Vague): "description": "Refund function." Why bad: No side effects mentioned. No dollar threshold. No when-not-to-use. The model will call it for anything that sounds refund-shaped.

GOOD (Specific): "description": "Refund a paid invoice. Use only when the customer has confirmed cancellation. Refunds over $200 require human approval - call escalate_human instead for those." Why good: What it does. When to use. When NOT to use. The $200 threshold. The fallback tool. The model now has a real contract.

Bad description: model refunds a $500 invoice. Good description: model calls escalate_human instead. The vague version costs you real dollars in mis-refunds. The specific version threads the $200 ceiling and the escalation fallback right into the model's prompt - so the model is choosing escalate_human before it ever attempts the refund.
summary: Side-by-side bad vs good description for process_refund, showing how a specific description with thresholds and fallback prevents the model from mis-refunding a $500 invoice.

text: Chapter 13.9 Tool Call Lifecycle walks the full journey of a tool call across six actors: User -> Model -> Runtime -> Tool -> Runtime -> Model. The model emits a tool_use block (type, id, name, input); the runtime validates and executes the real function; the runtime wraps the return in a tool_result block (type, tool_use_id, content, is_error) and appends it; the model re-reads everything and writes the final assistant message. A worked trace of ticket T1 ("I can't log in - reset my password") shows two tool calls (lookup_customer then reset_password) over three LLM turns. Streaming pauses on tool_use (which arrives as a whole JSON block, not token by token), runs the tool, then resumes. Latency budget for T1 is 3.4s total: 3.0s (88%) in LLM calls, 0.4s (12%) in tool calls - the model is the bottleneck.
summary: Full lifecycle of a tool call across six actors, with message shapes, a worked T1 trace, streaming behavior, and latency budget.

text: Swim-lane diagram: From Request To Final Answer. Six rows top to bottom showing the actors and messages: 1) User -> User message ("I can't log in - reset my password."). 2) Model -> tool_use block (reads context, picks a tool, emits name+input). 3) Runtime -> Function call (validates tool_use, looks up real function, calls it). 4) Tool -> Function return (runs real side effect on DB/email/refund, returns value or error). 5) Runtime -> tool_result block (wraps return value, appends to conversation). 6) Model -> Assistant message (re-reads conversation, writes final answer). Six actors. Two message kinds (tool_use, tool_result) carry the call from model to runtime and back. The model never touches the tool directly - the runtime is the single boundary where every real-world effect happens.
summary: Six-actor swim-lane showing the full path of a tool call from user message to assistant answer.

text:
Tool-Result: Runtime Returns The Outcome. After the runtime executes the real function, it wraps the return value into a tool_result block and appends it to the conversation. Canonical message:
{
  "type": "tool_result",
  "tool_use_id": "toolu_01abc...",
  "content": "Password reset email sent to alice@example.com.",
  "is_error": false
}
Fields: type is always "tool_result" - tells the model this block carries a return value. tool_use_id MUST match the id from the tool_use - this is how the model pairs request to response. content is the return value - usually a string, can be structured; this is what the model reads next. is_error is a flag - true means the runtime failed, the model should recover, not pretend it worked. tool_use_id pairs the response to the request. Same id in, same id out. If the tool failed (DB down, validation error, network blip) the runtime still produces a tool_result but flips is_error to true so the model adapts instead of assuming success.
summary: Anatomy of the tool_result block the runtime appends after executing a tool (type, tool_use_id, content, is_error).

text:
Trace: Ticket T1 Password Reset. Playing the whole lifecycle on a real ticket. T1 is "I can't log in - reset my password." The model loops twice (lookup_customer, then reset_password) before it can answer. Six steps:
Step 1 User: "I can't log in - reset my password." Plain English. No customer_id, just an intent.
Step 2 Model: tool_use { name: lookup_customer, input: { email: "alice@example.com" } }. Email comes from conversation header. Model needs customer_id before it can reset.
Step 3 Runtime: tool_result { content: { customer_id: "c-9924", plan: "Pro" } }. DB hit. Runtime turns function return into tool_result.
Step 4 Model: tool_use { name: reset_password, input: { customer_id: "c-9924" } }. Now it has c-9924 from step 3. Second turn of the loop.
Step 5 Runtime: tool_result { content: "Email sent." }. Side effect happens here. Runtime is the single chokepoint.
Step 6 Model: "Done - check alice@example.com for the reset link." Final assistant message. No more tool_use - model is satisfied.
T1: 2 tool calls (lookup_customer + reset_password), 3 LLM turns, 1 final answer. The model never has c-9924 in step 2 - it only learns the id from step 3's tool_result. Each turn unlocks the next call by giving the model new evidence.
summary: End-to-end six-step trace of ticket T1 password reset showing two tool calls across three LLM turns.

text: Tools While Streaming. In production you usually stream assistant tokens to the UI for the typewriter effect. Tool calls fit cleanly inside a stream - but with one twist: text streams token by token; tool blocks arrive whole. Seven phases left to right: 1) Stream begins - assistant tokens start arriving one by one. 2) Partial text streams - "Let me look that up for you..." appears word by word. 3) Stream pauses - model decides to call a tool, token stream halts. 4) tool_use block emitted - block arrives as one complete JSON object, NOT token-by-token. 5) Runtime executes - your code runs the real function, could take 200ms or 2s. 6) tool_result delivered - result message appended; model re-invoked to continue. 7) Stream resumes - new assistant tokens flow again, building the final answer. Tool blocks aren't streamed token-by-token; they arrive complete. The UI shows partial text as it streams but holds the spinner during tool execution. When the tool_result lands and the stream resumes, the next batch of tokens picks up where it left off.
summary: How tool calls interleave with a streaming assistant response: text streams token by token but tool_use blocks arrive whole and pause the stream.

text:
Where The Seconds Go. Ticket T1 took 3.4 seconds end-to-end. Latency budget breakdown:
LLM call 1 (reads user, emits lookup_customer): 1200ms
lookup_customer tool execution: 200ms
LLM call 2 (reads result, emits reset_password): 1100ms
reset_password tool execution: 200ms
LLM call 3 (reads result, emits final answer): 700ms
Total: 3400ms = 3.4 seconds.
LLM calls: 3.0s (88%). Tool calls: 0.4s (12%). The model is the bottleneck. Every extra LLM call adds about 1 second. Speeding up T1 means cutting LLM turns, not tool latency. Parallel tool calls let you fire lookup_customer and lookup_subscription in one turn instead of two - shaving a whole LLM call from the budget.
summary: Latency budget for T1: 3.4s total, with 88% spent in LLM calls and only 12% in tool execution.

text: Chapter 25.4 (Parallel Tools + Tool Choice) covers when an agent can fan out multiple tool_use blocks in the same assistant turn versus when calls must serialize, and how the tool_choice field (auto, required, none, specific) controls model routing behavior. Using support tickets T2 (reset password + email change) and T4 (cancel + refund + escalate), it contrasts serial timelines (each tool waits for the prior LLM turn) with parallel timelines (independent lookups run concurrently), shows a 6.8s to 4.7s latency drop (31% faster, one fewer LLM call), and walks an end-to-end T2 trace where turn 1 fans out lookup_customer and lookup_subscription in parallel while change_email and reset_password serialize because the reset link must go to the updated email.
summary: Chapter overview of parallel tool calls, tool_choice modes, and latency savings using ticket T2 and T4 examples.

text: Two Lookups, Two Strategies. Ticket T2: 'Reset my password but my email also changed to new@example.com'. It needs two facts before acting: who is this customer, and what is their subscription. Naive (serial) runtime asks one tool, waits, asks the next, waits again. Parallel runtime emits both tool_use blocks in the same assistant turn and runs them concurrently. Serial steps: Step 1 LLM call 1 (LLM, 1200ms), Step 2 lookup_customer (Tool, 200ms), Step 3 LLM call 2 (LLM, 1200ms), Step 4 lookup_subscription (Tool, 200ms). Parallel steps: Step 1 LLM call 1 (LLM, 1200ms), Step 2 lookup_customer + lookup_subscription concurrent (Tool, 200ms). Same two tool calls. Serial = 400ms tool work + 2 LLM turns. Parallel = 200ms tool work + 1 LLM turn. Parallel only works when the two calls do not read each other's output. For independent lookups, parallel halves tool latency and cuts an entire LLM turn.

text: Independent? Parallel. Dependent? Serial. The rule: if call B needs an argument from call A's result, you cannot parallelize - the model has to wait for A before it can even form B. If neither call reads the other, fan out. Independent example (verdict: Parallel): lookup_customer(email) and lookup_subscription(email). Neither call reads the other's output. Both take an email and return a record. Rule: both tool_use blocks are emitted in the SAME assistant turn. Runtime fans out, awaits both, returns both tool_results. Dependent example (verdict: Serial): lookup_customer(email) then process_refund(customer_id, ...). process_refund needs customer_id from lookup_customer's result. The second call cannot be formed until the first returns. Rule: two assistant turns required - Turn 1 = lookup, Turn 2 = refund (after the tool_result is appended). Parallelize when calls don't read each other's outputs. process_refund needs customer_id so lookup_customer must run first in its own turn, but lookup_customer and lookup_subscription both take the same email and return independent records, so they share one turn.
summary: Decision rule: parallelize tools when neither call reads the other's output; serialize when call B depends on call A's result.
queries: independent vs dependent tool calls agent

text: Tool Choice = Auto (Default). By default the model decides whether to call a tool, which tool, and how many tools. Set tool_choice: 'auto' (or leave unset) and let the model route. For a customer-support agent this covers most flows. Config: tool_choice: 'auto'. The model decides per-message. Scenarios: (1) Ticket T5: 'Pro vs Enterprise - what's different?' -> Model picks search_kb(query='Pro vs Enterprise'). Knowledge question, picks the docs tool. (2) User: 'Hey, good morning!' -> No tool. Plain assistant reply. Chitchat - calling a tool would be wasted latency. (3) Ticket T2: 'Reset my password and email also changed.' -> lookup_customer AND lookup_subscription. Two tool_use blocks in one turn. Auto still picks - and it can pick more than one. Auto is the default for a reason: it covers chitchat (no tool), single lookups, and parallel lookups all from one config. Only switch modes when you need to control the model's hand.
summary: Explains tool_choice 'auto' (the default) which lets the model decide per-message, with scenarios for KB lookup, chitchat, and parallel lookups.
queries: model decides which tool to call
model picks zero tools for greeting

text: Four Modes Of Tool Choice. tool_choice is a single field with four shapes; each locks down the model's behavior a different amount. (1) auto - Model decides whether to call a tool and which one(s). Config: tool_choice: 'auto'. Use as default for most flows - chitchat, mixed Q&A, anything where the next step depends on the input. (2) required - Model MUST call at least one tool. Plain-text replies are blocked. Config: tool_choice: 'required'. Use when intent is already routed (e.g., user clicked 'Run a search'); forces the tool path. (3) none - Model MUST NOT call any tool; can only emit plain text. Config: tool_choice: 'none'. Use for final summarization, drafting a reply from already-gathered context, or pure chitchat. (4) specific - Force a specific tool; model can ONLY call this one named tool. Config: tool_choice: { type: 'tool', name: 'search_kb' }. Use for deterministic routing - your classifier already decided which API to call, model fills the arguments. Summary: auto = decide, required = must call something, none = must not call, specific = force this tool. The specific shape {type: 'tool', name: 'X'} wires a deterministic router into the agent.
summary: Lists the four tool_choice modes (auto, required, none, specific) with behavior, config syntax, and when to use each.

text: Numbers: How Much Does Parallel Save? Ticket T4: 'Cancel my subscription, refund this month, and have someone call me.' Three independent lookups (customer, subscription, refund policy) feed one mutation (process_refund). Serial (4 turns): LLM call 1 1500ms, lookup_customer 200ms, LLM call 2 1500ms, lookup_subscription 200ms, LLM call 3 1500ms, lookup_refund_policy 200ms, LLM call 4 1500ms, process_refund 200ms. 4 LLM calls of 1.5s + 4 tool calls of 200ms = 6.8s. 4 LLM calls total. Parallel (2 turns of lookups, then mutation): LLM call 1 1500ms, lookup_customer + lookup_subscription + lookup_refund_policy concurrent 200ms, LLM call 2 1500ms, process_refund 200ms, LLM call 3 1300ms. 3 LLM calls + 3 lookups concurrent + 1 mutation = 4.7s. 3 LLM calls total. Savings: 6.8s -> 4.7s (31% faster, one fewer LLM call). Each LLM turn costs ~1.5s; you also save stacked tool latency. For a busy support agent firing thousands of tickets a day, 31% is real money and real customer wait time.
summary: Worked latency comparison for ticket T4: serial 6.8s (4 LLM calls) vs parallel 4.7s (3 LLM calls), a 31% savings.

how much faster is parallel tool calling
real money saved by parallel agents

text: Trace: Ticket T2 With Parallel Lookups. End to end. Turn 1 is parallel (two lookups). Turns 2 and 3 are sequential because change_email must complete before reset_password sends the link to the new address. Steps: (1) User: 'Reset my password but my email also changed to new@example.com' - two intents in one ticket. (2) Model turn 1 [PARALLEL]: tool_use [lookup_customer(email=old) AND lookup_subscription(email=old)] - both blocks emitted in the SAME assistant turn, runtime fans them out in parallel. (3) Runtime [PARALLEL]: tool_result [customer_id=c-9924 AND subscription=active] - both finish at ~200ms concurrent, two tool_result blocks appended in parallel. (4) Model turn 2: tool_use { name: change_email, input: { customer_id: 'c-9924', new_email: 'new@example.com' } } - change_email must complete before reset_password because the reset link goes to the NEW email. (5) Runtime: tool_result { content: 'Email updated.' } - customer record mutated. (6) Model turn 3: tool_use { name: reset_password, input: { customer_id: 'c-9924' } } - reset link sent to new@example.com because the prior turn already updated the record. (7) Runtime: tool_result { content: 'Reset email sent.' } - runtime is the single chokepoint where real-world effects happen. (8) Model turn 4: 'Updated your email to new@example.com and sent a reset link there.' - final assistant message, no more tool_use. Split is determined by data flow, not the model. Independent reads share one turn; dependent mutations serialize so each acts on the latest record.
summary: Full 8-step trace of ticket T2: parallel lookups in turn 1, sequential change_email then reset_password mutations, final assistant reply.

text: Tool Errors, Retries, Validation chapter summary. Tool failures fit into four classes: transient (rate limit, timeout, 503), permanent (auth failed, missing record), malformed (schema, missing fields, wrong types), and business-rule (policy caps, blocked customers). Errors are returned as data in a structured tool_result with is_error: true and an error_class field - not thrown as exceptions - so the model reads them like any other field and adapts. Retry policy depends on class: 3 retries with exponential backoff (200ms, 400ms, 800ms) for transient, 0 for permanent, 1 for malformed, 0 for business-rule. The runtime validates tool_use arguments against the JSON schema in microseconds before executing the expensive tool function so malformed calls fail fast with a precise reason. Mutations that may be retried require a deterministic idempotency_key derived from a stable input (hash(invoice_id), request_id, client UUID) so retries dedupe and never double-execute. Ticket T4 trace shows the loop in action: process_refund of $350 over the $200 cap returns a business_rule error and the model adapts on its own by calling escalate_human - no special-case routing code needed.
summary: Chapter overview: four error classes, errors-as-data, per-class retry policy, schema validation gate, idempotency keys, and the Ticket T4 recovery trace.

text: Production tools fail and the retry strategy depends on WHY the call failed. Group every failure into one of four classes. Transient (error_class: "transient", color yellow): rate limit hit (HTTP 429), network timeout, upstream 503. Handling: retry with exponential backoff. Why: the call would probably succeed if you tried again in a moment. Permanent (error_class: "permanent", color red): auth failed (HTTP 401), customer not found, missing record. Handling: surface to user, do not retry. Why: retrying changes nothing - the state, not the network, is the problem. Malformed (error_class: "malformed", color purple): schema validation failed, missing required field, wrong type. Handling: return the error to the model, let it self-correct. Why: the model wrote bad arguments and given the error message it usually fixes them next turn. Business-rule (error_class: "business_rule", color orange): refund $350 > $200 auto-approve cap, blocked customer, outside policy window. Handling: return structured "rule violated" reason - the model adapts or escalates. Why: the call is syntactically valid but policy says no - the model needs the WHY to pick a different path. Summary line: Transient | Permanent | Malformed | Business-rule. Classify on the way out of the tool, attach the class to the tool_result, and the rest of the agent loop knows what to do.
summary: Four error classes for agent tools - transient, permanent, malformed, business-rule - each with examples, handling rule, and reasoning.

text:
Errors are data, not exceptions. The runtime does not throw a Python exception up the stack. It returns a structured tool_result with is_error set to true and an error_class field. The model reads those same fields it reads on a successful call and adapts. Example tool_result message:
{
  "type": "tool_result",
  "tool_use_id": "toolu_01xyz...",
  "content": "Refund denied: amount $350 exceeds $200 auto-approve cap.",
  "is_error": true,
  "error_class": "business_rule"
}
Key fields: is_error: true - the model reads this flag the same way it reads any other field; if true, treat content as the failure reason, not the answer. error_class: "business_rule" - the class tells the model whether to wait and retry, fix arguments, escalate, or apologize. Same payload shape, different next action. $350 over the $200 cap = structured business_rule error, not a stack trace. Because the error is data, the model can route on it. The next assistant turn picks a different tool (escalate_human) on its own - you did not write "if business_rule then escalate", the model inferred it from the message.
summary: Errors return as structured tool_result messages with is_error and error_class fields so the model can read and adapt without exception handling.

text: Match the retry policy to the error class. One retry policy across all errors is wrong. Retrying a permanent auth failure burns three round-trips. Not retrying a transient 503 fails a perfectly good call. Per-class retry policy: Transient - retries: 3, strategy: exponential backoff 200ms, 400ms, 800ms. Why: brief downtime usually clears in under a second. Permanent - retries: 0, strategy: surface immediately, no backoff, no second attempt. Why: re-asking the same impossible question wastes turns and tokens. Malformed - retries: 1, strategy: return schema error to the model, one retry, then escalate. Why: the model fixes its own arguments on the second try ~95% of the time. Business-rule - retries: 0, strategy: return policy reason, model chooses an alternate tool or escalates. Why: retrying never flips a policy, the model must adapt the plan. Transient retry budget: 3 attempts with exponential backoff (200ms, 400ms, 800ms). Exponential backoff matters because thundering-herd retries amplify outages - each retry waits twice as long, spreading load and giving the dependency a chance to recover.
summary: Retry budget and backoff schedule by error class: 3 transient, 0 permanent, 1 malformed, 0 business-rule.
