summary: Semantic memory is shaped as a customer profile card with typed fields plus a nested preferences bag.
queries: typed fields vs nested fields agent

text: Key-Value Or Vector. Two storage substrates serve different fact shapes. Most production agents use BOTH: structured for the spine, vector for the long-tail freetext. Structured (Key-Value / Postgres): use when fields are typed, predictable, queried by exact match. Example: customer_id -> profile lookup. Vector (Embedding + Similarity): use when facts are free-text, queried by meaning. Example: 'User has a dairy allergy' embedded for retrieval. The spine (typed profile) is small and exact. The long-tail (free-form preferences and constraints) is large and fuzzy. Use the right tool for each shape.
summary: Semantic memory uses key-value storage for typed exact-match fields plus vector storage for free-text similarity retrieval.
queries: key-value vs vector storage agent memory

exact match vs semantic match storage

text: How The Profile Fills Up. Semantic memory grows over time. Alice's profile at signup has 4 fields. By day 30 it has 5. By day 90 it has nested preferences. Growth comes from confirmed observations. Day 1 (Signup): customer_id: c-9924, tier: Pro, signed_up: 2024-08, primary_email: alice@example.com. Day 30: (all Day-1 fields) + preferred_contact: email, learned from 'please email me at...'. Day 90: (all Day-30 fields) + preferences.skip_onboarding_emails: true, learned from user opt-out click. Profile fills only on CONFIRMED observations. A guess from one ambiguous message does not get written. A repeated preference observed across sessions does.
summary: The profile grows incrementally from 4 to 5 to nested fields as the agent confirms observations across days.
queries: Day 1 vs Day 30 vs Day 90 profile

text: What Counts As A Fact? Rule of thumb: if it would still be true in 6 months, write it to semantic memory. Otherwise leave it in the working scratchpad or skip it entirely. Write to semantic: stable preferences (always email), confirmed identity (Alice is account owner), constraints (dairy allergy). Do not write: transient mood ('frustrated today'), one-time requests ('send a copy this once'), guesses (uncertain inferences). 6-Months Test: would knowing this still help six months from now? If yes, write it. If no, it belongs in working memory or the chat history and should expire naturally.
summary: The 6-months test decides what to write: stable preferences, identity, constraints go in; transient moods, one-offs, and guesses stay out.

text: Diagram: five-stage horizontal pipeline showing recipe retrieval. Stage 1 New Ticket (free text). Stage 2 Embed (1024 floats). Stage 3 ANN Search (vs Library). Stage 4 Top-1 (or no match). Stage 5 Use Recipe (scaffolding). Arrows connect each stage left to right. New ticket text is embedded, ANN search runs against the recipe library trigger embeddings, and the top-one match is surfaced for the agent to use as scaffolding.

text: Procedural memory stores skills (how-to recipes) separately from facts. The customer-support agent maintains a small library of named recipes, each with a trigger embedding, an ordered step list, success_rate, and uses count. New tickets are embedded and matched via ANN search against trigger embeddings; the top-1 match becomes a scaffold that the agent adapts to the ticket. No match means fall back to first-principles reasoning, which can be mined into a future recipe. Versus baking instructions into the system prompt, procedural memory is retrieved on demand (lower token cost), externally stored, and updatable from outcomes (success_rate drives rewrites). Use prompting for universal rules and procedural memory for task-specific recipes.
summary: Chapter shows procedural memory as a retrievable, updatable library of how-to recipes that complements semantic facts and static system prompts.
queries: what is procedural memory in LLM agents
how do agents learn and reuse skills

how does procedural memory work end to end

text: Procedural memory encodes skills (HOW to do something), not facts about the world. Contrast: a semantic fact says 'refund cap is $200' (WHAT is true). A procedural skill says 'When a refund > $200 is requested, look up customer tier first. Enterprise customers self-approve up to $500. Otherwise call escalate_human with urgency=Medium' (WHAT TO DO). The agent uses both: the fact gives the $200 cap, the procedure says what to do when the cap is exceeded.
summary: Procedural memory stores how-to recipes (skills) while semantic memory stores what-is-true facts, and agents use them together.

text: The recipe library is a small set of named workflows the customer-support agent reuses. Each recipe has a trigger condition and an ordered step list. Four example recipes: 1) Handle Refund Request - trigger: Customer asks for refund, 3 steps. 2) Resolve Password Issue - trigger: Login / password error, 4 steps. 3) Escalate Billing Dispute - trigger: Disputed invoice or charge, 2 steps. 4) Resolve Sync Failure - trigger: Data out-of-sync across devices, 5 steps. Each used to be a prompt-engineering exercise; once it stabilized, it got moved into procedural memory.
summary: A recipe library is a set of named, trigger-and-step workflows that an agent reuses for common tasks.

text: Recipe retrieval pipeline: a new ticket gets embedded; ANN search runs against the recipe library's trigger_embedding field; the top-1 match is surfaced (or 'no match' if similarity is below threshold). Five stages: New Ticket (free text) -> Embed (1024 floats) -> ANN Search (vs library) -> Top-1 (or no match) -> Use Recipe (scaffolding). The agent uses the retrieved recipe as a scaffold then adapts to specifics. If no recipe matches, agent falls back to first-principles reasoning, and that run becomes a candidate to mine into a new recipe.
summary: Agent retrieves a relevant recipe by embedding the ticket and running ANN search against stored trigger embeddings, falling back to reasoning when no match exists.

text:
A recipe is a structured record. Example shape:
{
  "id": "recipe-refund",
  "name": "Handle refund request",
  "trigger_embedding": "[1024 floats]",
  "steps": [
    "lookup_customer to get customer_id and tier",
    "if tier == Enterprise: auto-approve up to $500; else $200",
    "lookup_subscription for invoice_id",
    "process_refund(invoice_id, reason); if business_rule error: escalate_human"
  ],
  "success_rate": 0.92,
  "uses": 187
}
Trigger embedding is for retrieval; steps are for execution; success_rate and uses drive analytics and self-improvement. success_rate = 0.92 means 92% of runs produced a clean outcome. uses = 187 means the agent has run the recipe 187 times. A falling success_rate triggers a rewrite review.
summary: A recipe record stores an id, name, trigger embedding, ordered steps, success_rate, and uses count - enabling retrieval, execution, and self-improvement.

text: Prompting vs procedural memory comparison: 1) Where it lives - Prompting: inside the system prompt. Procedural: external store, retrieved on demand. 2) Token cost - Prompting: every conversation pays the prompt tokens. Procedural: only retrieved when relevant. 3) Can learn? - Prompting: static, cannot update from outcomes. Procedural: updatable, success_rate drives rewrite. 4) Best for - Prompting: universal rules (tone, format). Procedural: task-specific recipes. Prompting stays great for universal rules - tone, format, safety constraints. Procedural memory wins for task-specific recipes that change with real-world outcomes. Use both deliberately.
summary: Prompting is best for static universal rules while procedural memory is best for retrievable, learnable task-specific recipes - and serious agents use both.

Leaves = Raw Turn Batches (10 Turns Each). Mid = Per-Batch Summary. Top = Meta.

text: Chapter 26.12 covers summary memory and context window management. Long conversations exceed token budgets (8k window fills by turn 30 at 250 tokens/turn), forcing compression. Rolling summary compresses the oldest half whenever the window hits 50%, dropping back to 25%. Hierarchical summaries (leaves -> per-batch summaries -> meta-summary) handle week-long conversations. After summarization, retention uses Recency, Relevance, or Hybrid (top-K recent + top-K relevant), with Hybrid the production default. Compression triggers at fixed thresholds: 25% do nothing, 50% compress half, 75% aggressive + hybrid retrieval, 90% panic (drop all but summary + last 5 turns + top retrieved). The final prompt assembly stacks system prompt, memory block, conversation summary, recent turns, retrieved turns, working memory, and current user message.
summary: Overview of context window management: rolling and hierarchical summaries, recency/relevance/hybrid retention, threshold-based compression, and seven-layer prompt assembly.

text: 100 turns won't fit. Long conversations are not free. With an 8k token context window and ~250 tokens per turn, the window fills by turn 30. Past that, something has to give. Past turn 30, the agent must compress old turns, drop them, or both. The rest of this chapter is about HOW to manage that ceiling.
summary: An 8k context window holds only ~30 turns at 250 tokens each, forcing compression or dropping after that.

text: Compress the oldest half. The rolling-summary technique: when the window hits 50% capacity, summarize the oldest half into a 2-paragraph block. The summary replaces the raw turns. Capacity drops to 25%. Repeat every 50%. The model itself usually generates the summary (a 2-paragraph LLM call per compression cycle). The summary lives in the conversation history at the spot where the raw turns used to be.
summary: Rolling summary compresses the oldest half of context into a 2-paragraph block whenever the window hits 50% capacity.

text: Summaries of summaries. For week-long conversations, flat rolling summary degrades. The hierarchical approach organizes raw turns into batches (leaves), per-batch summaries (mid), and a meta-summary of the whole conversation (top). Hierarchical wins for week-long conversations because the meta-summary stays small while you can still zoom into specific batches when needed.
summary: Hierarchical summaries organize turns into leaf batches, per-batch summaries, and a top meta-summary for week-long conversations.
queries: Why does flat rolling summary degrade?

text: Most recent vs most relevant. After summarization, what stays in the prompt? Three strategies, each with a different tradeoff between continuity and topical fit. Recency: keep most-recent N turns raw, drop summary of older turns, good for continuity-heavy conversations. Relevance: keep turns most similar to current query, drop summary of less-relevant turns, good for long-range topical recall. Hybrid: keep top-K recent + top-K relevant, drop summary of everything else, best default in practice. Hybrid wins in production because most conversations have BOTH continuity needs (the user said 'this' earlier) AND topical recall needs (the user asked a similar thing 80 turns ago).
summary: Three retention strategies (Recency, Relevance, Hybrid) trade continuity vs topical fit; Hybrid is the production default.
queries: What stays in prompt after summarization?
How to pick turns from old history?
User said this earlier continuity need

text: Summarize at 50% capacity. Production agents trigger compression at fixed thresholds, not on demand. Four bands cover the production rule: 25% Do Nothing - plenty of room; 50% Compress Half - summarize oldest 25%; 75% Aggressive - summarize older half + start hybrid retrieval; 90% Panic - drop everything except summary + last 5 turns + top retrieved. Threshold-based compression is production-friendly because the cost is predictable. On-demand compression can hide behind a single user turn that suddenly explodes the window.
summary: Production agents trigger compression at fixed bands: 25% nothing, 50% compress, 75% aggressive, 90% panic with summary + last 5 turns.

text: Context is where real work happens. Memory plus context-engineering decide what the model sees on EVERY turn. Chapter 24.6 covered the prompt-assembly stack. The assembly order: 1. System Prompt: static + recipe from procedural memory. 2. Memory Block: retrieved episodes + profile card from semantic. 3. Conversation Summary: rolling / hierarchical summary of old turns. 4. Recent Turns: last N raw turns (recency). 5. Retrieved Turns: top-K relevant turns from older history (hybrid). 6. Working Memory: current scratchpad state. 7. Current User Message: what the user just said. Every layer comes from somewhere. Memory chapters supplied the sources; the assembly stack joins them into a single prompt; this chapter closes the loop with summarization that keeps it all under the token budget.
summary: The seven-layer prompt assembly order combines memory sources, summary, recent and retrieved turns, scratchpad, and the user message.

text: Chapter 27.1 (Why Multi-Agent?) explains when to split one agent into many. A single agent loaded with retrieve, classify, refund, troubleshoot, and escalate hits a performance ceiling because each role pulls the system prompt differently. Three motivations for going multi-agent: specialization (one agent per role with tighter prompt and 1-2 tools instead of 8), parallelism (independent sub-tasks like lookup_customer, lookup_subscription, search_kb run concurrently so wall-clock = max latency 1.6s instead of sum 4.2s, ~3x faster), and planner/worker separation (planner decomposes, workers execute single steps, neither crosses layers). Anti-patterns where single agent wins: single-step tasks, context-continuity tasks, latency-critical loops with 100ms SLA, and small dev teams. Decision rule: multi-agent IF specialization OR parallelism OR planner/worker fits; otherwise single agent.
summary: Overview of when multi-agent beats single agent: specialization, parallelism, planner/worker, plus anti-patterns.

text: Why one agent sometimes isn't enough. A single agent loaded with all customer-support roles - retrieve, classify, refund, troubleshoot, escalate - has a crowded system prompt. Each role pulls the prompt in a different direction. A performance ceiling appears. Each new role added to the system prompt costs precision on every other role. The ceiling is real: at some point, splitting roles into separate agents beats stuffing them into one.
summary: One agent given five different jobs hits a precision ceiling because roles compete inside one system prompt.

text: Specialization: one agent per role. Replace the crowded single agent with 3 focused agents. Each has a tighter system prompt, a smaller tool set, a clearer goal. Each does its job better than a generalist would. Specialists: (1) Billing Specialist - tools lookup_subscription, process_refund - prompt 'Handle refunds, invoices, tier changes'. (2) Troubleshooting Specialist - tools search_kb, lookup_customer - prompt 'Diagnose errors, outages, sync failures'. (3) Triage Router - tools lookup_customer - prompt 'Classify each ticket and route to specialist'. Each specialist has 1-2 tools, not the full 8-tool inventory. Smaller surface = fewer wrong-tool errors. The triage router picks which specialist a ticket goes to.
summary: Three specialist agents with tighter prompts and 1-2 tools each replace one generalist; a triage router routes tickets.

text: Run independent tasks at the same time. Some sub-tasks have no data dependencies. Run them on separate agents concurrently. Wall-clock cost is max(sub-task latency), not the sum. Dispatcher fans out to Agent A (lookup_customer, 1.2s), Agent B (lookup_subscription, 1.4s), Agent C (search_kb, 1.6s). Sequential = 1.2 + 1.4 + 1.6 = 4.2s. Parallel = max(1.2, 1.4, 1.6) = 1.6s. Almost 3x faster. The win compounds as you add more independent sub-tasks.
summary: Independent agents run in parallel so wall-clock = slowest leg (1.6s) not sum (4.2s), ~3x speedup.

text: Planner vs Worker. Some agents are good at decomposing tasks; others are good at executing single steps. Separating these roles produces a 2-layer setup where each agent only does its own job. Planner Agent at top decomposes the task. Worker 1 does Lookup Customer; Worker 2 does Process Refund. The planner does NOT execute steps. The workers do NOT decompose. Each agent stays on its layer. This separation makes both layers more reliable than one mixed agent.
summary: Two-layer architecture: planner decomposes tasks, workers execute single steps, neither crosses layers.
queries: planner doesn't execute, worker doesn't decompose

text: Don't multi-agent a small problem. Multi-agent costs something: more LLM calls, more state to track, more places for failure. Four anti-patterns where one agent is right: (1) Single-Step Tasks - just call the tool; adding a second agent doubles latency for zero gain. (2) Context Continuity Tasks - if the same context carries through every step, hand-off loses information. (3) Latency-Critical Loops - each agent adds 1-2 seconds of reasoning; a 100ms SLA loop cannot afford this. (4) Small Dev Teams - multi-agent failures (chapter 27.6) are hard to debug; a 2-person team will drown. Decision rule: Multi-agent IF specialization OR parallelism OR planner/worker fits the workload. Otherwise single agent.
summary: Four anti-patterns where single agent wins, plus the decision rule: multi-agent only if specialization, parallelism, or planner/worker fits.
queries: decision rule single vs multi agent

text: Chapter 27.2 covers the orchestrator-worker pattern: a hub-and-spoke topology with a central orchestrator that plans, dispatches, and aggregates while N workers each handle one isolated sub-task. The orchestrator runs three phases (Plan, Dispatch, Aggregate) as separate LLM calls. Workers run their own reason-act-observe loop within their tool set and return structured results; they never talk to each other. A concrete Ticket T3 trace shows 3 parallel workers (search_kb x2 + lookup_customer) cutting latency to max(worker) + plan + aggregate. Three aggregation patterns - concatenation, voting, synthesis - each fit different output types, with synthesis being the production default.
summary: Chapter-level summary of the orchestrator-worker multi-agent pattern, its phases, worker isolation, parallel latency, and aggregation choices.

text: The orchestrator-worker pattern is the most common production multi-agent shape. A central orchestrator plans and aggregates; workers each handle one sub-task. Hub and spoke topology: workers only talk to the orchestrator, never to each other. The hub-and-spoke shape means the orchestrator owns coordination cost; workers stay simple. No edges between workers means no peer-to-peer chatter and no shared-state corruption.
summary: Introduces orchestrator-worker as a hub-and-spoke multi-agent topology where a central planner coordinates isolated workers.

text: The orchestrator runs three phases per task. Each phase is a separate LLM call with a distinct system prompt - the same agent loops the model through three different mindsets. PLAN: Break the incoming task into 3-5 sub-tasks each workable in isolation. DISPATCH: Send each sub-task to its assigned worker agent. AGGREGATE: When workers return, merge results into a single coherent answer. The 3 phases give the orchestrator 3 separate minds: one to plan, one to dispatch, one to aggregate. Each is simpler than a single do-everything prompt.
summary: Breaks the orchestrator's job into three distinct LLM phases: plan, dispatch, aggregate.

text: Each worker takes one sub-task description, runs its own agent loop within its tool set, and returns a structured result. Workers do NOT talk to each other - only to the orchestrator. Example: input sub-task 'Lookup customer tier for c-9924' -> Worker Agent runs internal loop Reason -> Act -> Observe within its tool set only -> returns structured result { tier: 'Pro', usage: 'heavy' }. If Worker A's result feeds Worker B's input, the orchestrator does that chaining. This is what makes parallel dispatch safe.

text: Ticket T3 = 'Dashboard slow + 500 errors'. The orchestrator plans 3 parallel sub-tasks, dispatches them to workers, then aggregates. STEP 1 Orchestrator: Receives T3. Plans 3 sub-tasks: A) search_kb for 'Dashboard Slow'; B) search_kb for '500 Errors'; C) lookup_customer for usage tier. STEP 2 Worker A: Returns top-3 KB articles on dashboard performance. STEP 3 Worker B: Returns top-3 KB articles on 500 errors. STEP 4 Worker C: Returns Alice's tier (Pro) + usage stats (heavy dashboard user). STEP 5 Orchestrator: Aggregates - composes a single answer combining KB hits + customer context. Recommends dashboard optimization and escalates 500 errors to engineering. Workers A, B, and C ran in parallel - steps 2-4 happened roughly at the same time. Total latency = max(worker time) + plan + aggregate, not sum.

text: Aggregation - merging worker outputs into a single answer - has three common patterns. CONCATENATION: Stitch all worker outputs together in order. Simple but verbose. Best for quick prototypes; outputs already well-sectioned. VOTING: Workers each propose; orchestrator picks majority. Good for classification. Best for categorical outputs and hallucination reduction. SYNTHESIS: Orchestrator reads all worker outputs and writes a single coherent answer. Most common, most expensive. Best as production default for natural-language answers. Synthesis is the production default for natural-language answers. Voting wins when workers are doing classification. Concatenation is fine for structured outputs that need no rephrasing.
summary: Compares three aggregation patterns - concatenation, voting, synthesis - and when to pick each.

text: Hierarchical multi-agent is a tree of supervisors delegating to sub-supervisors and leaf specialists, with three levels as the sweet spot. Each supervisor at every level runs the same five-step loop: Receive, Plan, Route Down, Aggregate, Return Up, with routing as the new responsibility versus orchestrator-worker. Pick hierarchical when the domain has genuine sub-specialties (billing, troubleshooting); flatten when depth exceeds 3. A concrete customer-support tree has Support Supervisor over Billing, Troubleshooting, and Escalation sub-supervisors with narrow-tool leaves. Failures bubble up: a leaf escalates to its supervisor, which may retry or escalate further, eventually reaching the Escalation Supervisor that calls escalate_human - the safety net that prevents silent stalls.
summary: Chapter overview of hierarchical multi-agent: tree topology, per-supervisor five-step loop, decision rule vs orchestrator-worker, support-agent example, and bottom-up escalation safety net.

text: Hierarchical multi-agent is a tree with multiple levels of delegation. A top supervisor delegates to sub-supervisors which delegate to leaf specialists. Each leaf has 1-2 narrow tools. This is different from orchestrator-worker which has only a single level of workers. Three levels is the practical sweet spot. Two levels is just orchestrator-worker renamed. Four levels usually signals over-engineering.
summary: Hierarchical agents form a multi-level tree of supervisors delegating down to leaf specialists, with three levels as the sweet spot.

text: At each level a supervisor does five things: Receive a sub-task from its parent. Plan whether a single child handles it or break it into smaller sub-tasks. Route Down by picking the child agent(s) best matched to the sub-task. Aggregate by waiting for children and combining their results. Return Up the aggregated result to the parent supervisor. Every supervisor performs the same five-step loop at its own level. Routing is the new responsibility versus orchestrator-worker - a supervisor must know which child agent fits which sub-task type.
summary: Each supervisor runs a five-step loop - Receive, Plan, Route Down, Aggregate, Return Up - with routing as the new responsibility.

text: Choosing hierarchical over orchestrator-worker is a domain shape question. Pick Orchestrator-Worker when workers are peer specialists at one level, example: 3 knowledge base search workers run in parallel. Pick Hierarchical when the domain has clear sub-domains, example: billing has refund / invoice / tier-change as sub-areas. Avoid Hierarchical when tree depth > 3 levels - usually means over-engineering; flatten the design. Most production customer-support agents fit hierarchical because billing and troubleshooting are genuinely different sub-domains with different tools and policies.
summary: Pick hierarchical when the domain has real sub-specialties; pick orchestrator-worker for peer workers; flatten anything past three levels.
queries: when to use hierarchical agents vs orchestrator-worker

text: The customer-support agent fits hierarchical cleanly. Root: Support Supervisor - all-tickets entry point that classifies and routes. Sub-supervisors: Billing Supervisor with leaves Refund Specialist, Invoice Specialist, Tier-Change Specialist. Troubleshooting Supervisor with leaves Database Specialist, Network Specialist. Escalation Supervisor with leaf Human Hand-Off (escalate_human). Each leaf specialist uses 1-3 tools from the canonical 8-tool inventory: refund specialists use process_refund + lookup_subscription, network specialists use search_kb, escalation calls escalate_human.
summary: Concrete support tree: Support Supervisor routes to Billing, Troubleshooting, Escalation sub-supervisors, each with specialist leaves using narrow tool sets.

text: A leaf specialist that cannot finish escalates up to its supervisor. The supervisor decides: retry with a different leaf, or escalate to its own parent. Eventually a top-level escalation reaches the Escalation Supervisor which calls escalate_human. Worked trace: Refund Specialist hits business_rule error on a $250 refund and escalates up. Billing Supervisor considers Tier-Change Leaf, rules it out, escalates up. Support Supervisor routes to Escalation Supervisor. Escalation Supervisor calls escalate_human with full trace. Escalation up the tree is the safety net; without it a leaf failure becomes a silent stall.

text: Hand-offs (Swarm pattern) let an agent finish its part and return the next agent, with the runtime switching control directly - no central orchestrator. Each agent declares a handoffs list of allowed peers. The hand-off package carries full conversation history, a working memory snapshot, and a hand-off reason so the receiver picks up cleanly. Example T4 ticket flows Triage to Billing to Escalation, each step carrying full context. Topology is a peer ring/graph - fitting equal-level domains, in contrast with tree topology for nested domains.

text: Hand-offs use the Swarm pattern (now in the OpenAI Agents SDK): instead of an orchestrator routing sub-tasks, an agent finishes its part and RETURNS the next agent. The runtime then switches control directly. No central coordinator. Orchestrator-Worker style (delegation): orchestrator picks who handles next, waits for result, picks again - central control. Swarm/Hand-off style: agent A returns 'next = agent B', loop switches control directly, no central coordinator. Hand-off is decentralized; orchestration is centralized. Both ship to production - the pick depends on whether you want a single brain or peer-to-peer routing.
summary: Hand-offs are the Swarm pattern where an agent returns the next agent to run, decentralizing control versus a central orchestrator.

text:
Agents as routes. Each agent declares which other agents it can hand off to via a handoffs field - similar to routes in a web framework. Shape:

Agent {
  name: "triage",
  instructions: "Classify intent. Hand off to billing for refund/invoice, troubleshooting for errors.",
  handoffs: [billing_agent, troubleshooting_agent]
}

name and instructions are this agent's own identity. handoffs is the set of other agents this agent can transfer control to - basically a small DAG of allowed transitions.
summary: Each Swarm agent declares a handoffs list naming the other agents it is allowed to transfer control to, forming a small DAG of routes.

text: What travels with the hand-off. When agent A hands off to agent B, B does not start fresh. It receives a hand-off package with everything it needs to pick up cleanly. Package parts: (1) Full Conversation History - all turns since session start; (2) Working Memory Snapshot - current scratchpad state at hand-off time; (3) Hand-Off Reason - why the sender decided to hand off (e.g. 'Classified As Billing'). The receiving agent reads the entire package before its first reasoning step. No re-litigation from scratch. The conversation history plus working memory plus reason is the contract.
summary: A hand-off carries full conversation history, working memory snapshot, and a hand-off reason so the receiving agent can pick up without restarting.

text: Chapter 27.5 covers Critic / Debate / Reflection-as-Multi-Agent patterns. A critic is a separate agent (not the worker self-critiquing) that reviews the worker's draft with a skeptical system prompt and no shared memory. The critic-revise loop has the worker draft, the critic score 0-10, revise if below threshold (e.g. <7), re-score, looping until score is high enough or a retry cap is hit. Debate is a 3-agent variant: Agent Pro argues for, Agent Con argues against, and a Judge agent reads both and picks. Example: a refund agent draft is caught by a policy critic that notices an invoice is >30 days old and revises $180 down to $120 (60% policy). Critic and debate add LLM cost and latency (critic = +1 call per draft, debate = +3 calls per round), so use critic for high-stakes mutations like refunds and escalations, debate for contested decisions with multiple readings, skip both for low-stakes lookups and classifications.
summary: Chapter overview of critic and debate multi-agent patterns: separate critic reviews worker drafts, bounded revise loops, 3-agent Pro/Con/Judge debate, refund policy critic example, and when to pay the cost.

text: A Second Agent Checks The First. Critic is reflection-as-multi-agent: instead of one agent self-critiquing (as in 26.5), a separate critic agent reviews the worker's draft. Same idea, more isolation - the critic does not share working memory with the worker. The critic has a different system prompt - it is told to be SKEPTICAL of the worker's draft. The separation makes it less likely the model just rubber-stamps its own earlier output. The visual shows a Worker Agent on the left producing a draft answer for the customer, passing the Draft to a Critic Agent on the right that reads the draft plus the question and scores or rewrites it.

text: Loop: Draft - Critique - Revise. The critic-revise loop is bounded. Worker drafts; critic scores 0-10 with reasoning; if below threshold, worker revises with critic's feedback; critic re-scores. Loop until score is high enough OR the retry cap is hit. The diagram shows four stations in a circle: Worker Drafts, Critic Scores 0-10, If < 7 Revise, Critic Re-Scores, with arrows clockwise back to the worker. The retry cap matters. Without it, a worker that can't please the critic loops forever - one of the cost-runaway failure modes covered in 27.6.
summary: Explains the bounded critic-revise loop: worker drafts, critic scores 0-10, revise if below threshold like 7, re-score, capped by retry limit to prevent infinite loops.

Debate triples the LLM bill vs single-agent. Use it sparingly - high-stakes contested decisions where you genuinely want two voices, not noise-reduction on a routine task.

text: Policy Critic For The Refund Agent. Concrete example: a refund agent makes a draft decision; a policy critic catches that the invoice is >30 days old; the refund gets revised down. Trace: Step 1 - Customer: requests refund for Invoice #INV-9924, $180. Step 2 - Refund Agent: 'Approved, processing $180 refund now.' Step 3 - Policy Critic: 'Hold. Check if invoice was paid > 30 days ago. If yes, partial refund only (60% policy for >30 days).' Step 4 - Refund Agent (Revised): 'Confirmed >30 days. Approved for $120 (60% policy).' The critic caught $60 the worker would have over-refunded. Multiplied across thousands of refunds per month, the policy critic pays for its own LLM bill many times over.

text: Critic Adds Cost; Pick Battles. Critic and debate are not free. Each extra agent doubles or triples the LLM bill and adds latency. Pick where they earn their cost, skip where they do not. Three patterns: Use Critic - when: high-stakes mutations; examples: refunds, escalations, contract changes; cost: +1 LLM call per draft. Use Debate - when: contested decisions with multiple readings; examples: policy edge cases, customer-vs-business tension; cost: +3 LLM calls per round. Skip Both - when: simple, low-stakes tasks; examples: lookups, classifications, factual recall; cost: no overhead. Rule of thumb: if a wrong answer is worth less than the extra LLM cost, skip the critic. If a wrong answer can cost $200 in refund overage, paying $0.01 for a critic call is the easiest trade you will ever make.
summary: Decision guide for when to use Critic (+1 call, high-stakes mutations), Debate (+3 calls, contested decisions), or Skip both (low-stakes tasks) - based on whether wrong-answer cost exceeds extra LLM cost.

text: Multi-agent systems fail in four characteristic ways in production: Drift (agents interpret the user goal differently), Infinite Loop (hand-offs ping-pong forever), Deadlock (agents wait on each other circularly), and Cost Runaway (unbounded recursion drives spend vertical). Each has a distinct signal and threshold to alert on: drift detected by comparing plan summaries across agents, infinite loop by hand-off rate > 5 per turn, deadlock by both agents WAITING for > 30 seconds, cost runaway by > 3x baseline spend per turn. Fixes: shared plan summary, hand-off counter + chain history, timeouts on inter-agent waits, recursion depth cap + per-turn spend cap + circuit breaker.
summary: Chapter overview: four multi-agent failure modes, their signals, thresholds, and fixes.
queries: what are common multi-agent failure modes

what to monitor in multi-agent systems

text: How Multi-Agent Falls Apart. Four failure modes show up in production multi-agent systems. Each has a distinct signature, a distinct signal, and a distinct fix. Knowing them is the difference between shipping multi-agent and leaving it in the lab. The four modes are: Drift (agents disagree on the goal), Infinite Loop (agents bounce hand-offs forever), Deadlock (agents wait on each other), and Cost Runaway (unbounded recursion, spend vertical). Each subsequent sub-step zooms into one mode; the final sub-step maps each failure to a production alerting threshold.
summary: Introduces the four canonical multi-agent failure modes and frames each as having a signature, signal, and fix.
queries: what are the four multi-agent failure modes
why multi-agent is hard to operate

text: Drift: Agents Pull In Different Directions. Drift = different agents in the same run interpret the user's intent differently. They each work hard toward their own version of the goal. The final answer is incoherent. Example: ticket T4 reaches three agents - Agent A interprets it as 'Cancel subscription now', Agent B as 'Refund the last invoice', Agent C as 'Save the customer (anti-churn)'. Fix drift with a shared 'plan summary' output that every agent must read before its first reasoning step. If two agents disagree about the goal, the alert fires before any tools run.
summary: Defines drift as agents diverging on goal interpretation, illustrated with a ticket interpreted three different ways.
queries: what is drift in multi-agent systems
alert when agents disagree on intent
cancel vs refund vs save customer example

text: Hand-Off Ping-Pong (Infinite Loop). Infinite loop = hand-off ring spins forever. Triage hands off to billing; billing hands off back to triage; triage hands off to billing again. No agent commits to handling the ticket. Symptom: hand-off rate per turn does not decrease (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, ...). Never decreases = infinite loop detected. Fix with a hand-off counter per turn plus a hand-off chain history. If the same edge fires twice in a row, halt and escalate to human.
summary: Explains hand-off ping-pong infinite loops between agents and the counter + chain-history fix.
queries: how to detect infinite loop multi-agent

text: Deadlock: Two Agents Wait Forever. Deadlock = circular wait. Agent A is waiting on Agent B's result. Agent B is waiting on Agent A's result. Neither makes progress. Symptom: both agents in WAITING state simultaneously for > 30 seconds with no state change. Fix deadlock with timeouts on every inter-agent wait. If A doesn't hear from B within N seconds, A surfaces 'timeout waiting on B' and the runtime breaks the cycle.
summary: Defines deadlock as circular waiting between agents and prescribes per-wait timeouts as the fix.
queries: two agents waiting on each other
agent A waits on B example

text: Unbounded Recursion: Cost Goes Vertical. Cost runaway = a parent agent spawns sub-agents; each sub-agent spawns more; cost grows exponentially. By iteration 20, a normal $0.50 run is $50. Signal: spend rate climbing per turn without a corresponding answer-quality gain. Fix with a recursion depth cap, a per-turn spend cap, and a circuit breaker that halts when either is hit. If the agent decides to spawn 12 sub-agents, something is wrong with the planner prompt.
summary: Explains exponential cost blow-up from recursive sub-agent spawning and the depth/spend cap + circuit breaker fix.
queries: what is cost runaway in multi-agent

text: What To Alert On. Each failure mode has a production signal. Each signal has a threshold. Each threshold crossing should page a human. Drift: planner outputs disagree on intent; threshold = detected by comparing plan summaries across agents. Infinite Loop: hand-off rate not decreasing; threshold = > 5 hand-offs in a single turn. Deadlock: agents in WAITING state; threshold = > 30 seconds with no state change. Cost Runaway: spend rate per turn climbing; threshold = > 3x baseline cost per turn. Every multi-agent system in production must monitor all four. Skipping any one is a bet that the corresponding failure mode will never bite - and it always eventually does. Alert thresholds are the price of multi-agent.
summary: Maps each failure mode to its production signal and exact alerting threshold.

text: Chapter 27.7 Agentic RAG. Naive RAG is a one-shot pipeline (query, retrieve, generate). Agentic RAG wraps retrieval in a four-station loop: Search, Judge, Refine, Repeat - terminating when Judge says enough evidence is collected or max iterations is hit. The Judge is the critical control: too lenient gives naive RAG with extra steps, too strict causes cost runaway. Query rewriting sharpens each iteration. Worked example: 'Find all customer-impact issues past 90 days' walks through 5 steps and 3 query rewrites adding severity > 2 and status = resolved filters, ending in an aggregated 8-incident table. Decision: naive RAG for factoid lookups (1 retrieval, cheap); agentic RAG for research, multi-hop, find-all, compare queries (3-10x cost, slow). Frames agentic RAG as the closing multi-agent pattern; mechanics cross-reference Sections 19.2 and 22.8.
summary: Chapter overview: agentic RAG turns one-shot retrieval into a Search-Judge-Refine loop, costing 3-10x naive RAG but unlocking research-style queries.

text: Retrieve Once vs Retrieve In A Loop. Naive RAG retrieves once and generates an answer. Agentic RAG puts retrieval inside an agent loop: search, judge the results, refine the query, search again. Iterative instead of one-shot. Naive RAG is a pipeline; agentic RAG is a loop with retrieval inside. Section 19.2 covers the naive pipeline; Section 22.8 covers the loop mechanics. Framed here as a multi-agent pattern closing the multi-agent chapters.

text: Search, Judge, Refine, Repeat. The agentic RAG loop has four stations. Search returns candidates; Judge asks 'do I have enough? what is missing?'; Refine rewrites the query with the gap in mind; Search again. Loop until Judge says 'done' or max iterations is hit. Each station is its own LLM call (or its own internal agent). The Judge is the most critical - if it always says 'I have enough', you have naive RAG with extra steps. If it never says 'enough', you have cost runaway.
summary: Describes the four stations of the agentic RAG loop and why the Judge step is the critical control point.

text: The Agent Rewrites Its Own Query. Query rewriting is the smartest part of the loop. The Judge identifies what is missing; the Refine step turns that gap into a sharper next query. Three iterations on a real research question. Iteration 1: query 'Customer Issues Past 90 Days' returns 5 generic articles, too broad. Iteration 2: query 'Customer-Impact Incidents Past 90 Days, Severity > 2' returns better hits, 8 incidents. Iteration 3: query 'Customer-Impact Incidents Past 90 Days, Severity > 2, Status = Resolved' returns best hits, 8 incidents all with resolution details. Each iteration narrows toward what the Judge wanted. By iteration 3, the query is so specific that the top-K results are all relevant. The agent has effectively learned the query specification by doing the search.
summary: Walks through three query-rewrite iterations narrowing a vague research question into a precise filtered query.

text: Example: Customer-Impact Issues Past 90 Days. The research-style query the agent answers across the full loop. Step 1 Initial Query: 'Find all customer-impact issues in the past 90 days.' Step 2 Search Top-5: vector search returns 5 knowledge base articles. Step 3 Judge: only 2 are customer-impact. Need broader retrieval with severity filter. Step 4 Rewrite + Search: query adds severity > 2 filter. New search returns 8 articles. Step 5 Final Aggregation: 8 incidents synthesized into a summary table with dates, severities, resolutions. The final aggregation is a structured artifact: 8 incidents with dates, severity ratings, and resolution summaries. Naive RAG could not have produced this - it has no way to broaden a search when the first attempt is too narrow.
summary: Five-step walkthrough of an agentic RAG run producing an aggregated incident summary that naive RAG could not.

text: When To Iterate Retrieval. Agentic RAG costs 3-10x more than naive RAG. Use it when answer quality matters more than latency. Otherwise stick with the naive pipeline (Section 19.2). Naive RAG: pick for direct factoid lookups and single-doc questions like 'What is the refund policy?' Cost: 1 retrieval, 1 generation. Cheap. Agentic RAG: pick for research questions, multi-hop, find-all or compare queries like 'Find all customer-impact issues past 90 days.' Cost: 3-10x naive cost. Slow. Naive RAG pipeline covered in Section 19.2; agentic RAG mechanics in Section 22.8; framed here as a multi-agent pattern closing out the multi-agent chapters.
summary: Decision guide between naive and agentic RAG based on query type, cost multiplier, and latency tradeoffs.

text: Chapter 28.1 - Why Eval Agents Differently. Agents need different eval than pure LLMs along three axes: non-determinism, multi-step traces, and silent failures. Real production incidents ($50K unauthorized refunds, hallucinated policies, chitchat drift, cross-tenant leaks) show what happens without eval. Production needs both offline eval (frozen golden set, before every deploy, catches regressions) and online eval (1-5% live traffic sampling, continuous, catches drift). Three failure modes still need humans: tone, hidden hallucinations, cross-session drift - budget 20-50 human-reviewed traces per week per agent. The full pipeline has five stages: eval set, per-trace step grading, end-to-end LLM-as-judge, online sampling + drift signal, alerting + rollback.
summary: Chapter overview: why agent eval differs from LLM eval, real incidents, offline vs online loops, human review categories, and the five-stage eval pipeline.
queries: production incidents and eval pipeline summary
agent eval differences from LLM eval summary
why agents need different eval recap

text: Three reasons agents are harder to eval than pure LLMs. A pure LLM eval grades input vs output - one shot, one score. Agents differ on three structural axes that make naive eval miss most production failures. 1) Non-Determinism: the same ticket can take different traces on different runs, so you cannot compare outputs directly. 2) Multi-Step: eight tool calls in a row means eight places to fail; the final answer hides where the trace went wrong. 3) Silent Failure: the agent does not crash - it calls the wrong tool, sends the wrong email, or misses an escalation without raising an error. Every production failure mode maps to at least one of these axes. Eval design has to address all three or production incidents slip through.
summary: Agents are harder to eval than LLMs along three axes: non-determinism, multi-step traces, and silent failures.

text: Real production incidents from teams that shipped agents without an eval pipeline. Every one went unnoticed for days or weeks. Unauthorized Refunds: a refund agent issued $50K in unauthorized refunds over a weekend before anyone noticed. Hallucinated Policy: a support agent confidently quoted a non-existent refund policy for 3 weeks of production traffic. Drift to Chitchat: a multi-agent system drifted from billing questions to open-ended chitchat over 2 days. Cross-Tenant Data Leak: a tool security regression let one customer's profile leak into another's active session. Each incident shows the same shape: silent failure + production traffic + late detection. Eval is the only tool that converts late detection into same-day.
summary: Four real production incidents - $50K unauthorized refunds, hallucinated policy, chitchat drift, cross-tenant leak - all silent failures caught late without eval.
queries: what happens when you ship agents without eval
agent ran for weeks before noticed

text: Offline (before ship) vs Online (after ship) eval. Two complementary loops. Offline grades a frozen golden set before every deploy. Online samples real production traffic continuously. Production agents need BOTH; either alone leaves a gap. Comparison: Input - offline uses a frozen golden eval set, online uses sampled live traffic. Cadence - offline runs before every deploy, online runs continuously at 1-5% sampling. Cost - offline is cheap per run on a small set, online is expensive at scale. Catches - offline catches regressions on known cases, online catches drift, new failure modes, and the real distribution. Offline tells you if known cases regressed. Online tells you if the actual production distribution shifted. Skip one and you ship blind on that dimension.
summary: Offline eval grades a frozen golden set pre-deploy; online eval samples 1-5% of live traffic continuously - both required.

difference between offline and online agent eval

text: Some failure modes need humans. Automated judges are cheap but blind on three categories. Plan budget for a small but permanent human review loop on every production agent. 1) Tone Failures: rude, robotic, condescending responses need human spot-check. 2) Hidden Hallucinations: invented policy the judge cannot detect because the judge does not know the policy either; needs a RAG-grounded judge plus human. 3) Cross-Session Drift: long-term personality or style drift across many sessions; needs a human reviewer looking at the whole population. Rule of thumb: 20-50 human-reviewed traces per week per agent. Cheap relative to a single production incident, and the only signal the judge cannot produce.
summary: Three failure modes - tone, hidden hallucinations, cross-session drift - require human review; budget 20-50 traces per week per agent.

text: What a full pipeline looks like. Five stages, each handled by a later chapter. Stage 1 - Eval Set: Golden + Adversarial + Regression Cases (chapter 28.5). Stage 2 - Per-Trace Step Grading: Score Each Tool Call (chapter 28.4). Stage 3 - End-To-End Judge: Score Final Answer With LLM-As-Judge (chapter 28.3). Stage 4 - Online Sampling: Grade 1-5% Of Production Traffic + Drift Signal (chapter 28.5). Stage 5 - Alerting + Rollback: Page Engineer When Quality Drops (Ops). Map: Stage 2 = 28.4, Stage 3 = 28.3, Stage 1 + Stage 4 = 28.5. The pipeline costs more than a single judge call, but it is the only structure that catches the three failure modes (non-determinism, multi-step, silent failure) before they reach a customer.
summary: Five-stage agent eval pipeline: eval set, per-trace step grading, end-to-end judge, online sampling, alerting + rollback.
queries: pipeline that catches non-determinism multi-step silent failure

text: Chapter 28.2 Eval Dimensions teaches the four axes used to grade production agents: correctness (resolution rate > 90%), latency (P95 < 8s), cost (< $0.50 per ticket), and safety (refusal rate > 99%). Each axis gets its own metric, target, and example. The chapter ends with a composite trace score: 0.5*correctness + 0.2*latency_score + 0.2*cost_score + 0.1*safety_score, target > 0.85.
summary: Four-axis agent evaluation framework covering correctness, latency, cost, safety, plus a weighted composite trace score.

text: Production agents are graded on four axes simultaneously: Correctness, Latency, Cost, and Safety. A trace that scores 1.0 on correctness but blows the latency budget or leaks a refund is still a failure. Treat all four as first-class. Axis cards: Correctness target > 90% resolution, weight 0.5. Latency target P95 < 8s, weight 0.2. Cost target < $0.50 / ticket, weight 0.2. Safety target > 99% refusal, weight 0.1. The radar chart shows a sample agent run scoring 0.92 correctness, 0.78 latency, 0.84 cost, 0.97 safety, with a green ring marking the target zone (score > 0.85).
summary: Introduces the four eval axes with their targets and weights and shows a sample agent run on a radar chart.

text: Correctness: Did the task complete? Correctness is binary at the task level. Either the user's stated goal was achieved with no incorrect side effects, or the trace counts as a failure even if it looked plausible. Definition: Task Completed = User Goal Achieved AND No Incorrect Side Effects. Metric: Resolution Rate = Correct Traces / Total Traces. Target > 90%. Example T2: reset password + update email - agent called reset_password and update_email, both effects persisted, user confirmation email sent. Result: CORRECT. Counter-example T4 (refund + cancel): correctness fails if the agent issued the refund but skipped the cancel, even if the user got a polite response.
summary: Defines correctness as a binary task-completion check with resolution rate > 90% as target, illustrated by reset_password and refund+cancel examples.

text: Latency: How long did it take? Latency is the wall-clock time from user message to final answer. Average alone hides the bad tail. Track percentiles: P50, P95, P99. The tail is where users churn. Sample histogram: P50 = 3.2s, P95 = 7.5s, P99 = 14s, target P95 < 8s. Latency budget for a support ticket: 1.5s LLM call x 3 iterations + 200ms tool call x 6 = 5.7s. The remainder (under 8s P95) absorbs network jitter and retries.
summary: Explains agent latency as wall-clock duration measured by P50/P95/P99 percentiles with an example budget of 5.7s under an 8s P95 target.

how to fit under 8 second target

text: Cost: How much did each trace consume? Cost is the dollar amount per trace. Input tokens are cheap, output tokens cost 5x more, and tool calls add their own infrastructure cost. Runaway iterations are the number-one source of cost overshoots. Breakdown: Input Tokens $0.06 + Output Tokens $0.20 + Tool Calls $0.12 = $0.38 / ticket. Target: $0.50. Runaway iteration risk: each extra loop adds $0.20 in output tokens. Cap max iterations (10-20 per loop termination policy from 26.6) and you cap the worst-case cost. Without the cap, a 100-step runaway can hit $5 on a single trace.
summary: Breaks down per-trace cost into input tokens, output tokens, and tool calls with an example $0.38 total against a $0.50 target and warns about runaway iteration cost.
queries: how to measure agent cost per trace
input vs output token cost ratio
how much does an agent loop cost

text: Safety: Did the agent refuse what it should? Safety has four sub-metrics, each with a target and a customer-support example. Refusal Rate (Prohibited Actions) target > 99%: agent refuses to issue refunds above policy limit without manager approval. False Refusal Rate target < 1%: agent should not reject legitimate requests by mistake. Escalation Rate (Refund > $200) target 100%: every large refund must route to a human reviewer. Prompt-Injection Detection target > 95%: agent catches user inputs trying to override the system prompt. Production agents that fail safety even at 99% will still produce dozens of incidents per million traces. For sensitive verticals push to 99.9%+.
summary: Lists the four safety sub-metrics (refusal rate, false refusal, escalation, prompt injection detection) with targets and customer-support examples.
queries: what is refusal rate in agent eval

text: Composite Trace Score: stakeholders want a single trace score. Weight the four axes; correctness dominates for support agents but weights are tunable per use case. Trigger an alert when the composite drops below the target line. Formula: trace_score = 0.5 * correctness + 0.2 * latency_score + 0.2 * cost_score + 0.1 * safety_score. Each component normalized 0-1. Weights tunable per use case. Target composite > 0.85. Example sample run from sub=0: 0.5 * 0.92 + 0.2 * 0.78 + 0.2 * 0.84 + 0.1 * 0.97 = 0.885 > 0.85 (passes).
summary: Defines weighted composite trace score formula and shows it passing at 0.885 on the sample run from sub=0.
queries: how to combine four axes into one score

text: LLM-as-Judge uses one LLM to grade another LLM's output. Two scoring styles dominate: pairwise (A vs B, picks better) and scalar (0-10 score with justification). Scalar is the production default. A written rubric with explicit ranges per criterion cuts variance roughly in half. The canonical customer-support rubric totals 10 points across Correctness (0-3), Completeness (0-2), Tone (0-2), Citations (0-2), Escalation (0-1). Three known biases must be mitigated: length bias (longer = higher score, mitigate via length-normalization or truncation), position bias (first answer favored 5-10%, mitigate via randomization and averaging both orderings), and self-preference (judge from model X rates X higher, mitigate by using a different model family). Calibrate against human annotators on 50-100 traces; require correlation > 0.7; re-calibrate quarterly. Judge output must be machine-readable JSON with locked field names. Same technique extends from RAG (faithfulness, answer-relevance) in section 23.2 to agent-level evals (task completion, trace correctness, multi-step tools).
summary: Chapter overview of LLM-as-Judge: pairwise vs scalar scoring, rubric design, three biases, calibration, JSON output, and agent extension.

text: LLM-as-Judge means using one LLM to grade another LLM's output. Two scoring styles dominate production. Pairwise: the judge sees Answer A and Answer B side by side and picks which is better. Best for ranking two models or two prompt variants head-to-head. Weakness: cannot express absolute quality, reports only relative wins. Scalar: judge scores one answer from 0 to 10 with a written justification. Best for absolute quality tracking, dashboards, regression alerts. Weakness: more variance across runs, needs a rubric to reduce drift. Pairwise is great for model selection sprints. Scalar is the production default for ongoing trace quality dashboards.
summary: Two grading styles: pairwise (A vs B, relative) for model selection, scalar (0-10 with rubric) for production dashboards.

text: Without a rubric, scalar scores drift week over week. With a rubric the judge has anchors. Customer-support judge rubric (total = 10 points): Correctness (0-3): answer matches the policy / knowledge base; 3 = exact, 0 = contradicts policy. Completeness (0-2): answer addresses every part of the user question. Tone (0-2): friendly + professional, penalize condescension or robot-speak. Citations (0-2): policy references included when applicable. Escalation (0-1): correctly escalated when outside scope (refund > $200, account lock). Explicit ranges plus per-criterion definitions cut judge variance by roughly half compared to a bare 'score this 0-10' prompt without anchors. The rubric is the most valuable artifact in the eval pipeline.
summary: Five-criterion customer-support rubric totaling 10 points with explicit ranges anchors scalar scores and halves variance.
queries: How to write a rubric for an LLM judge?
What criteria should an LLM judge use?

Why does the judge need explicit ranges?
How much does a rubric reduce variance?
Why is the rubric the most valuable artifact?

text: LLM judges have three known biases, all must be mitigated before trusting the dashboard. Length Bias: longer answers get higher scores even when content is identical. Mitigation: length-normalize scores or truncate to a standard budget before judging. Position Bias: in pairwise, the FIRST answer is favored 5-10% of the time regardless of quality. Mitigation: randomize position per run and average across both orderings. Self-Preference: a judge using model X rates model X's outputs higher than other models. Mitigation: use a judge from a different model family, diversify judge mix periodically. Three biases, three mitigations: length normalization, position randomization, judge diversification. None are optional in production.
summary: Three judge biases - length, position, self-preference - and their mitigations: normalization, randomization, model diversification.
queries: What biases do LLM judges have?

Use different model family for judging

text: Trust but verify: calibrate the judge against human annotators. Sample 50-100 traces, have a human grade each one, plot judge score vs human score, compute correlation. Re-tune the judge prompt until correlation > 0.7. The example calibration scatter plot shows judge score on the x-axis (0-1) and human score on the y-axis (0-1), with a green dashed diagonal indicating perfect agreement. Sample points cluster near the diagonal, with a correlation badge reading r = 0.78 (Pass). Re-calibrate quarterly. Judges drift as the underlying judge LLM updates. Without re-calibration the dashboard slowly stops reflecting human judgment.
summary: Calibrate judge against 50-100 human-graded traces; require correlation > 0.7 (example r = 0.78); re-calibrate quarterly.
queries: How to calibrate an LLM judge?
How many traces to calibrate a judge?

text: Canonical judge prompt with the rubric baked into a runnable template. Output is forced into machine-readable JSON so a dashboard can parse and graph per-criterion scores. Prompt: 'System: You are an evaluator for a customer-support agent. Score the agent's response on this rubric (total 10): Correctness (0-3) answer matches policy / knowledge base; Completeness (0-2) answer addresses every part of the question; Tone (0-2) friendly + professional, no condescension; Citations (0-2) policy references included when applicable; Escalation (0-1) correctly escalated when outside scope. User Question: {{user_message}} Agent Response: {{agent_response}} Output ONLY this JSON (no other text): {"correctness": N, "completeness": N, "tone": N, "citations": N, "escalation": N, "total": N, "reasoning": "Why you scored each field."}'. JSON output is non-negotiable. Free-form prose from the judge cannot land in a dashboard. Lock the field names. Every later eval (trace evals, online evals) reuses this same shape.
summary: Runnable customer-support judge prompt template emitting locked JSON fields parseable by a dashboard.
queries: How to force JSON output from judge LLM
How to parse judge output in dashboard

text: Same technique, agent scope. Section 23.2 used LLM-as-Judge for RAG generation quality - faithfulness (did the generation stay grounded in retrieved chunks?) and answer-relevance (did the generation address the user query?) on a one-shot input/output pair. Here we extend the same technique to agent-level evals: task completion (did the agent achieve the goal?), trace correctness (did every tool call and response step land?), safety refusals, with multi-step and side-effecting tools. Mechanics are identical: rubric + JSON output + calibration. Only the rubric content changes. A team already running 23.2 RAG evals has 80% of the agent eval infrastructure in place.
summary: Same LLM-as-Judge mechanics extend from RAG eval (faithfulness, answer-relevance) to agent eval (task completion, trace correctness, multi-step tools).

text: Trace eval grades every step of an agent trace independently using a four-dimensional rubric (Tool Choice, Tool Input, Result Handling, Next-Step Planning), in contrast to end-to-end LLM-as-Judge which only judges the final answer. Successful traces like T2 (password reset + email change) confirm every step passed, while failure traces like T4 (refund + cancel) reveal exactly where things broke - step 4 missed the cancel side-effect and step 5 lied about it. Output is a structured JSON record with per-step scores, failure_mode tags (missed_escalation, false_assertion, hallucinated_tool, wrong_argument_type), and an overall_grade. Trace eval costs N× more than end-to-end (8× for an 8-step trace), so the production pattern is to run trace eval on a 5% sample (25% for high-stakes traces) and end-to-end LLM-as-Judge on the rest.
summary: Chapter overview - trace eval grades each step on four criteria, surfaces exact failure locations and modes via JSON records, and is sampled at ~5% due to N× cost.

text: End-to-end LLM-as-Judge tells you if the final answer was correct. Trace eval grades EACH step independently, so when a trace fails you can locate where. T2 (reset password + email change) is a 5-step trace that passes at every step. Steps: 1) classify_intent → Intent: Password Reset + Email Change (PASS); 2) lookup_customer → Customer c-9924 (Alice) Found, Pro Tier (PASS); 3) change_email → Email Updated To alice-new@example.com (PASS); 4) reset_password → Reset Token Emailed To Alice (PASS); 5) respond → Polite Confirmation, Mentions Both Effects (PASS). All 5 steps pass and the trace passes end-to-end. Trace eval still adds value: it confirms WHY the trace passed (every step was correct), not just that the final output happened to look right.
summary: A 5-step successful trace (T2) where trace eval grades each step independently and confirms why the trace passed.

text: T4 (refund + cancel subscription) is the diagnostic trace. The final answer says 'both processed' but trace eval surfaces TWO failures. Steps: 1) classify_intent → Intent: Refund + Cancel Subscription (PASS, correct read); 2) lookup_customer → Customer c-9924, Pro Tier Active (PASS, right tool, right args); 3) lookup_subscription → Active, $240 Annual, 9 Months Used (PASS, all good so far); 4) process_refund → Refund Of $60 Issued. Business Rule Fired Correctly (FAILED) - Judge: Refund Was $60. Policy Requires Escalation > $200. Pass on threshold but agent forgot to cancel; 5) respond → Told Alice Both Refund AND Cancellation Were Processed (WRONG) - Judge: Cancel Tool Was Never Called. Response Lied About Side Effects. Insight: step 4 made the right tool call (process_refund) but the agent did not adapt when it noticed cancel was missing. Step 5 then propagated a false assertion. Trace eval surfaces both; end-to-end eval would only flag the final wrong answer.
summary: A failing trace (T4) showing how trace eval pinpoints a missing cancel call at step 4 and a false assertion at step 5.

text: Each step gets graded on four sub-rubric criteria: (1) Tool Choice - Did the agent pick the right tool for the sub-goal? (2) Tool Input - Were the arguments well-formed and correct? (3) Result Handling - Did the agent interpret the tool result correctly? (4) Next-Step Planning - Did this step's outcome feed the next planned action? For T4 step 4: Tool Choice PASS, Tool Input PASS, Result Handling FAIL, Next-Step Planning FAIL. Trace eval pinpoints where the failure starts: the agent picked process_refund correctly and called it with correct arguments, but misread the result and failed to plan the cancel step. The four sub-criteria mirror an engineer's mental debug pass: did I pick the right thing, did I call it right, did I read the answer right, did I plan the next step right. Production teams build dashboards keyed on these four columns.
summary: The four per-step rubric criteria - tool choice, tool input, result handling, next-step planning - and how they pinpoint where a failure starts.

text:
Trace eval output is a structured JSON record: one entry per step, four scores per entry, plus per-step failure-mode tags and an overall_grade summary. Example shape:
{
  "trace_id": "tr-8492",
  "ticket": "T4",
  "agent_version": "support-agent@v3.1",
  "steps": [
    { "step": 1, "tool": "classify_intent",
      "scores": { "choice": 1, "input": 1, "result": 1, "next": 1 } },
    { "step": 2, "tool": "lookup_customer",
      "scores": { "choice": 1, "input": 1, "result": 1, "next": 1 } },
    { "step": 3, "tool": "lookup_subscription",
      "scores": { "choice": 1, "input": 1, "result": 1, "next": 1 } },
    { "step": 4, "tool": "process_refund",
      "scores": { "choice": 1, "input": 1, "result": 0, "next": 0 },
      "failure_mode": "missed_escalation" },
    { "step": 5, "tool": "respond",
      "scores": { "choice": 0, "input": 0, "result": 0, "next": 0 },
      "failure_mode": "false_assertion" }
  ],
  "overall_grade": "failed_at_step_4_propagated_to_step_5",
  "judged_at": "2026-05-16T08:14:00Z"
}

text: End-to-end LLM-as-Judge costs 1 judge call per trace. Trace eval costs N judge calls per trace (one per step). For an 8-step trace that is 8x the eval cost. Production rule: reserve trace eval for a sample of production traffic; run end-to-end on the rest. The 5% sample is the sweet spot for most production traffic. Use higher rates (e.g., 25%) for high-stakes traces (refunds, account changes) where locating the failing step matters more than overall throughput cost.
summary: Trace eval costs N× more than end-to-end because each step needs its own judge call, so sample ~5% of traffic for trace eval and run end-to-end on the rest.

text: Chapter 28.5 covers eval sets and continuous eval. Eval set is a static portfolio of test cases composed of three decks: Golden (50 typical cases), Adversarial (20 edge/injection cases), and Regression (10 past incidents) - total 80 cases. Eval sets go stale; refresh 10-20% of golden cases per quarter from anonymized production traces. Online eval samples 1-5% of live traffic, grades asynchronously with LLM-as-Judge, and writes to the trace store / dashboard. A drift detector watches the 7-day moving average against a baseline (0.85) and alerts when it drops more than 0.05 (to 0.80). Closing principle: build the eval set before the agent - if you cannot write 20 test cases your agent must pass, you don't know what it's supposed to do.
summary: Production agent evals combine an 80-case static eval set, quarterly freshness refresh, 1-5% async online sampling with LLM-as-Judge, and a moving-average drift alert.

text: An eval set is the static portfolio of test cases you grade your agent against. Production composition uses three decks. Golden: 50 cases of typical, well-defined tickets (reset password, refund, cancel, status lookup), used as regression tests before every deploy. Adversarial: 20 cases of edge cases, ambiguous wording, multi-intent, prompt-injection attempts, catching failure modes Golden misses. Regression: 10 cases of past production bugs, one case per confirmed incident, grows permanently with each new bug. Total eval set: 80 cases. Small enough to run cheaply on every deploy; large enough to capture distribution + adversarial + regression. Most production teams start with this size and grow gradually.
summary: Eval set has three decks - Golden (50), Adversarial (20), Regression (10) - totaling 80 cases, each catching a different failure class.

text: An eval set frozen at month 0 looks great. By month 3 real traffic has shifted: new product features, new customer segments, new attack patterns. Eval pass rate no longer predicts production success. Refresh 10-20% of cases per quarter from recent production traces. Mitigation: every quarter, replace 10-20% of golden cases with recent production traces (anonymized). Adversarial set grows separately as new attack patterns surface. Regression set only grows.
summary: Eval sets go stale as traffic shifts; refresh 10-20% of golden cases each quarter from recent anonymized production traces.

text: Offline eval covers known cases. Online eval samples live production traffic, grades it asynchronously with LLM-as-Judge, and writes the result back to the trace store for the dashboard. Sampling rate is a cost/detection tradeoff. Flow: Production Traffic (100% of tickets) -> Sampler (1-5% selection) -> Async Judge (LLM-as-Judge) -> Trace Store (results + scores) -> Dashboard (drift signal). 1% sample = cheap but slow. 5% sample = catches issues in hours but 5x cost. Pick 1-5% as default. Bump to 25% for the first week after a deploy; drop back once dashboard is stable. Async grading means production latency is unchanged - the judge runs after the user already got their answer.
summary: Online eval samples 1-5% of live traffic and grades it asynchronously with LLM-as-Judge, writing scores to the dashboard without adding production latency.
queries: how to grade live production tickets

text: Online sampling produces a time series of composite scores. A drift detector watches the 7-day moving average against a baseline. Alert if moving average drops more than 0.05 from baseline. Page the engineer, freeze the deploy, investigate. Baseline = 0.85. Alert Floor = 0.80. Time series hovers near 0.85 until day 10, then drops; alert fires when moving average hits 0.78. Real incident shape: an LLM provider rolled a silent model update overnight; the composite score drifted 0.07 from baseline; alert fired the next morning; engineers switched to the pinned-version endpoint within 4 hours. Without continuous eval the drop is invisible for weeks.
summary: Drift detector watches the 7-day moving average of composite scores and fires an alert when it falls more than 0.05 below the 0.85 baseline.
queries: how much score drop triggers alert

how fast did engineers respond to drift

text: The closing principle. The eval set is not an afterthought; it is the production-readiness gate. Production teams that skip eval ship more agents and watch more of them fail. The Principle: 'If you cannot write 20 test cases your agent must pass, you do not know what your agent is supposed to do. Build the eval set FIRST. Build the agent SECOND.' Anti-Pattern: Ship agent. See what breaks in production. Build eval set reactively. Repeat until customers churn. Section 13 Way: Write 20 test cases defining success. Then iterate agent until it passes all 20. Then ship. Then add continuous eval on top. Every Section 13 chapter (prompting, tools, loops, memory, multi-agent, evals) was structured to give a production-ready playbook. The closing message is the discipline that ties it together: eval before ship.
summary: Build the eval set before the agent - if you cannot write 20 test cases defining success, you do not know what the agent should do.
queries: discipline of writing tests before code for agents
how to know what your agent should do
what is the closing message of agent evals

text: Observability and tracing chapter: an agent run is a tree of OTel-shaped spans rooted at the whole run, with loop iterations as children and LLM/tool calls as leaves. Spans carry trace_id, parent_span_id, name, duration_ms, and attributes. Vendors LangSmith, Weave, and Phoenix all store the same shape so picking is about fit. Each span class (LLM, tool, loop iter) needs inputs for replay and outputs for grading. The same tree powers latency and cost dashboards. Alerts on latency P95, per-trace cost, tool failure rate, and quality drift turn traces into production protection.
summary: Chapter teaches how OTel span trees power agent debugging, vendor-portable storage, replay/grade attributes, cost/latency dashboards, and production alerts.

text: An agent run is a tree of spans. One ticket = one trace = one tree of spans. Every operation worth measuring becomes a span. The root span covers the whole run. Each loop iteration is a child span. Inside each iteration, the LLM call and tool call are leaf spans. Example span tree for ticket T2: root Agent Run T2 (3400ms), Loop Iter 1 (1400ms) containing LLM Call 1 (1200ms) and Tool: lookup_customer (187ms), Loop Iter 2 (1300ms) containing LLM Call 2 (1100ms) and Tool: change_email (196ms), Loop Iter 3 (410ms) containing LLM Call 3 Final Answer (400ms). The tree answers every 'why was that ticket slow / wrong / expensive?' Click into a span, read attributes, jump to parent or sibling. No tree = no debugger.
summary: Agent run forms a hierarchical tree of spans where root covers whole run, loop iterations are children, and LLM/tool calls are leaves.

text:
OpenTelemetry (OTel) is the vendor-neutral open standard for spans. Every modern agent tracer speaks OTel underneath. Learn the shape once, read traces in any vendor. Span shape example:
{
  "trace_id":       "tr-8492",
  "span_id":        "sp-3",
  "parent_span_id": "sp-1",
  "name":           "tool.lookup_customer",
  "start_time":     "2026-05-16T10:32:11.204Z",
  "duration_ms":    187,
  "attributes": {
    "customer_id":  "c-9924",
    "tool_name":    "lookup_customer",
    "tool_status":  "success",
    "input_hash":   "sha256:9f4a..."
  }
}
trace_id ties together every span in the whole run. parent_span_id builds the tree. attributes carry per-span detail (who, what, how long, success or failure).
summary: OpenTelemetry (OTel) is the vendor-neutral standard span format used under every agent tracer, with trace_id, parent_span_id, and attributes building the tree.
queries: how do spans link into a tree

text: Three vendors, same concepts. All store OTel-shaped spans underneath; pick by integration fit since core capabilities converge. LangSmith: by LangChain, tight LangChain + LangGraph integration, managed cloud, eval features built in. Use when you already use LangChain stack. Weave: by Weights & Biases, broader ML observability, agent traces alongside model training, on-prem option. Use when you want one pane for ML + agents. Phoenix: by Arize AI, open-source, OTel-native, on-prem first, lightweight self-host. Use when you need on-prem and open standards. Swapping vendors later is mostly a config change, not a rewrite. Lock in the OTel shape today.
summary: LangSmith, Weave, and Phoenix all store OTel spans so vendor choice is about integration fit (LangChain, ML stack, or on-prem open-source) not core features.
queries: which agent tracing tool to pick
best agent tracer for LangChain stack

text: What to attribute: per-span metadata makes the tree searchable, gradable, and replayable. Capture inputs (to replay) AND outputs (to grade). Three span classes, three metadata profiles. LLM Call Span: Model Name, Input Tokens / Output Tokens, Cost ($), Latency (ms), Prompt Fingerprint. Tool Call Span: Tool Name, Input Args (Hashed), Output Or Error, Status: Success / Failure, Retry Count. Loop Iteration Span: Iteration Number, Reason Summary, State Transition, Decision Reached, Halt Signal If Final. Production rule: capture inputs for replay AND outputs for grading. Without both, you cannot reproduce a bad trace OR score it later.
summary: Each span class carries specific attributes (LLM call: tokens/cost/latency/fingerprint; tool: args/status/retries; loop iter: reason/decision/halt) so traces can be replayed and graded.

text: Full T2 trace with cost: same tree as sub=0, now with cost overlaid on each LLM call span. Tool calls themselves are nearly free (compute only). LLM calls dominate. LLM Call 1 = $0.025, LLM Call 2 = $0.030, LLM Call 3 = $0.040. Total Trace Cost = $0.095. Sum across all LLM spans = total trace cost. This is what every cost dashboard reduces to. The same tree powers latency dashboards (bar widths) AND cost dashboards (per-LLM span values). One trace, many views, all from the same OTel spans.
summary: Overlaying $0.025 + $0.030 + $0.040 cost on LLM call spans gives total trace cost $0.095, showing one OTel tree powers both latency and cost dashboards.

text: Turn traces into alerts: traces debug one ticket, alerts protect production. Every alert rule converts a per-span aggregate into a paging signal. Four rules cover most agent incidents. Latency: P95 > 8s for 5 minutes -> Page on-call. Cost: per-trace cost > $1 -> Slack notify + block user. Tool Failure Rate: error rate > 5% over 10 minutes -> Page on-call + open incident. Quality Drift: composite score < baseline - 0.05 (see 28.5) -> Slack notify, trigger eval sweep. The drift rule chains back to 28.5: when composite eval score drops past threshold, alerting kicks in BEFORE end-users notice. Tracing + eval together close the production loop.
summary: Four alert rules (latency P95, per-trace cost, tool failure rate, quality drift) convert span aggregates into paging signals that protect production.

text: Cost control for production agents. Five levers: (1) Know where dollars go - output tokens dominate (priced 5x input), retries compound, tool calls negligible. (2) Cache the prefix - prompt caching of the 12k system prompt + tools + history saves ~80% on input cost since agents send near-identical prefixes every iteration. (3) Model routing - tiny classifier sends 60% of tickets to a small model, 40% to a large model, blending $0.13 vs $0.30 per ticket (~50% savings). (4) Hard budget cap per ticket ($1) terminates runaway loops; pair with iteration cap. (5) Classify failures before retrying - transient/malformed retry, permanent/business-rule escalate. Busy-retry on permanent errors doubles cost without succeeding.
summary: Five levers for agent cost control: know the breakdown, cache the prefix, route by complexity, hard budget cap, and smart retry classification.
queries: five levers for cheaper LLM agents
main techniques to cut agent cost
how to save money on agent runs

text: Where the dollars go. A typical multi-iteration agent run on the customer-support corpus costs about $0.30 per ticket. Cost breakdown: Input Tokens 40%, Output Tokens 35%, Retries 20%, Tool Calls 5%. Output tokens are usually the dominant slice because output is priced 5x input. Retries quietly compound. Tool calls themselves are negligible. The biggest wins target the biggest slices. Output tokens first (use smaller models where possible). Retries second (better error handling). Input tokens third (prompt caching). Tool calls almost never.
summary: Breakdown of where agent run cost goes - output tokens dominate at 5x input pricing, retries compound, tool calls negligible.
